topic: RISC Zero zkVM operational profile for PQ-STARK-BENCH on Apple M3 Max (aarch64 macOS, 16 cores, 64 GB RAM, no CUDA)
bottom_line: RISC Zero v3.0.6 (published 2026-07-17) is the current stable release, Apache-2.0, and arm64 macOS is a first-class rzup-supported platform with automatic Metal GPU acceleration for local proving. Two round-1 findings need correction: (a) RISC Zero DOES ship a keccak accelerator via a patched `tiny-keccak` (gated behind the `unstable` feature), which is directly relevant to the SHAKE critical path in all three FIPS PQ schemes, and (b) Bonsai is not merely deprecated, it was shut down as of December 2025, so no $/proof figure exists to cite. Cycle counts come free without proving via the `Executor::execute()` trait returning `SessionInfo`.
details:
ACCESS DATE FOR ALL SOURCES: 2026-07-23.

=== 1. CURRENT VERSION + INSTALL ===

Latest STABLE: v3.0.6, published_at "2026-07-17T22:29:16Z" (GitHub Releases API via MCP, repo risc0/risc0). Release body verbatim, in full: "- Repair Rust 1.97.0 guest build with `heap-embedded-alloc` feature". Author: bobbobbio. prerelease:false, draft:false.

Prior stable: v3.0.5, published_at "2026-02-03T00:24:01Z" (security-relevant backports incl. "Avoid unbounded host buffer allocation during execution (#3545)" and "On SuccinctReceipt, check that the control_id on seal matches metadata (#3687)").

VERSION-LINE ODDITY WORTH FLAGGING: a prerelease tagged v5.0.0-rc.1 exists, published_at "2026-01-15T00:56:37Z" — i.e. it PREDATES the v3.0.5 and v3.0.6 stables. There is no v4.x in the release list at all. Do not assume v5 supersedes v3.0.6 for benchmarking; v3.0.6 is the newest non-prerelease. Pin explicitly.
NOTE: docs.rs "latest" currently resolves risc0-zkvm to 3.0.5, one patch behind the GitHub tag. Expect a short crates.io lag; verify with `cargo risczero --version`.

INSTALL COMMANDS (verbatim from https://dev.risczero.com/api/zkvm/install, "Installation | RISC Zero Developer Docs"; no visible pub date on page):
  curl -L https://risczero.com/install | bash
  rzup install
Specific version: `rzup install cargo-risczero <version>`
Update: `rzup update`
PLATFORM STATEMENT (measured-and-published, official docs): the page names "x86-64 Linux" and "arm64 macOS" as the two platforms where rzup install is the recommended path. Other systems (x86-64 macOS, arm64 Linux) require manual/from-source install. The M3 Max is arm64 macOS, so it is on the happy path.
Also on arm64 macOS: `rzup install cpp`.
CI/CD pinning form shown in docs (example values, NOT current versions): `rzup install rust 1.81.0` / `rzup install cpp 2024.1.5` / `rzup install r0vm 2.0.0` / `rzup install cargo-risczero 2.0.0`. Docs state r0vm and cargo-risczero versions must match each other and align with the project's risc0-zkvm crate version.
rzup lives in the risc0 monorepo (path `rzup/`), NOT a separate repo — https://api.github.com/repos/risc0/rzup/releases/latest returns 404 Not Found. The v3.0.1 release notes state "Ensure `rzup` version is >= 0.5.0" as a floor for v3.x.

=== 2. LOCAL PROVING ON macOS aarch64: YES, WITH METAL ===

Source: https://dev.risczero.com/api/generating-proofs/local-proving ("Local Proving", RISC Zero Developer Docs).
Verbatim: on Apple silicon "RISC Zero will use the integrated Metal compute cores." No special configuration is required; the docs present this as automatic. CPU proving is stated to work on "nearly any modern CPU (x86 or ARM)" at lower performance. Feature flags exist to toggle "between CPU and GPU proving".
Caveat, verbatim and CRITICAL for the benchmark: "The Groth16 prover currently _only_ works on x86 architecture, and so Apple Silicon is _currently unsupported_ (even via Docker)."
Second caveat, verbatim: "When run for the first time, the GPU (e.g. Metal or CUDA) kernels may need to be JIT compiled. This can take a few minutes, but should only happen once." => DISCARD the first timed run or you will record a one-off JIT cost as prover time.

BENCH IMPLICATION (my inference, labeled): on the M3 Max you can produce composite and succinct (STARK) receipts with Metal acceleration, but you CANNOT produce the Groth16 wrapper locally. If the benchmark's headline metric is STARK prover time, this is fine. If it ever needs on-chain-sized Groth16 receipts, that leg must run on x86-64 Linux.
This is an ASYMMETRY vs SP1, which per round 1 has NO GPU acceleration at all on macOS aarch64. RISC Zero gets Metal, SP1 gets scalar CPU. Any RISC-Zero-vs-SP1 wall-clock chart on this machine is comparing GPU-accelerated against scalar-CPU and MUST disclose that, or it is meaningless.

=== 3. CYCLE COUNTS WITHOUT PROVING ===

(a) Executor-only, host side. From https://docs.rs/risc0-zkvm/latest/risc0_zkvm/trait.Executor.html:
  pub trait Executor {
      fn execute(&self, env: ExecutorEnv<'_>, elf: &[u8]) -> Result<SessionInfo>;
  }
Doc text verbatim: "Execute the specified ELF binary. This only executes the program and does not generate a receipt."

(b) SessionInfo fields, from https://docs.rs/risc0-zkvm/latest/risc0_zkvm/struct.SessionInfo.html:
  segments: Vec<SegmentInfo> — "The number of user cycles for each segment."
  journal: Journal — "The data publicly committed by the guest program."
  exit_code: ExitCode — "The ExitCode of the session."
  receipt_claim: Option<ReceiptClaim> — "The ReceiptClaim associated with the executed session."
  method cycles() — "The total number of user cycles across all segments, without any overhead for continuations or po2 padding."
DISCONFIRMATION: I could not find `SessionStats` / `total_cycles` / `paging_cycles` / `reserved_cycles` on the current SessionInfo docs page. Older RISC Zero material references those; treat them as NOT PRESENT in the 3.x SessionInfo until confirmed against the installed crate. Use `SessionInfo::cycles()` and per-segment `SegmentInfo`.
Also note cycles() explicitly EXCLUDES continuation overhead and po2 padding, so it is a lower bound on what actually gets proven. For a prover-cost benchmark, report BOTH cycles() and the padded per-segment po2 totals, or you will understate work.

(c) In-guest instrumentation, verbatim from https://dev.risczero.com/api/zkvm/optimization ("Guest Optimization Guide"):
  fn my_operation_to_measure() {
    let start = env::cycle_count();
    // potentially expensive or frequently called code
    let end = env::cycle_count();
    eprintln!("my_operation_to_measure: {}", end - start);
  }

(d) Flamegraph profiling, verbatim from the same page:
  cd examples/ecdsa
  RISC0_PPROF_OUT=ecdsa_verify.pb RISC0_DEV_MODE=true cargo run
  go tool pprof -http 127.0.0.1:8000 ecdsa_verify.pb
This is the tool to use for attributing cycles to SHAKE vs NTT vs rejection sampling inside ML-DSA.

(e) Dev mode, from https://dev.risczero.com/api/generating-proofs/dev-mode: setting `RISC0_DEV_MODE` gives "fake receipt creation and pass-through 'verification' function"; proving is bypassed; "receipts generated in dev-mode still include public outputs written to the journal". Build production with the `disable-dev-mode` feature flag, and if that flag is set while the env var is present "the project will panic". As of v2.3.0 there is also programmatic `with_dev_mode` on `VerifierContext` and `ProverOptions`.

=== 4. CYCLE / SEGMENT / CONTINUATION MODEL ===

From https://dev.risczero.com/terminology ("Key Terminology"), verbatim:
  Cycle: "The smallest unit of compute in the zkVM circuit, analogous to a clock cycle on a physical CPU."
  Segment: "The execution trace of a portion of a guest program. The execution of a segment begins at some initial memory image (identified by the ImageID) and proceeds until terminated by the system or user."
  Session: "The execution trace of a guest program. The session's execution starts from an initial memory image (which includes the starting program counter) and proceeds until either a `sys_halt` or a `sys_pause` syscall is encountered."
  Executor: "The portion of the zkVM responsible for generating the execution trace."
  Receipt: "A receipt attests to valid execution of a guest program..."
  Journal: "The portion of the receipt that contains the public outputs of a zkVM application."
  Seal: "The portion of the receipt that cryptographically attests to the receipt claim and the correct execution of the guest program."
Continuations were introduced in version 0.15 and split execution into independently-provable segments.

SEGMENT SIZE CONTROL, verbatim from https://docs.rs/risc0-zkvm/latest/risc0_zkvm/struct.ExecutorEnvBuilder.html, method `segment_limit_po2`: "Set a segment limit, specified in powers of 2 cycles. Lowering this value will reduce the memory consumption of the prover. Memory consumption is roughly linear with the segment size, so lowering this value by 1 will cut memory consumpton by about half. The default value is chosen to be performant on commonly used hardware. Tuning this value, either up or down, may result in better proving performance. Given value must be between risc0_zkp::MIN_CYCLES_PO2 and risc0_zkp::MAX_CYCLES_PO2 (inclusive)." [sic: "consumpton" is the docs' own typo]
Constant values, verbatim declarations from docs.rs/risc0-zkp:
  pub const MIN_CYCLES_PO2: usize = 13;   (= 8,192 cycles)
  pub const MAX_CYCLES_PO2: usize = 24;   (= 16,777,216 cycles)
The DEFAULT segment_limit_po2 value is NOT PUBLISHED on that page — it says only "chosen to be performant on commonly used hardware". Read it at runtime rather than assuming.
Session cap: `session_limit(Option<u64>)`, docs example `.session_limit(Some(32 * 1024 * 1024)) // 32M cycles`.

HOW PROVING TIME SCALES (this is INFERENCE from the documented mechanism, not a published curve): a session of N total cycles is chopped into ceil(N / 2^segment_limit_po2) segments, each padded up to a power of two, and each segment is proven independently. Prover time is therefore approximately LINEAR in total cycle count, and peak RAM is set by the SEGMENT size (not the session size), which is exactly why segment_limit_po2 is the memory knob. This is fully consistent with the round-1 established finding that amortized prover cost per signature is flat in batch size: batching PQ verifications into one session multiplies cycles linearly, so prover seconds grow linearly and only the proof BYTES amortize. RISC Zero's architecture gives no reason to expect otherwise. I found NO published RISC Zero cycles-per-second figure for Apple Silicon Metal; that number is NOT PUBLISHED and you must measure it.

=== 5. PRECOMPILES / ACCELERATED CRATE PATCH TABLE ===

Source: https://dev.risczero.com/api/zkvm/precompiles ("Precompiles | RISC Zero Developer Docs"). NOTE: the sibling page https://dev.risczero.com/api/zkvm/acceleration ("Cryptography Acceleration") fetched EMPTY for me (200 but no body content) — flagging that as a soft disconfirmation; use the /precompiles page.

Circuits stated to exist: SHA-256, RSA, elliptic curve operations, modular multiplication.

PATCH TABLE (crate | supported versions | git patch example | unstable?):
  sha2 | 0.10.8, 0.10.7, 0.10.6, 0.9.9 | sha2 = { git = "https://github.com/risc0/RustCrypto-hashes", tag = "sha2-v0.10.8-risczero.0" } | No

  k256 | 0.13.4, 0.13.3, 0.13.2, 0.13.1 | k256 = { git = "https://github.com/risc0/RustCrypto-elliptic-curves", tag = "k256/v0.13.3-risczero.1" } | No

  curve25519-dalek | 4.1.3, 4.1.2, 4.1.1, 4.1.0 | ed25519-dalek = { git = "https://github.com/risc0/curve25519-dalek", tag = "curve25519-4.1.3-risczero.0" } | No
  rsa | 0.9.6 | rsa = { git = "https://github.com/risc0/RustCrypto-RSA", tag = "v0.9.6-risczero.0" } | No
  substrate-bn | 0.6.0 | substrate-bn = { git = "https://github.com/risc0/paritytech-bn", tag = "v0.6.0-risczero.0" } | No
  bls12_381 | 0.8.0 | bls12_381 = { git = "https://github.com/risc0/zkcrypto-bls12_381", tag = "bls12_381/v0.8.0-risczero.1" } | No
  blst | 0.3.14 | blst = { git = "https://github.com/risc0/blst", tag = "blst/v0.3.14-risczero.0" } | No
  crypto-bigint | 0.5.5, 0.5.4, 0.5.3, 0.5.2 | crypto-bigint = { git = "https://github.com/risc0/RustCrypto-crypto-bigint", tag = "v0.5.5-risczero.0" } | No
  c-kzg | 1.0.3, 2.1.0, 2.1.1, 2.1.5 | c-kzg = { git = "https://github.com/risc0/c-kzg-4844", tag = "v2.1.5-risczero.0" } | No
All go under [patch.crates-io] in the GUEST's Cargo.toml.
"unstable" column meaning (from docs): those patches depend on precompiles still undergoing revision; you must set the `unstable` feature flag on BOTH the guest's risc0-zkvm and the host's risc0-build, and it requires risc0 crates >= 1.2.0.
v3.0.1 release notes (2025-08-21) state: "Stabilize bigint and keccak features".

>>> CORRECTION TO ROUND 1 <<<
Round 1 recorded: "SP1 has a sha3/SHAKE precompile; RISC Zero does not list one." That is HALF WRONG and materially so.
- Literally true: no `sha3` crate appears in RISC Zero's patch table.
- But `tiny-keccak` IS patched, and tiny-keccak provides Keccak-f[1600] plus Shake128/Shake256. Keccak-f[1600] is the identical permutation underneath SHAKE128/SHAKE256/SHA3, which is the hot inner loop for ML-DSA-44, ML-KEM, and SLH-DSA.
- v3.0.1 explicitly "stabilized" the keccak feature, so this is not vaporware.
=> A RISC Zero ML-DSA guest CAN get a hardware-accelerated SHAKE path, by routing the XOF through patched tiny-keccak instead of the `sha3` crate. Any chart claiming "RISC Zero has no SHAKE acceleration" would be wrong. What you must instead disclose is the NTT/lattice asymmetry: neither prover has an NTT or ML-DSA precompile, while both have Ed25519/ECDSA precompiles. That asymmetry stands from round 1 and is the real one to caveat.
ACTION: benchmark ML-DSA-44 on RISC Zero BOTH ways (stock `sha3` vs patched `tiny-keccak`) and publish both numbers. The delta is itself a publishable result and nobody appears to have measured it.

=== 6. GUEST CONSTRAINTS ===

Target triple: `riscv32im-risc0-zkvm-elf`. Confirmed in-repo at rzup/src/rust-toolchain-build-config.toml and risc0/cargo-risczero/src/commands/config.toml, both containing verbatim:
  [build]
  target = ["riscv32im-risc0-zkvm-elf"]
  extended = true
  tools = ["cargo", "cargo-clippy", "clippy", "rustfmt"]
ELF output dir constant, from risc0/build/src/docker.rs verbatim: pub const TARGET_DIR: &str = "target/riscv-guest/riscv32im-risc0-zkvm-elf/docker";

ISA, verbatim from https://dev.risczero.com/api/zkvm/zkvm-specification ("zkVM Technical Specification"): the zkVM implements "the `RV32IM` instruction set. This is the `RV32I` base instruction along with the multiplication (`M`) feature." Explicit exclusions, verbatim: "We do not implement `CRS` instructions" and "We do not implement loads or stores to unaligned addresses."

Memory layout, verbatim table from the same page:
  0x00000000 - 0x0000FFFF | 64 KiB | Invalid page | "dereferencing a NULL address will result in a failure"
  0x00010000 - 0xBFFFFFFF | ~3 GB  | User Memory  | "Contains code, heap, and stack of the guest program"
  0xC0000000 - 0xFF000000 | ~1 GB  | Kernel Memory| "Contains kernel code and data such as ecall/trap dispatch, register contents"
=> Max guest address space is ~3 GB of user memory, shared by code + heap + stack. A batch of N ML-DSA signatures plus public keys must fit in that ~3 GB.

FLOATING POINT: NOT PUBLISHED as an explicit statement. The spec page makes no mention of FPU/soft-float, and a repo-wide code search for "floating point"/"soft-float"/"softfloat" returned total_count:0. INFERENCE (clearly labeled as mine, not vendor-stated): RV32IM contains neither the F nor D extension, so any f32/f64 in guest code must be lowered to soft-float library calls by the compiler, at a large cycle cost. Practical guidance: keep the PQ guest strictly integer. This matters because some reference ML-DSA/Falcon code uses floating point — Falcon's reference signing uses FP, and while VERIFICATION is integer-only in Falcon, do not blindly port reference code.

no_std: the docs I fetched did not give me a clean verbatim no_std statement, so I am marking that NOT VERIFIED here rather than asserting it. Related datum: v3.0.6's entire changelog is about the `heap-embedded-alloc` feature, which implies an embedded-allocator path exists for guests. Verify against the risc0-zkvm crate features before relying on it.
Stack limits: NOT PUBLISHED as a separate number; the stack lives inside the ~3 GB user memory region. Note the v2.0.2 changelog references a guest-stack profiler failure mode ("attempted to follow a return with an empty call stack"), so stack depth is observable but not documented as a hard constant.

=== 7. COST FIGURES / BONSAI PRICING ===

>>> LOUD DISCONFIRMATION. THIS IS THE MOST IMPORTANT ITEM IN THE REPORT. <<<
There is NO Bonsai pricing to cite, because Bonsai no longer exists.
- https://dev.risczero.com/api/bonsai/bonsai-overview returns HTTP 404 Not Found.
- The dev.risczero.com top-level API nav has NO Bonsai section at all.
- https://dev.risczero.com/api/generating-proofs/remote-proving now presents Boundless as the remote proving option, describing it verbatim as "a decentralized proving marketplace which lets users generate proofs for their R0VM applications, without using their own hardware for proof generation," where "permissionless provers generate proofs on behalf of the user." That page contains NO Bonsai status info and NO pricing.
- https://docs.boundless.network/provers/proving-stack refers verbatim to "Bonsai (now deprecated - see [here](/developers/tutorials/bonsai#what-happened-to-bonsai))".
- https://docs.boundless.network/developers/tutorials/bonsai states verbatim: "Bonsai was RISC Zero's centralized proving service, delivering proofs via an API. As of December 2025, Bonsai is no longer available." Boundless is the stated replacement; migration path is the "Request a Proof" tutorial.
=> Any benchmark, blog post, or slide citing Bonsai $/proof is citing a dead product. If round-1 or any competing work quotes Bonsai pricing, that is a stale-source error and is worth calling out publicly.
=> $/proof on Boundless is market-determined (provers bid, and per the proving-stack page provers must post collateral), so it is NOT a fixed published rate. I found no cost-per-proof figure on either docs site. NOT PUBLISHED.
=> For PQ-STARK-BENCH this argues for reporting prover-SECONDS and peak RAM on named hardware as the primary metric, with $/proof either omitted or derived transparently from a stated cloud instance hourly rate. That is also what IACR ePrint 2021/1048 did, so it keeps you methodologically consistent with the prior art.

=== 8. LICENSE ===

=== RECOMMENDED PINNED SETUP FOR THE M3 MAX ===
1. curl -L https://risczero.com/install | bash
2. rzup install cargo-risczero 3.0.6 ; rzup install r0vm 3.0.6 ; rzup install cpp
3. Pin risc0-zkvm = "3.0.6" in host and guest; record `cargo risczero --version` in results JSON.
4. Cycle counts: Executor::execute() -> SessionInfo::cycles(), plus per-segment SegmentInfo. Record both raw and po2-padded.
5. Proving: default prover picks up Metal automatically. Throw away run #1 (kernel JIT). Do NOT attempt Groth16 locally.
6. Record segment_limit_po2 actually in use (read it, do not assume the default) since it sets peak RAM.
7. Run ML-DSA-44 twice: stock sha3 crate vs patched tiny-keccak with the `unstable` feature on both risc0-zkvm and risc0-build.

blockers:
(1) GROTH16 IS IMPOSSIBLE LOCALLY. Docs state verbatim that the Groth16 prover 'only works on x86 architecture, and so Apple Silicon is currently unsupported (even via Docker)'. Fallback: restrict the M3 Max leg to composite/succinct STARK receipts and run any Groth16 wrapping on an x86-64 Linux box or cloud instance. This does not block the headline metric if that metric is STARK prover time.

(2) NO BONSAI = NO VENDOR $/PROOF NUMBER. Bonsai shut down December 2025 and Boundless pricing is a bid-based market with no published fixed rate. Fallback: make prover-seconds and peak-RAM-on-named-hardware the primary metric (matching ePrint 2021/1048's methodology), and if $/proof is shown at all, derive it transparently from a stated cloud instance hourly rate rather than citing any vendor price.

(3) GPU ASYMMETRY POISONS ANY NAIVE RISC-ZERO-VS-SP1 CHART ON THIS MACHINE. RISC Zero gets automatic Metal acceleration on the M3 Max; SP1 (per round 1) has no GPU acceleration on macOS aarch64 at all. Comparing them head-to-head on wall-clock here compares GPU against scalar CPU. Fallback: either disclose this in every cross-prover chart, or force RISC Zero to CPU-only via its documented CPU/GPU feature flags for the apples-to-apples run and publish the Metal number separately as a best-case.

(4) DEFAULT segment_limit_po2 IS NOT PUBLISHED, only bounded to [13, 24]. Since peak prover RAM is roughly linear in segment size, an unrecorded default makes RAM figures irreproducible. Fallback: read and pin the value explicitly, and record it in the results JSON for every run.

(5) SessionInfo::cycles() EXCLUDES continuation overhead and po2 padding, so reporting it alone understates real prover work. Fallback: report both raw cycles() and padded per-segment po2 totals.

(6) VERSION-LINE AMBIGUITY. A v5.0.0-rc.1 prerelease (2026-01-15) predates the v3.0.5/v3.0.6 stables and there is no v4.x at all; docs.rs 'latest' still resolves risc0-zkvm to 3.0.5 while the GitHub tag is 3.0.6. Fallback: pin v3.0.6 explicitly via rzup, verify with `cargo risczero --version`, and record that string in results JSON rather than trusting 'latest'.

(7) no_std guest requirements and floating-point handling are NOT PUBLISHED in the docs I could reach; my soft-float claim is an inference from RV32IM lacking the F/D extensions, not a vendor statement. Fallback: verify empirically with a trivial f64 guest and a cycle count before porting any reference PQ code that touches floating point.

topic: SP1 (Succinct) operational profile for PQ-STARK-BENCH on Apple M3 Max (aarch64 macOS, 16 cores, 64 GB, no CUDA)
bottom_line: SP1 stable is v6.3.1 (published 2026-06-25); install is `curl -L https://sp1up.succinct.xyz | bash` then `sp1up`, and a darwin_arm64 CLI asset is shipped, so the toolchain installs natively on M3 Max. Local CPU proving (core/compressed) works on macOS ("SP1 can currently be run natively on Linux and macOS"), but there is NO GPU path on Apple Silicon (CUDA is "only supported on Linux x86_64", AVX is Intel-only), and Groth16/PLONK wrapping is documented as requiring Docker (+16GB RAM Groth16, 64GB+ PLONK; wrap step "roughly 14GB for Groth16 and 60GB for PLONK"). BIGGEST FINDING, and it breaks a cross-prover assumption: as of v6.x the SP1 guest target is `riscv64im-succinct-zkvm-elf` (RV64IM, LP64, soft-float), NOT riscv32im — so an SP1 cycle and a RISC Zero cycle (riscv32im) are executing different ISAs and are NOT directly comparable.
details:
ACCESS DATE for everything below: 2026-07-23. Where a page had no visible publication date (Succinct docs render as an undated Mintlify-style site), I say "no visible date".

=== 1. VERSION + INSTALL (measured/published) ===
GitHub API, https://api.github.com/repos/succinctlabs/sp1/releases/latest -> `"tag_name":"v6.3.1"`, `"name":"v6.3.1"`, `"published_at":"2026-06-25T11:45:21Z"`, `"prerelease":false`. Release notes body verbatim: "* **prover-types**: expose cluster component build info", "* bump SP1 version to v6.3.1". HTML: https://github.com/succinctlabs/sp1/releases/tag/v6.3.1
Assets relevant to M3 Max: `cargo_prove_v6.3.1_darwin_arm64.tar.gz` (size 18310298 bytes, download_count 136). Also `cargo_prove_v6.3.1_darwin_amd64.tar.gz`, `cargo_prove_v6.3.1_linux_amd64.tar.gz`, `cargo_prove_v6.3.1_linux_arm64.tar.gz`, and `sp1_gpu_server_v6.3.1_x86_64.tar.gz` (note: GPU server is x86_64 ONLY — there is no arm64 GPU server asset).
Install (https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/getting-started/install, no visible date):
  `curl -L https://sp1up.succinct.xyz | bash`
  `sp1up`
Verify:
  `cargo prove --version`
  `RUSTUP_TOOLCHAIN=succinct cargo --version`   (or `cargo +succinct --version`)
Page states verbatim: "SP1 can currently be run natively on Linux and macOS." Prebuilt binaries are "built on Ubuntu 20.04 (22.04 on ARM) and macOS." Prereqs listed: Git, Rust, Docker, `protoc`. Rate-limit escape hatch: `--token` flag with a GitHub token. Building from source "30 mins to an hour depending on your machine".
Toolchain version note (https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/developers/common-issues): "The latest Succinct Rust toolchain is version 1.93" (paraphrase of the page's version-mismatch section; the verbatim error quoted there is "package `alloy` cannot be built because it requires rustc 1.91 or newer, while the currently active rustc version is 1.90.0"). C toolchain: "Missing `riscv64-unknown-elf-gcc` requires reinstalling with `sp1up --c-toolchain`" — note the riscv64 prefix, corroborating the 64-bit target.

=== 2. PROVING ON macOS aarch64 / PROVER MODES ===
Proof types (https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/generating-proofs/proof-types, no visible date), verbatim:
 - Core (Default): "generates a list of STARK proofs that in aggregate have size proportional to the size of the execution."
 - Compressed: "generates STARK proofs that have constant size."
 - Groth16 (Recommended): "generates a SNARK proof that is ~260 bytes large and can be verified onchain for around ~270k gas." Trusted setup = Aztec Ignition.
 - PLONK: "generates a SNARK proof that is ~868 bytes large and can also be verified onchain for around ~300k gas", "does not require a trusted setup", ~90 seconds slower than compressed.
 That page contains NO statement about x86/CUDA/Docker/Apple Silicon.
Hardware requirements (https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/getting-started/hardware-requirements, no visible date), verbatim table values:
 - Mock/Network: CPU "1+, single-core perf matters"; RAM "8GB+, more is better"
 - Core/Compress: CPU "16+, more is better"; RAM "16GB+, more if you have more cores"
 - Groth16 and PLONK (EVM): CPU "16+, more is better"; RAM "Groth16: 16GB+, PLONK: 64GB+"
 - "The final wrapping step requires roughly 14GB of memory for Groth16 and 60GB for PLONK."
 - "SP1 currently supports GPUs with CUDA Compute Capability >= 8.0 (e.g., A100, RTX 30/40 series, H100)"; "GPU proving is only supported on Linux x86_64."
Hardware acceleration (https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/generating-proofs/hardware-acceleration, no visible date): CUDA requires "Linux x86_64" + "CUDA 12 runtime and a compatible NVIDIA driver", "24GB or more VRAM". AVX256/AVX512 via Plonky3 on "Intel x86 CPUs", flags `RUSTFLAGS="-C target-cpu=native"` and `-C target-feature=+avx512f`. The page does NOT mention Metal or Apple Silicon.
=> CONSEQUENCE for M3 Max: core/compressed proving runs, but on scalar NEON-less-accelerated CPU only. No CUDA, no AVX, no documented Metal backend. This is asymmetric vs RISC Zero v3.0.6 which does use Metal on Apple Silicon (round-1 finding, not re-verified here) — DISCLOSE THIS IN EVERY CHART.
Groth16/PLONK on Apple Silicon — the honest answer is "documented via Docker, native path exists in code but is not documented as supported":
 - https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/verification/getting-started (no visible date), verbatim: "The Groth16 and PLONK provers are only guaranteed to work on official releases of SP1. To use Groth16 or PLONK proving & verification locally, ensure that you have Docker installed and have at least 16GB of RAM", plus "you might need to increase the memory limit for docker desktop if you're running on Mac."
 - https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/developers/common-issues: proof aggregation failures on macOS "may require increasing Docker Desktop memory to at least 32GB" (docs explicitly contemplate a macOS Docker path).
 - Code evidence for a NATIVE macOS path (my own inference, clearly labelled): https://raw.githubusercontent.com/succinctlabs/sp1/refs/tags/v6.3.1/crates/recursion/gnark-ffi/build.rs builds a Go c-archive ("sp1gnark") with cgo and contains an explicit macOS branch: "Static linking doesn't really work on macos, so we need to link some system libs", gated on `cfg!(target_os = "macos")`, linking CoreFoundation and Security, with an escape hatch env var `SP1_GNARK_FFI_SKIP_MAC_FRAMEWORKS`; a `groth16-cuda` feature adds `-tags=icicle`. So native (Go-toolchain) Groth16/PLONK wrapping on darwin/arm64 is plausible under the `native` feature. NOT VERIFIED BY RUNNING IT. Docs never say "Apple Silicon supported" for Groth16/PLONK. Treat as: plan for Docker, budget 14GB (Groth16) / 60GB (PLONK) wrap RAM. On a 64 GB M3 Max, PLONK wrapping is at the edge and likely infeasible under Docker Desktop; Groth16 should fit.
 - DISCONFIRMATION: a web-search snippet claimed "You will need at least 128GB RAM to generate a Groth16 or PLONK proof." That figure does NOT appear on the current hardware-requirements page, which says 16GB+/64GB+. Do not use 128GB.
 - I found NO GitHub issue in succinctlabs/sp1 matching "groth16 arm64 macOS docker" (search returned total_count 0) — so there is no public issue confirming or denying arm64 Groth16. Absence of evidence, not evidence of absence.

=== 3. CYCLE COUNTS WITHOUT PROVING ===
https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/optimizing-programs/cycle-tracking and the official template https://raw.githubusercontent.com/succinctlabs/sp1-project-template/main/script/src/bin/main.rs, verbatim:
  let client = ProverClient::from_env();
  let (output, report) = client.execute(FIBONACCI_ELF, stdin).run().unwrap();
  println!("Number of cycles: {}", report.total_instruction_count());
Prover gas (https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/optimizing-programs/prover-gas):
  let (_, report) = client.execute(ELF, &stdin).run().unwrap();
  let prover_gas = report.gas.unwrap_or(0);
ExecutionReport struct, verbatim from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/succinctlabs/sp1/refs/tags/v6.3.1/crates/core/executor/src/report.rs:
  pub opcode_counts: Box<EnumMap<Opcode, u64>>,
  pub syscall_counts: Box<EnumMap<SyscallCode, u64>>,
  pub cycle_tracker: HashMap<String, u64>,
  pub invocation_tracker: HashMap<String, u64>,
  pub touched_memory_addresses: u64,
  pub exit_code: u64,
  pub(crate) gas: Option<u64>,
  methods: `total_instruction_count()` = `self.opcode_counts.values().sum()`, `total_syscall_count()` = `self.syscall_counts.values().sum()`.
  Doc comment warning worth quoting in the benchmark methodology: "The serde format is stable only within a single SP1 version, since `Opcode`/`SyscallCode` gain variants across releases."
Per-region tracking requires the profiling feature: `sp1-sdk = { version = "6.1.0", features = ["profiling"] }`; markers `println!("cycle-tracker-start: compute")` / `cycle-tracker-end`, or `cycle-tracker-report-start/end` which populate `report.cycle_tracker` and `report.invocation_tracker`. Docs: "The `cycle_tracker` and `invocation_tracker` fields in the `ExecutionReport` are only populated when the `profiling` feature is enabled." Macro form: `#[sp1_derive::cycle_tracker]`.

=== 4. WHAT IS A CYCLE, AND IS IT COMPARABLE TO RISC ZERO? ===
SP1 side, verbatim from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/succinctlabs/sp1/refs/tags/v6.3.1/crates/core/executor/src/syscall_code.rs (module doc): "A system call is invoked by the the `ecall` instruction with a specific value in register t0." and "Byte 2: Previously, this was the number of additional cycles the syscall uses. Now, this byte is unused, as each syscall instruction increments the clock by 256 additionally."
 -> So in SP1 v6 every syscall/precompile invocation costs a FLAT +256 clock, regardless of which precompile. That means cycle counts systematically under-represent precompile cost, which is exactly why Succinct introduced prover gas.
Prover gas (https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/optimizing-programs/prover-gas), verbatim: "Prover gas is a more accurate metric for estimating the real proving 'costs' of an SP1 program introduced in versions >= 4.1.4."; "Two programs of the same cycle counts may require significantly different proving times."; "Although keccak256-300kb constitutes more cycles (at approximately 5.6 million cycles) than ECDSA recovery (at approximately 4.4 million cycles), ECDSA recovery takes more than twice the amount of proving time."; "Prover gas has been roughly calibrated to be of a similar magnitude to RISC-V cycle count".
RISC Zero side (https://dev.risczero.com/api/zkvm/optimization, no visible date), verbatim: "a 'clock cycle' is the smallest unit of time in a CPU's operation, representing one tick of the CPU's internal clock and the time it takes to execute a basic CPU operation (e.g. adding two integers)"; the zkVM implements riscv32im; memory is split into 1 kB pages; "Page-in and page-out operations each require between 1,094 and 5,130 cycles, averaging approximately 1,130 cycles".
VERDICT (my inference, grounded): SP1 cycles and RISC Zero cycles are NOT comparable, for three independent reasons: (a) different ISA width — SP1 v6 guest is RV64IM, RISC Zero is RV32IM, so the same Rust source compiles to a different instruction stream (a 64-bit limb multiply in ML-DSA/NTT costs very differently); (b) SP1 charges a flat +256 clock per syscall while RISC Zero charges paging cycles (1,094-5,130) that SP1 has no analogue of; (c) SP1's own docs disown cycles as a proving-cost proxy in favour of PGUs. For PQ-STARK-BENCH the only cross-prover-honest headline is wall-clock prover seconds and peak RSS on identical hardware; cycles/PGUs must be reported per-prover, never on a shared axis.

=== 5. PRECOMPILES (authoritative, from source, not docs) ===
Full syscall/precompile enum at tag v6.3.1, https://raw.githubusercontent.com/succinctlabs/sp1/refs/tags/v6.3.1/crates/core/executor/src/syscall_code.rs (values verbatim):
 HALT 0x00_00_00_00; WRITE 0x00_00_00_02; ENTER_UNCONSTRAINED 0x03; EXIT_UNCONSTRAINED 0x04;
 SHA_EXTEND 0x00_30_01_05; SHA_COMPRESS 0x00_01_01_06;
 ED_ADD 0x00_01_01_07; ED_DECOMPRESS 0x00_00_01_08;
 KECCAK_PERMUTE 0x00_01_01_09;
 SECP256K1_ADD 0x0A; SECP256K1_DOUBLE 0x0B; SECP256K1_DECOMPRESS 0x0C;
 BN254_ADD 0x0E; BN254_DOUBLE 0x0F;
 COMMIT 0x10; COMMIT_DEFERRED_PROOFS 0x1A; VERIFY_SP1_PROOF 0x1B;
 BLS12381_DECOMPRESS 0x1C; BLS12381_ADD 0x1E; BLS12381_DOUBLE 0x1F;
 BLS12381_FP_ADD/SUB/MUL 0x20/0x21/0x22; BLS12381_FP2_ADD/SUB/MUL 0x23/0x24/0x25;
 BN254_FP_ADD/SUB/MUL 0x26/0x27/0x28; BN254_FP2_ADD/SUB/MUL 0x29/0x2A/0x2B;
 SECP256R1_ADD 0x2C; SECP256R1_DOUBLE 0x2D; SECP256R1_DECOMPRESS 0x2E;
 UINT256_MUL 0x1D; U256XU2048_MUL 0x2F; UINT256_ADD_CARRY 0x30; UINT256_MUL_CARRY 0x31;
 MPROTECT 0x32; POSEIDON2 0x33; SIG_RETURN 0x34; HINT_LEN 0xF0; HINT_READ 0xF1; HINT_MPROTECT_FLUSH 0x35;
 debug-only: DUMP_ELF 0x40, INSERT_PROFILER_SYMBOLS 0x41, DELETE_PROFILER_SYMBOLS 0x42.
CRITICAL FOR PQ: there is NO lattice/NTT/ML-DSA/Falcon/SHAKE-specific precompile. The only PQ-relevant accelerator is KECCAK_PERMUTE (keccak-f[1600]), which SHA3/SHAKE ride on, plus SHA_EXTEND/SHA_COMPRESS for SHA-2 (relevant to SLH-DSA-SHA2 variants). Round-1's phrasing "SP1 has a sha3/SHAKE precompile" should be tightened to: SP1 patches the `sha3` crate so SHA3/SHAKE route through the KECCAK_PERMUTE syscall; there is no SHAKE-specific circuit.
Patched-crate table (https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/optimizing-programs/precompiles, no visible date), verbatim git deps:
 sha2 = { git = "https://github.com/sp1-patches/RustCrypto-hashes", package = "sha2", tag = "patch-sha2-0.10.9-sp1-6.0.0" }   (versions 0.9.9, 0.10.6, 0.10.8, 0.10.9)
 sha3 = { git = "https://github.com/sp1-patches/RustCrypto-hashes", package = "sha3", tag = "patch-sha3-0.11.0-sp1-6.0.0" }   (0.10.8, 0.11.0)
 crypto-bigint = { git = "https://github.com/sp1-patches/RustCrypto-bigint", tag = "patch-0.5.5-sp1-6.0.0" }   (0.5.5)
 tiny-keccak = { git = "https://github.com/sp1-patches/tiny-keccak", tag = "patch-2.0.2-sp1-6.0.0" }   (2.0.2)
 curve25519-dalek = { git = "https://github.com/sp1-patches/curve25519-dalek", tag = "patch-4.1.3-sp1-6.0.0" }   (4.1.3)
 curve25519-dalek-ng = { ... tag = "patch-4.1.1-sp1-6.0.0" }   (4.1.1)
 k256 = { git = "https://github.com/sp1-patches/elliptic-curves", tag = "patch-k256-13.4-sp1-6.0.0" }

 secp256k1 = { git = "https://github.com/sp1-patches/rust-secp256k1", tag = "patch-0.29.1-sp1-6.0.0" }   (0.29.1, 0.30.0)
 substrate-bn = { git = "https://github.com/sp1-patches/bn", tag = "patch-0.6.0-sp1-6.0.0-substrate-bn" }
 bls12_381 = { git = "https://github.com/sp1-patches/bls12_381", tag = "patch-0.8.0-sp1-6.0.0" }

Enable in the GUEST crate's Cargo.toml, verbatim example:
 [patch.crates-io]
 p256 = { git = "https://github.com/sp1-patches/elliptic-curves", package = "p256", tag = "patch-p256-13.2-sp1-6.0.0" }
Note the Ed25519 acceleration path for the classical baseline is `curve25519-dalek` -> ED_ADD/ED_DECOMPRESS. That is the asymmetry round 1 flagged: classical gets a curve precompile, ML-DSA gets nothing but keccak-f.
Also note: https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/optimizing-programs/precompile-specification renders its syscall table via client-side JS and returned only "Loading..." to a fetcher — the page is NOT machine-readable. Use the source file above instead. Also read https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/security/safe-precompile-usage before publishing precompile-based numbers (not fetched in this round).

=== 6. GUEST CONSTRAINTS ===
TARGET TRIPLE — LOUD DISCONFIRMATION OF THE DOCS, AND OF THE COMMON ASSUMPTION THAT SP1 IS RV32:
 - https://raw.githubusercontent.com/succinctlabs/sp1/refs/tags/v6.3.1/crates/build/src/lib.rs, verbatim: `pub const DEFAULT_TARGET: &str = "riscv64im-succinct-zkvm-elf";` and
   `pub const CLANG_FLAGS: &[&str] = &["--target=riscv64-unknown-none-elf", "-march=rv64im", "-mabi=lp64", "-ffreestanding", "-fno-builtin", "-fno-stack-protector", "-nostdlibinc"];`
 - https://raw.githubusercontent.com/succinctlabs/sp1/refs/tags/v6.3.1/zkevm/README.md, verbatim: "Target triple: `riscv64im-succinct-zkvm-elf` (RV64IM, LP64, soft-float). ISA-equivalent to eth-act's `riscv64im_zicclsm-unknown-none-elf`." and "Syscall: `ecall` with the syscall number in `t0`, args in `a0..a7`. Return values via `t0` (lateout) or `a0` out-pointer."
 - The install doc agrees: "the `riscv64im-succinct-zkvm-elf` compilation target."
 - BUT https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/writing-programs/compiling still says, verbatim: "Under the hood, this CLI command calls `cargo build` with the `riscv32im-succinct-zkvm-elf` target and other required environment variables and flags." THIS DOC PAGE IS STALE / SELF-CONTRADICTORY WITH THE INSTALL PAGE AND WITH v6.3.1 SOURCE. Anyone who cites the compiling page for "SP1 is RV32IM" is citing a bug. (The toolchain still ships both targets: crates/cli/src/commands/build_toolchain.rs passes "riscv32im-succinct-zkvm-elf,riscv64im-succinct-zkvm-elf".)
FLOATING POINT: rv64im has no F/D extension and the ABI is explicitly "LP64, soft-float" (zkevm/README.md). A repo-wide code search for the string "floating point" in succinctlabs/sp1 returned total_count 0 — i.e. there is NO published statement about float support. My inference: floats compile but are software-emulated by compiler-builtins and will be expensive; avoid them in guest code. Label as inference, not vendor claim.
no_std / entrypoint: guest uses `#![no_main]` + `sp1_zkvm::entrypoint!(main);` (official template https://raw.githubusercontent.com/succinctlabs/sp1-project-template/main/program/src/main.rs). IO: `sp1_zkvm::io::read::<T>()`, `sp1_zkvm::io::commit_slice(&bytes)`. `no_std` is not asserted anywhere I fetched — SP1 provides a std shim; treat "std works" as inference from the template using std types, unverified.
MEMORY LIMITS, verbatim from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/succinctlabs/sp1/refs/tags/v6.3.1/crates/primitives/src/consts.rs:
 `pub const MAXIMUM_MEMORY_SIZE: u64 = (1u64 << 48) - 1;`  // "The maximum size of the memory in bytes."  (= 281474976710655 bytes)
 `pub const MAX_JIT_LOG_ADDR: usize = 40;`  // "The maximum log2 size of native executor's memory"
 `pub const STACK_TOP: u64 = 0x7800_0000;`  // "The stack top for the 64-bit zkvm."
 `LOG_PAGE_SIZE = 12`, `PAGE_SIZE = 1 << LOG_PAGE_SIZE` (4096 bytes), `WORD_SIZE = 4` ("size of a word in limbs"), `WORD_BYTE_SIZE = 2 * WORD_SIZE` (= 8 bytes -> 64-bit words), `INSTRUCTION_WORD_SIZE = 4`.
 Guest-side allocator check (crates/zkvm/entrypoint/src/syscalls/memory.rs): `if overflowed || MAXIMUM_MEMORY_SIZE < heap_pos as u64 { panic!("Memory limit exceeded"); }`. Memory map per zkevm/README.md: "`.text` at `0x7800_0000` (= `STACK_TOP`); stack grows down from there ...; the SP1 executor rejects ELFs with segments below `STACK_TOP`."
 The real limit for a batch-of-N-signatures guest is HOST RAM during proving, not the 2^48 address space.
Other operational gotchas (https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/developers/common-issues): "SP1 only supports release builds" — stack overflow / bus error / segfault symptoms come from forgetting `--release`; `ProverClient` construction is slow, wrap in `Arc` and reuse across batch runs (matters a lot for an N-sweep harness).

=== 7. PROVER NETWORK PRICING ===
https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/prover-network/faq (no visible date), verbatim: "The cost to generate a proof is based on the computational resources it consumes, which is measured in prover gas units (PGUs)."; "Payments for proofs are made in $PROVE, which is the native token of the Succinct Prover Network." (token contract linked: 0x6BEF15D938d4E72056AC92Ea4bDD0D76B1C4ad29); "The price per PGU is set through a competitive auction. When you request a proof, provers on the network submit bids for the job."; "$PROVE Cost Estimate = Base Fee + PGUs * Price per PGU"; "Both are dynamic — the auctioneer adjusts them based on prevailing market conditions." Volume discounts exist via "reserved capacity".
=> NO fixed $/proof, no published PGU price, no published base fee. Cost per proof is NOT PUBLISHED — it is auction-determined. For the benchmark, the network number you can publish is PGUs consumed (deterministic, from `report.gas`), plus the observed clearing price at run time, timestamped.
Network setup: `NETWORK_PRIVATE_KEY` env var set to "your requester account's private key"; client built via `ProverClient::builder().network_for(NetworkMode::Mainnet)`; example mode call verbatim: `let _proof = client.prove(&pk, stdin).compressed().await.unwrap();`.

=== 8. LICENSE ===
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/succinctlabs/sp1/refs/tags/v6.3.1/LICENSE-MIT — "The MIT License (MIT)", "Copyright (c) 2023 Succinct Labs". The filename LICENSE-MIT implies a dual MIT/Apache-2.0 layout, but I did NOT fetch a LICENSE-APACHE file, so "dual-licensed" is UNVERIFIED. Verified claim: MIT, Copyright (c) 2023 Succinct Labs.

blockers: (1) HARD BLOCKER RISK: Groth16/PLONK wrapping on Apple Silicon is undocumented. Docs prescribe Docker + 14GB (Groth16) / 60GB (PLONK) for the wrap step; on 64 GB shared with macOS, PLONK is very likely infeasible and Groth16 is tight. FALLBACK: publish core + compressed proofs locally on the M3 Max, and produce Groth16/PLONK either on a Linux x86_64 box (rented GPU/CPU) or via the Succinct Prover Network, clearly labelled as a different machine. Do NOT put local-Mac and remote-Linux prover times on the same axis. (2) NO GPU PATH ON M3 MAX: CUDA is Linux x86_64 only and AVX is Intel-only, so SP1 on this Mac is scalar CPU, while RISC Zero uses Metal. Any SP1-vs-RISC-Zero wall-clock chart produced on this Mac is measuring backend availability, not prover design — this asymmetry must be stated on every chart or the benchmark is indefensible. FALLBACK: run the cross-prover comparison on a Linux x86_64 host where both have comparable backends, and treat the M3 Max as a dev/soak machine only. (3) RV64 vs RV32: SP1 v6 guests are RV64IM, RISC Zero is RV32IM, so cycle counts are categorically incomparable and even the compiled algorithm differs (64-bit limbs change ML-DSA/NTT cost structure). FALLBACK: headline on wall-clock seconds + peak RSS + proof bytes; report cycles/PGUs per-prover in separate panels. (4) The official SP1 'Compiling' docs page contradicts the source and the install page on the target triple — if any prior-art or competitor claim rests on 'SP1 is riscv32im', it needs re-checking against v6.x. (5) Network cost per proof is NOT PUBLISHED (auction-priced in $PROVE); you cannot quote a $/proof figure without timestamping an observed clearing price. (6) ExecutionReport serde is only stable within one SP1 version — pin v6.3.1 for the whole benchmark run or results become non-comparable across the sweep.

topic: Fair cross-prover methodology for PQ-STARK-BENCH: RISC Zero vs SP1 (and later Stwo/Cairo)
bottom_line: RISC Zero "cycles" and SP1 "cycles" are NOT the same unit and must never be plotted on a shared axis: RISC Zero cycles are trace rows that include multi-thousand-cycle paging events and are padded to a power-of-two segment, while SP1's own docs concede "cycle counts are not a perfect proxy for the work required to generate a proof" and ship a separate "prover gas" model precisely because of that. The defensible primary metric is prover wall-clock seconds per signature on one pinned machine, at a normalized soundness level, with peak RSS / proof bytes / on-chain verify gas as mandatory co-reported secondaries. Critically, the two systems do NOT default to the same security level (RISC Zero documents 96 bits for the RISC-V prover and 99 for recursion; SP1 documents 100 bits conjectured for Turbo and publishes NO soundness-bit number at all for Hypercube), so any raw time comparison is invalid until that gap is disclosed in every chart.
details:
## 1. Are RISC Zero cycles and SP1 cycles the same unit? No.

**RISC Zero (dev.risczero.com/api/zkvm/optimization, "Guest Optimization Guide", accessed 2026-07-23):**
- "Both in the zkVM and on physical CPUs, the cost of an operation is measured in 'clock cycles'." and "Proving times for the zkVM are directly related to the number of cycles in an execution."
- Per-op charging is explicitly non-uniform: 1-cycle for add/compare/jump/shift-left/load/store; **2 cycles** for AND/OR/XOR, div, rem, shift-right.
- Memory: load/store is "exactly one cycle" only when paged in. Otherwise: **"A page-in or page-out operation takes between 1094 and 5130 cycles; 1130 cycles on average."** First op 5130 cycles (full Merkle path to root).
- Accelerators charge cycles too: "a SHA-256 compress operation typically takes 68 cycles per 64-byte block and 6 cycles to initialize. A 256-bit modular multiply takes 10 cycles."
- Padding is real and structural: `ExecutorEnvBuilder::segment_limit_po2` (docs.rs/risc0-zkvm/latest, accessed 2026-07-23) — "Set a segment limit, specified in powers of 2 cycles. Lowering this value will reduce the memory consumption of the prover. Memory consumption is roughly linear with the segment size, so lowering this value by 1 will cut memory consumpton by about half. The default value is chosen to be performant on commonly used hardware... Given value must be between `risc0_zkp::MIN_CYCLES_PO2` and `risc0_zkp::MAX_CYCLES_PO2` (inclusive)." NOTE: **the numeric default is NOT published on that page** — "The default value is chosen to be performant on commonly used hardware" is all it says. We must read it out of the crate at build time and record it.
- RISC Zero also distinguishes **total cycles vs user cycles** (total = including system/paging overhead; user = guest code). WARNING/DISCONFIRMATION: the canonical benchmarks page I was pointed at, https://www.mintlify.com/risc0/risc0/resources/benchmarks, returns **HTTP 410 Gone** (accessed 2026-07-23). The live page https://dev.risczero.com/api/zkvm/benchmarks does NOT publish numbers, hardware, or metric definitions — it only tells you to generate your own via `cargo run --release --example datasheet` and `cargo bench --bench fib`. So "RISC Zero's published benchmark methodology" is effectively **not published**; do not cite it as if it were.

**SP1 (docs.succinct.xyz, accessed 2026-07-23):**
- Cycle = RISC-V instruction executed, per the cycle-tracking docs ("it is useful to know how many RISC-V cycles a portion of the program takes"). `ExecutionReport` with `profiling` gives `cycle_tracker` (total cycles per tracked section) and `invocation_tracker` (invocation counts). The cycle-tracking page contains **no statement relating cycles to proving cost**.
- The Prover Gas page (docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/optimizing-programs/prover-gas) is the smoking gun: **"cycle counts are not a perfect proxy for the work required to generate a proof."** It gives a concrete counterexample: ECDSA recovery "takes more than twice as long to prove as keccak256-300kb despite having fewer cycles." Prover gas is a **linear-regression statistical model** over each core shard's "padded height of its trace and the base 2 logarithm of this quantity", predicting GPU proving time per shard, summed and scaled. It "incorporates RISC-V cycle count and the different cost profiles of precompiles."
  - Two consequences for us: (a) SP1 itself charges by **padded** shard height, i.e. power-of-two padding is priced in, exactly like RISC Zero segments; (b) prover gas is a **fitted model of one GPU's proving time**, so it is NOT a hardware-neutral unit and it is NOT comparable to RISC Zero cycles. The page does **not** state whether gas is stable across SP1 versions — treat it as version-bound and pin it.
- Shards are power-of-two sized (`SHARD_SIZE` values like `1 << 20`); more cycles → more shards → more recursion. Two runs with the same cycle count but different shard boundary alignment do not cost the same.

**Independent corroboration that nobody knows how to compare them:** Ream Labs, "Integrating block operations with RISC Zero (and some benchmarks)", hackmd.io/@reamlabs/HJhGr8LR1e, dated 2025-04-11, accessed 2026-07-23: **"I have no knowledge whether cycles of different zkVMs are comparable at all. But to compare them as they are..."** and "generally RISC Zero takes more cycles to read the pre-state, but takes less cycles to execute a transition function than SP1's implementation." That is a direct measured asymmetry in the *I/O* path, not the compute path — which for us is fatal, because a signature-batch workload is I/O-heavy (public keys + signatures streamed in).

**Verdict:** cycles are a within-prover regression metric only. In PQ-STARK-BENCH, cycles may appear in a per-prover panel, never on a cross-prover axis. Any chart with "cycles" on the y-axis and prover as the series is a lie by construction.

## 2. The fair common denominator

Ranked recommendation:

**PRIMARY: prover wall-clock seconds per signature, at fixed batch size N, on one pinned machine, at normalized soundness.** Rationale: it is the only quantity that (a) is defined identically for every proof system including Stwo/Cairo which has no RISC-V cycles at all, (b) is what a rollup operator actually pays for, and (c) cannot be gamed by redefining an internal accounting unit. Report as median of >=5 runs with min/max, plus a separate cold-start run.

**MANDATORY CO-REPORTED SECONDARIES (all four, always, same table):**
- **Peak RSS (GB)** — this is the binding constraint, not time. Fenbushi (fenbushicapital.medium.com, 2025-06-18) measured GPU paths "necessitated a minimum of 24GB of VRAM", recommend "a minimum of 32GB of system RAM", memory-intensive systems "128GB of RAM or greater". Also matches ePrint 2021/1048's choice to publish peak RAM alongside time. A prover that is 2x faster but OOMs at N=512 is worse for settlement.
- **Proof bytes** (and which proof: core/compressed/Groth16/PLONK) — the ONLY thing that actually amortizes with batch size, per our Round-1 finding.
- **On-chain verification gas** (and calldata bytes) — the settlement-relevant cost; also the place where the PQ-vs-classical story is real rather than artifactual.
- **$/tx** — derived, not measured: (wall-clock hours x published on-demand instance price) / N. Publish the instance SKU, region, and price-as-of date. Ethproofs already treats "proving cost" as a first-class published metric alongside latency and hardware config, so this is the market-standard framing.

**DO NOT use as primary:** cycles (incomparable, §1); SP1 prover gas (SP1-only, GPU-fitted, version-bound); throughput in kHz (RISC Zero's `fib` bench reports "time and throughput (how many numbers were added per second)" — a per-prover unit); Boundless PoVW cycles (RISC Zero-ecosystem-specific pricing unit; and no concrete $/cycle figure is published in anything I could fetch — **"per cycle" pricing figures: not published**).

**Cross-cutting normalizer to publish next to every number: seconds per signature per bit-of-conjectured-soundness is NOT a legitimate normalization** (soundness is not linear in time) — instead, re-run at matched parameters where the API allows it, and where it does not, state the mismatch in the chart footnote. See §4.

## 3. Known traps in zkVM benchmarking (with sources)

1. **Precompile asymmetry.** Justin Thaler / a16z crypto, "Understanding Jolt: clarifications and reflections" (dated 06.03.24 on page): "a RISC-V zkVM with one or more precompiles is no longer a zkVM for RISC-V: It is a zkVM for the new instruction set based on RISC-V by adding each precompile as a primitive instruction." And in "FAQs on Jolt's initial implementation" (dated 04.09.24): **"Our benchmarks seek to assess zkVMs and hence omit the use of precompiles."** and **"Any speedup over about 5x is not due to improvements in the zkVM, but rather to better precompiles."** Directly load-bearing for us: neither RISC Zero nor SP1 has an ML-DSA/NTT precompile, but SP1 has sha3/SHAKE and both have Ed25519/ECDSA acceleration — so a naive PQ-vs-classical chart double-counts the precompile gap.
2. **Confounding via engineering maturity.** Thaler again: "Engineering effort represents one of these confounding factors, although much of the community appears to take an opposite view", and "it will be difficult to strike the right balance between under- and over-controlling for confounding factors. Disagreements will be inevitable, and builders will have to provide the full context, details, and rationales behind any benchmarks so the community can understand and discuss them."
3. **Dueling-optimizations framing.** Blockchain.news summary of Thaler: "project A might show a 30x performance boost by comparing against project B's implementations without precompiles, while project B might achieve similar gains over project A through GPU enablement."
4. **Incomparable methodologies as the norm.** Fenbushi Capital, "Benchmarking zkVMs: Current State and Prospects" (2025-06-18): "most existing benchmarks employ incomparable methodologies, thereby obscuring crucial performance trade-offs"; recommend evaluations incorporate "assessments of security maturity, including factors such as formal verification efforts, completed third-party audits"; note "optimization exclusively for performance can potentially compromise security guarantees."
5. **Dev-mode / mock-prover contamination.** RISC Zero `RISC0_DEV_MODE=1` executes without proving and emits fake receipts (dev.risczero.com/api/generating-proofs/dev-mode); the `disable-dev-mode` feature makes the binary panic if the env var is present. SP1's `ProverClient` exposes `MockProver` alongside `LocalProver`/`NetworkProver`. Any harness that does not assert-at-runtime that it is in real-proving mode will eventually publish a fake number.
6. **Non-linearity of proving cost in workload size.** Aligned, "Neutral, standardized benchmarking of zkVM proving times" (blog.alignedlayer.com/zkbenchmarks/, dated 2025-03-24): "the relationship between proving time and the composition and size of the block being proved have a non-linear relationship". For us: never interpolate between measured batch sizes; measure every N you plot.
7. **Underspecified harnesses.** DISCONFIRMATION, stated loudly: the a16z/zkvm-benchmarks README (raw.githubusercontent.com/a16z/zkvm-benchmarks/master/README.md, accessed 2026-07-23) contains **only** install commands, `make bench-all`, "results should be outputted in CSV form in `benchmark_outputs`", and a legal disclaimer. It specifies **no hardware, no zkVM versions, no security parameters, no precompile policy, no repetition count**. The `main` branch path 404s; content is on `master`. It is a runner, not a methodology — do not cite it as a methodological model.
8. **Ethproofs is a leaderboard, not a spec.** ethproofs.org/learn (accessed 2026-07-23) does **not** contain formal definitions of proving time, proving cost, cycles, or any statement about normalization across teams — verbatim finding: no metric definitions and no comparability caveats on that page. It does publish per-proof hardware config, cost, latency, proof size, verification time, and audit status, and defines real-time proving as proving 90%+ of mainnet blocks in under 12 seconds. Model the *reporting schema* on Ethproofs; do not claim it gives you a normalization procedure.
9. **Security-level mismatch (the trap almost nobody controls for).** See §4.

## 4. Do RISC Zero and SP1 default to the same security level? NO.

**RISC Zero — dev.risczero.com/api/security-model (accessed 2026-07-23):**
- RISC-V Prover: **96 bits**, quantum-safe.
- Recursion Prover: **99 bits**, quantum-safe.
- STARK-to-SNARK Prover: **99+ bits**, not quantum safe.
- On-chain Groth16-over-BN254 verifier: **96 bits**, not quantum safe.
- Assumption: "The Toy Problem conjecture, specified in detail in the ethSTARK documentation, says that the best known attack on STARK proof systems is the best possible attack."

**SP1 — docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/security/security-model (accessed 2026-07-23):**
- SP1 **Turbo**: "SP1 Turbo's FRI parameters have `num_queries = 100 / log_blowup` with `proof_of_work_bits = 16`" and "Despite recent attacks on proximity gaps, these parameters still conjecturally provide **100 bits of security**." BabyBear field P=15*2^27+1, Poseidon2 SBOX degree 7, 13 internal rounds.
- SP1 **Hypercube**: "SP1 Hypercube does not rely on proximity gap conjectures. Rather, it relies on proximity gap theorems established in (https://eprint.iacr.org/2020/654.pdf), while operating in the unique decoding regime." KoalaBear field P=2^31-2^24+1, Poseidon2 width 16, SBOX degree 3, 8 external + 20 internal rounds.
- **DISCONFIRMATION / GAP TO STATE LOUDLY: the SP1 security-model page publishes NO soundness-bit number for Hypercube's FRI.** The only "bits" figure on the page besides Turbo's 100 is "the selected elliptic curve provides at least 100 bits of security against known attacks" (that is the extension-field EC used in recursion, not the FRI soundness). And **the page does not state which configuration is the default in the current release.** So "SP1's default soundness bits" is: **not published**. We must determine it from the shipped `FriConfig`/verifier constants of the exact version we pin (v6.3.1) and publish the constants we read, labeled as our own inference, not as a vendor claim.
- On-chain: "SP1 utilizes Gnark's implementation of Groth16 or PLONK over the BN254 curve" and "assumes all cryptographic assumptions required for the security of Groth16 and PLONK" — **no bit-level figure given for the wrapper.**

**Net:** RISC Zero's documented STARK layer is 96 bits; SP1 Turbo's documented claim is 100 bits conjectured; SP1 Hypercube's is unstated. That is a >=4-bit documented gap plus one unknown. FRI soundness scales roughly with query count, and query count scales prover *and* verifier work, so a 96-vs-100-bit comparison is a real thumb on the scale, in RISC Zero's favor on time.

**How to set them.** RISC Zero exposes `segment_limit_po2` (memory/time knob, NOT a soundness knob); the FRI query count is baked into the circuit/verifier and is not a user-facing parameter in the public API I could find — **user-settable RISC Zero soundness parameters: not published**. SP1's `num_queries`/`log_blowup`/`proof_of_work_bits` are named in its docs as configuration of the FRI config, but the docs page does not document a supported user API for changing them (changing them would break the canonical verifier and the on-chain wrapper anyway). Practical consequence: **you cannot equalize soundness by config.** Therefore the only defensible move is disclosure + a sensitivity note, not silent normalization.

**Supporting cryptographic context for why this matters (fetch status noted):** Block & Tiwari, "On the Concrete Security of Non-interactive FRI", eprint.iacr.org/2024/1161, SCN 2024 — deployed FRI parameters "nearly achieve their desired security targets (being at most 1-bit less secure than their targets)" *conjecturally*, but "In all but one set of parameters, the provable security of non-interactive FRI under these parameters is severely lacking, being anywhere between 21- and 63-bits less secure than the conjectured security." NOTE: I read this from search-result extraction and the free eprint listing; the Springer chapter (link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-71070-4_13) is **paywalled — it 303-redirects to idp.springer.com/authorize**. Before we cite the exact wording in a paper, fetch the PDF at eprint.iacr.org/2024/1161 directly and re-verify verbatim.

## 5. Recommended methodology for PQ-STARK-BENCH (defensible to a cryptographer)

**A. Declare the unit and the disclaimers up front.**
- Primary axis: **amortized prover wall-clock seconds per signature** = (median total prove wall-clock for batch N) / N. Secondary axes: peak RSS, proof bytes, verifier gas, $/tx.
- On every cross-prover figure, a fixed footer block: prover + exact version, guest toolchain version, proof mode (core / compressed / Groth16 / PLONK), documented soundness bits (RISC Zero 96 STARK / 96 Groth16; SP1 100 conjectured Turbo / **unpublished** Hypercube), precompiles enabled, hardware SKU, and date.
- A permanent banner sentence, per our Round-1 finding: *neither prover has a lattice/NTT/ML-DSA/Falcon precompile while both accelerate Ed25519/ECDSA, so the PQ-vs-classical ratio shown here overstates the intrinsic PQ penalty.*

**B. Run two precompile regimes, always both.**
- **Regime A "as-shipped"**: every precompile the prover offers, enabled. This is what an operator would deploy.
- **Regime B "level playing field"**: precompiles disabled (or, minimally, SHA3/SHAKE disabled on SP1 so it is not compared against a RISC Zero that lacks one). This follows Thaler's stated benchmarking policy verbatim ("Our benchmarks seek to assess zkVMs and hence omit the use of precompiles"). Publishing only Regime A is the single most common way these benchmarks mislead.

**C. Pin everything, hash everything.**
- Pin prover versions (SP1 v6.3.1, RISC Zero v3.0.6), Rust toolchain, guest ELF. **Publish the guest ELF's image ID / vkey hash** so a third party can prove they ran the same program. This is the zkVM-native equivalent of a checksum and no existing harness I found does it.
- Record and publish, per run: total cycles (per-prover panel only), segment/shard count, `segment_limit_po2` / `SHARD_SIZE` actually in effect (read at runtime, not assumed), SP1 prover gas (SP1 panel only), peak RSS via cgroup/`/usr/bin/time -v`, wall-clock from process start to receipt-in-hand.

**D. Hard guards against the fake-number class of bug.**
- Build RISC Zero guests with the `disable-dev-mode` feature so a stray `RISC0_DEV_MODE` panics rather than silently producing a fake receipt.
- Assert `SP1_PROVER != mock` at harness start; refuse to write a results row otherwise.
- **Verify every proof before recording its timing row**, and record verification wall-clock. A timing number attached to a proof that was never verified is worthless.

**E. Statistics.**
- N in {1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512, 1024} where RAM permits; record OOM as a first-class result (an OOM at N=512 is a finding, not a missing cell).
- >=5 repetitions, report median + min + max, discard run 1 (page cache / JIT / GPU clock warm-up) but publish it separately as cold-start.
- Never interpolate between measured N — Aligned explicitly documents the non-linearity.
- One machine, one prover at a time, no co-tenancy; publish `lscpu`/`nvidia-smi`/`system_profiler` output as an artifact.

**F. Hardware honesty (specific to our constraint).**
- Per Round-1: SP1 v6.3.1 on macOS aarch64 has **no** GPU acceleration (CUDA is Linux x86_64, AVX is Intel-only) while RISC Zero v3.0.6 **does** use Metal on Apple Silicon. Therefore **any Apple Silicon number is a RISC-Zero-favoring configuration and must never be used for the headline cross-prover chart.** Use Apple Silicon for development/smoke only; run the publishable matrix on one Linux x86_64 box (CPU-only for the fair baseline, then a separate CUDA panel). Also per Round-1, RISC Zero's Groth16 prover is x86-only, so the "settlement-ready proof" leg cannot even exist on the Mac.

**G. Reporting schema.** Model the published table on Ethproofs' field set (per-proof: proving time, proving cost, hardware config, proof size, verification time, audit status, open-source status) since that is the field set the ecosystem already reads — but add the three fields Ethproofs does NOT define or publish: documented soundness bits, precompile regime, and program identity hash. Ship raw per-run JSON + a Jupyter/notebook reproduction (the one genuinely good habit from Aligned's zkbenchmarks, which shipped "a fully reproducible Jupyter notebook").

**H. What we explicitly refuse to claim.**
- No cross-prover cycle chart.
- No "X is faster than Y" headline without both precompile regimes and the soundness-bit footnote.
- No claim about SP1 Hypercube's soundness bits until we read them out of the pinned source, and then labeled as our inference.
- No batch-amortization "discovery" framing (Round-1: prover time and RAM are linear in N; only proof bytes amortize).
