blockers: 1) SP1 Hypercube's soundness bits are NOT published anywhere I could fetch, and the SP1 docs do not say which config (Turbo vs Hypercube) is default in the shipped release. Until we read the FriConfig constants out of the pinned v6.3.1 source, our security-normalization footnote is incomplete. Fallback: publish RISC Zero's 96/99 as vendor-documented, SP1 Turbo's 100 as vendor-documented, and mark SP1 Hypercube "not published; constants read from source at commit <sha>" as our own inference. 2) RISC Zero's default segment_limit_po2 numeric value is not on the docs page - must be read from the crate at build time and recorded per run. 3) Soundness levels are NOT user-configurable in either prover's public API as far as the docs show, so we cannot equalize them; the fallback is mandatory disclosure in every chart footer plus an explicit "not normalized" caveat, never a silent comparison. 4) Two supporting sources are unverified at the verbatim level: eprint 2024/1161 (read via search extraction, Springer version paywalled) and the blockchain.news Thaler summary (search summary only). Re-fetch both before quoting them in public copy. 5) RISC Zero's own benchmark page publishes no methodology and the older mintlify benchmarks URL is 410 Gone - do not cite "RISC Zero's published benchmarks" as prior art. 6) Hardware asymmetry is unresolvable on Apple Silicon (SP1 has no GPU path there, RISC Zero has Metal); the headline matrix must run on Linux x86_64 or the comparison is invalid.

topic: PQ-STARK-BENCH Phase 2 feasibility: will the Rust PQ signature crates compile and run inside riscv32im zkVM guests (RISC Zero / SP1)?
bottom_line: Phase 2 is FEASIBLE. fips204 (ML-DSA) is proven working in BOTH provers by third-party code — SP1 with a full measured cycle breakdown (postfiatorg/postfiatl1v2, 2026-07-18), RISC Zero via a shipped guest Cargo.lock (AXIOM-Origin-Validator/axiom-core). Falcon verification is proven working in SP1 via zmanian/sp1-falcon using Pornin's fn-dsa-vrfy, which is `#![no_std]`, stack-only, integer-only. The one NO-GO is the pqcrypto-* PQClean C wrappers: zero public evidence of anyone building them in a zkVM guest, and the pure-Rust fallback is strictly better anyway. IMPORTANT CORRECTION to Round 1: "ML-DSA in a general-purpose zkVM = only hobby repos with no measurements" is now WRONG — PostFiat published a 13-row cycle-tracker table for ML-DSA-65 verify inside SP1 6.3.1.
details:
All URLs fetched 2026-07-23.

=====================================================================
0. HEADLINE DISCONFIRMATION OF A ROUND-1 FINDING
=====================================================================
Round 1 said: "The genuinely empty cell is ML-DSA-44 in a general-purpose zkVM: only hobby repos with no measurements."

That is now materially wrong for ML-DSA-65. Found via GitHub code search:

REPO: postfiatorg/postfiatl1v2
FILE: docs/specs/mldsa-ntt-precompile-spec.md
TITLE: "ML-DSA-65 verification acceleration for the SP1 egress guest"
Visible date in doc: "Date: 2026-07-18". "SP1: `6.3.1`; ML-DSA oracle: crates.io `fips204 = 0.4.6`"
URL: https://github.com/postfiatorg/postfiatl1v2/blob/main/docs/specs/mldsa-ntt-precompile-spec.md

Verbatim measured numbers (MEASURED-AND-PUBLISHED by the repo authors on their own hardware; NOT peer-reviewed, NOT a paper):
- "ML-DSA-65 verify calls observed by the tracker: 468; distinct `public_key_hex` values: 6"
- Tracked verify total row: "468 | 695,624,363 | 1,486,377 | 100.00%" (calls | total cycles | cycles/verify | share)
  => 1,486,377 SP1 cycles per ML-DSA-65 verification, reference path, WITH official SP1 6.0.0 SHA2/SHA3 patches beneath it.
- Component split (cycles/verify): SHAKE total 439,183 (29.55%); Forward+inverse NTT 358,724 (24.13%); Pointwise polynomial arithmetic 177,138 (11.92%); public-key+signature decode 161,206 (10.85%); hint reconstruction + commitment encode 90,170 (6.07%); other/residual 259,956 (17.49%).
- Label detail: "`mldsa.shake.expand_a` | 468 | 196,832,292 | 420,582" — ExpandA alone is ~28% of one verify.
- "Tracked verification is 48.80% of the instrumented whole guest."
- Whole-guest before/after: "Execute cycles | 1,424,788,289 | 1,050,064,162 | -374,724,127 (-26.30%)"; "Maximum host RSS | 11,160,140 KiB | 10,647,548 KiB | -512,592 KiB (-4.59%)".
- CUDA proof: "SP1 6.3.1 ran its GPU server on the RTX 5090, with an observed 65% utilization and 22,006 MiB VRAM during a sampled core-proving interval"; "proof report `setup_and_prove_ms`: 122,161 ms"; "Groth16 calldata: 356 bytes"; "serialized proof: 3,181 bytes".
- Soundness caveat worth quoting in any chart: "A host hook that returns an NTT result without an AIR constraint is unsound: a malicious prover could choose coefficients that make an invalid signature pass."
- Independently confirms Round 1's precompile-asymmetry point: "A true SP1 NTT syscall is a promising follow-up, but it requires an SP1 executor/AIR change so that every returned coefficient is constrained; that change is outside this repository's consensus boundary."

MY INFERENCE (not measured): 1,486,377 cycles/verify for ML-DSA-65 is the best calibration number available for scoping Phase 2. ML-DSA-44 should be cheaper (K=4,L=4 vs K=6,L=5) but I found NO published ML-DSA-44 zkVM number — that cell remains genuinely empty.

=====================================================================
1. PER-CRATE GO / NO-GO
=====================================================================

--- fips204 (ML-DSA) — GO on RISC Zero, GO on SP1 (highest confidence) ---
https://docs.rs/fips204/latest/fips204/ — version 0.4.6, released 2026-05-23. Verbatim: "The implementation's key- and signature-generation functionality operates in constant-time, does not require the standard library, e.g. `#[no_std]`, has no heap allocations, e.g. no `alloc` needed." MSRV: "Requires Rust **1.70** or higher." No floating point mentioned anywhere.
Feature table https://docs.rs/crate/fips204/latest/features — "This version has 5 feature flags, 4 of them enabled by default": `default-rng` (default), `ml-dsa-44` (default), `ml-dsa-65` (default), `ml-dsa-87` (default); non-default: `dudect`.
BUILD TRAP: `default-rng` pulls rand_core/getrandom. On bare metal that fails to LINK (not compile). Both real users handle it explicitly:
 - AXIOM guest (RISC Zero) avm-guest/src/main.rs verbatim: "The guest only verifies signatures — never generates keys — so getrandom is never called at runtime. This satisfies the linker requirement from ed25519-dalek, fips204, and fips205 which pull in rand_core → getrandom. use getrandom::register_custom_getrandom;"
 - AXIOM avm/Cargo.toml: `fips204 = { version = "0.4", default-features = false, features = ["ml-dsa-65"], optional = true }`
RECOMMENDATION: always `default-features = false, features = ["ml-dsa-44"]` for verify-only benchmarks.
EVIDENCE OF RUNNING:
 - RISC Zero: https://github.com/AXIOM-Origin-Validator/axiom-core/blob/main/zkvm-guest/guest/Cargo.lock contains `name = "fips204" / version = "0.4.6"` in a guest lockfile whose guest Cargo.toml declares `risc0-zkvm = { version = "3.0", default-features = false, features = ["getrandom"] }`. Same for avm-guest/Cargo.lock, whose Cargo.toml says "This crate compiles to riscv32im-unknown-none-elf".
 - SP1: the PostFiat spec above, plus https://github.com/postfiatorg/postfiatl1v2/blob/main/crates/crypto_provider/Cargo.toml with `sp1-zkvm = { version = "=6.3.1", optional = true }` and features `mldsa-guest-acceleration = ["fips204/precompute-verify-matrix", "fips204/riscv32-verify-arithmetic"]`, `sp1-cycle-tracking = ["fips204/sp1-cycle-tracking", "dep:sp1-zkvm"]`. NOTE those three features are from their VENDORED fork at third_party/fips204, NOT upstream crates.io.
FALLBACK if it breaks: RustCrypto `ml-dsa` 0.1.1 (2026-06-05, https://docs.rs/ml-dsa/latest/ml_dsa/), "Pure Rust implementation of the Module-Lattice-Based Digital Signature Standard (ML-DSA) as described in the FIPS 204 (final)". Its docs.rs front page does NOT state no_std — unverified, do not assume.

--- fips205 (SLH-DSA) — GO on RISC Zero (evidence), GO on SP1 (inferred) ---
https://docs.rs/fips205/latest/fips205/ — version 0.4.1, released 2026-05-23. Verbatim: "does not require the standard library, e.g. `#[no_std]`", "has no heap allocations, e.g. no `alloc` needed", "from server down to the bare-metal". Deps: rand_core, sha2, sha3, zeroize.
Features https://docs.rs/crate/fips205/latest/features — "13 feature flags, 13 of them enabled by default": `default-rng` (= "rand_core/^0.6.4/getrandom") plus all 12 parameter sets slh_dsa_{sha2,shake}_{128,192,256}{f,s}. Same getrandom trap.
EVIDENCE: fips205 appears in AXIOM's RISC Zero guest lockfile (zkvm-guest/guest/Cargo.lock) and in the guest source comment quoted above. AXIOM root Cargo.toml pins `fips205 = { version = "0.4", features = ["slh_dsa_sha2_128s"] }` — note NO `default-features = false` there, a latent trap.
NO published SP1 evidence found. Pure-Rust no_std with the same dependency shape as fips204, so SP1 is a safe inference.
PERF (inference, not measured): SLH-DSA verify is thousands of hash calls. SP1 HAS a sha3/SHAKE precompile (`sha3 = { git = "https://github.com/sp1-patches/RustCrypto-hashes", package = "sha3", tag = "patch-sha3-0.11.0-sp1-6.0.0" }`, versions 0.10.8 and 0.11.0). RISC Zero's precompile table has NO sha3 row. Expect a large prover-dependent asymmetry on SLH-DSA specifically; must be disclosed per Round 1's rule.
FALLBACK: RustCrypto `slh-dsa` 0.1.0 (2025-02-05, https://docs.rs/slh-dsa/latest/slh_dsa/). WARNING verbatim: "This crate currently allocates signatures and intermediate values on the stack, which may cause problems for environments with limited stack space." Real risk in a zkVM guest.

--- Falcon / FN-DSA VERIFICATION: fn-dsa-vrfy — GO on SP1 (proven), GO on RISC Zero (inferred) ---
THIS IS THE RIGHT CRATE. https://docs.rs/fn-dsa-vrfy/latest/src/fn_dsa_vrfy/lib.rs.html line 1 is literally `#![no_std]`. Doc comment verbatim: "`verify()` uses stack allocation for its internal buffers (which are not large)." No `alloc`, no `f64`, no `float` anywhere in the file.
Version 0.4.0, released 2026-07-22 (one day before access). Only dependency: `fn-dsa-comm ^0.4` (dev-dep `hex`).
THE FLOAT QUESTION IS ANSWERED, from https://github.com/pornin/rust-fn-dsa README: "Key pair generation and signature verification use only integer operations." And: "on 64-bit x86 (`x86_64`) and ARMv8 (`aarch64`), the native floating-point type (`f64`) is used in signature generation, because on such platforms the type maps to the hardware support"; "on other platforms (including 32-bit x86 and 32-bit ARM), an integer-only implementation is used, which emulates the expected IEEE-754 primitives". Sub-crate rationale verbatim: "some applications will need only a subset of the features (typically, only verification)".
So: SIGN needs float emulation, VERIFY is integer-only. Verify in-guest, sign on the host. Exactly the Phase 2 shape.
PROOF SOMEONE DID IT: https://github.com/zmanian/sp1-falcon (Zaki Manian). README verbatim: "This program is a proof of concept for verifying the Falcon post quantum signature. It uses https://github.com/pornin/rust-fn-dsa for the falcon implementation." lib/Cargo.toml: `fn-dsa-vrfy = "0.3.0"`. program/Cargo.toml: `sp1-zkvm = "4.0.0"`. Commit "Working proving" f2d31be, 2025-02-23T22:46:00Z; head commit "add timing code" f0df600, 2025-02-23T22:57:19Z.
LOUD CAVEAT: NO CYCLE NUMBERS ARE PUBLISHED. script/src/bin/main.rs only contains `println!("Number of cycles: {}", report.total_instruction_count());` — no result is committed anywhere in the repo. Capability proven, measurement NOT published. That cell is still empty and is a real opportunity for PQ-STARK-BENCH.
CORRECTNESS WARNING to reproduce verbatim in any chart: docs.rs fn-dsa says "The FN-DSA standard is currently being drafted, but no version has been published yet ... Key pairs generated with this crate MAY fail to be interoperable with the final FN-DSA standard." The GitHub README adds: "what is implemented here is _not_ the 'real' FN-DSA". Do NOT label fn-dsa results "FIPS 206".
OTHER FALCON OPTION (weaker): `falcon-rs` 0.2.5, 2026-07-20, https://docs.rs/falcon-rs/latest/falcon/ — claims pure Rust and a `no_std` feature, BUT depends on `libm ^0.2`, i.e. software float. Prefer fn-dsa-vrfy. `Quantus-Network/rusty-falcon` also exists (unassessed).
Third-party confirmation the verify-only split is standard practice: https://github.com/carl-wallace/rust-pki certval/Cargo.toml comment verbatim: "# The verify-only sub-crate keeps signing/keygen (and their float emulation) out of the build." with `fn-dsa-vrfy = { version = "0.3.0", default-features = false, optional = true }`.

--- pqcrypto-mldsa / pqcrypto-falcon / pqcrypto-sphincsplus (PQClean C) — NO-GO (soft) ---
CAN C COMPILE TO A ZKVM GUEST? YES, both provers ship a RISC-V C toolchain:
 - RISC Zero https://dev.risczero.com/api/zkvm/rust-crates-with-cpp: `rzup install` "download[s] the guest Rust toolchain as well as a C++ toolchain that compiles C++ to riscv32im". Override via `CC_riscv32im_risc0_zkvm_elf` / `CFLAGS_riscv32im_risc0_zkvm_elf="-target riscv32-unknown-elf -march=rv32im"`. Explicit caveat verbatim: "Not all C code is guaranteed to build or run on the RISC Zero zkVM out of the box."
 - SP1 — verified in SOURCE, not just docs: https://github.com/succinctlabs/sp1/blob/main/sp1up/sp1up contains `export CC_riscv32im_succinct_zkvm_elf="$SP1_BIN_DIR/riscv32-unknown-elf-gcc"`, and crates/build/src/command/local.rs contains "// If CC_riscv32im_succinct_zkvm_elf is not set, set it to the default C++ toolchain downloaded by 'sp1up --c-toolchain'." Install with `sp1up --c-toolchain`.
   LOUD DOC BUG: https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/developers/common-issues renders the variable as `CC_riscv64im_succinct_zkvm_elf` and the binary as `riscv64-unknown-elf-gcc`. That contradicts the source (riscv32 / riscv32-unknown-elf-gcc). Trust the source. Separately, https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/developers/common-issues.md returns HTTP 404, and https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/writing-programs/compiling contains NO C-toolchain section at all.
CRATE IS GENUINELY no_std: https://docs.rs/pqcrypto-mldsa/latest/src/pqcrypto_mldsa/lib.rs.html has `#![no_std]` on line 13, `extern crate alloc;` ("// For no-std vectors"), and `#[cfg(feature = "std")] extern crate std;`. Version 0.1.2 (2025-08-05). Deps: `libc ^0.2.0`, build-dep `cc ^1.0`, `pqcrypto-internals ^0.2.6`, `pqcrypto-traits ^0.3.5`, `paste ^1.0`. So it DOES need alloc (unlike fips204/205).
pqcrypto-internals is also `#![no_std]` and exists only for entropy: `pub unsafe extern "C" fn PQCRYPTO_RUST_randombytes(buf: *mut u8, len: libc::size_t) -> libc::c_int { ... getrandom::fill(buf).expect("RNG Failed"); 0 }`. libc is used only for type aliases. So the same custom-getrandom stub trick would apply.
WHY NO-GO ANYWAY — SEARCH DISCONFIRMATION: GitHub code search for pqcrypto-* co-occurring with risc0/sp1 in a Cargo.toml returns 8 repos, and on inspection EVERY ONE is host-side or commented out: Luminous-Dynamics/symthaea, Numi2/Blockchain-Ledger-with-Blake3-hash-Dilithium3-sig, Zajfan/Phantom ("# zkVM (to be added later)"), robertringler/QRATUM ("# risc0-zkvm ... optional"), palaseus/Hauptbuch, sinescode/vcdn, TET-Network-Foundation/TET-OS, SealProjectDAO/seal-dao-public. Query `pqcrypto-dilithium OR pqcrypto-falcon filename:Cargo.toml risc0-zkvm OR sp1-zkvm` returns 0 results. Query `"fn-dsa" filename:Cargo.toml zkvm` returns 0 results. Nobody has publicly built PQClean-C PQ signatures in a zkVM guest.
UNRESOLVED: whether libc's default `std` feature breaks a no_std guest build, and whether PQClean's <string.h>/<stdint.h> use is satisfied by each prover's bundled newlib. I could not close these without compiling.
FALLBACK: drop pqcrypto entirely. fips204 + fips205 + fn-dsa-vrfy cover all three FIPS schemes in pure Rust with better zkVM ergonomics and no C toolchain dependency in CI.

--- ed25519-dalek / curve25519-dalek — GO both, PRECOMPILE-ACCELERATED both ---
RISC Zero https://dev.risczero.com/api/zkvm/precompiles: crate `curve25519-dalek`, versions "4.1.3, 4.1.2, 4.1.1, 4.1.0", fork https://github.com/risc0/curve25519-dalek, patch line verbatim: `ed25519-dalek = { git = "https://github.com/risc0/curve25519-dalek", tag = "curve25519-4.1.3-risczero.0" }` — yes, ed25519-dalek is patched FROM the curve25519-dalek repo; quote it exactly.
SP1 https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/optimizing-programs/precompiles: `curve25519-dalek = { git = "https://github.com/sp1-patches/curve25519-dalek", tag = "patch-4.1.3-sp1-6.0.0" }` (4.1.3); also `curve25519-dalek-ng = { git = "https://github.com/sp1-patches/curve25519-dalek-ng", tag = "patch-4.1.1-sp1-6.0.0" }` (4.1.1).
Real-world RISC Zero note from AXIOM zkvm-guest/guest/Cargo.toml (their claim, NOT a measurement I verified): "Ed25519 precompile: curve25519-dalek risc0 fork replaces ~500M RISC-V cycles with a single syscall. Local copy with hex dep fixed to no_std. crypto-bigint: risc0 fork required by curve25519-dalek risc0 backend." They add `crypto-bigint = { git = "https://github.com/risc0/RustCrypto-crypto-bigint", tag = "v0.5.5-risczero.0" }` — budget for that transitive patch.

--- k256 — GO both, precompiled both ---
RISC Zero: `k256`, versions "0.13.4, 0.13.3, 0.13.2, 0.13.1", https://github.com/risc0/RustCrypto-elliptic-curves, `k256 = { git = "...", tag = "k256/v0.13.3-risczero.1" }`.
SP1: `k256 = { git = "https://github.com/sp1-patches/elliptic-curves", tag = "patch-k256-13.4-sp1-6.0.0" }`, version listed as "13.4".

--- secp256k1 (the C bindgen crate) — GO on SP1, NOT LISTED on RISC Zero ---
SP1 lists it: `secp256k1 = { git = "https://github.com/sp1-patches/rust-secp256k1", tag = "patch-0.29.1-sp1-6.0.0" }`, versions "0.29.1, 0.30.0".
RISC Zero's precompile table has NO `secp256k1` row (k256 and p256 only).
FALLBACK / FAIRNESS: use k256 on BOTH provers for the ECDSA baseline. Using secp256k1 on SP1 and k256 on RISC Zero would silently bias the cross-prover comparison.

=====================================================================
2. TARGET-LEVEL CONSTRAINTS
=====================================================================
https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/platform-support/riscv32im-risc0-zkvm-elf.html — Tier 3. "Experimental support for `std`. The target supports `alloc` with a default allocator." So std is not forbidden, just experimental — which matters because pqcrypto needs alloc.
Environment, verbatim: "Single threaded, non-preemptive execution environment"; "No privileged instructions or unaligned accesses supported"; "192 MB of memory with address range `0x400` - `0x0C000000` for text/data, heap, and stack"; C: "clang and ld.lld linker support this target with -march=rv32im, -mabi=ilp32".
No hardware float (`im` only, no F/D extension) — which is precisely why fn-dsa VERIFY (integer-only) is fine and fn-dsa SIGN would hit software emulation.

=====================================================================
3. PHASE 2 RECOMMENDED CRATE MATRIX
=====================================================================
ML-DSA-44    -> fips204 0.4.6, default-features=false, features=["ml-dsa-44"]        GO / GO
SLH-DSA-128s -> fips205 0.4.1, default-features=false, features=["slh_dsa_sha2_128s"] GO (RISC Zero evidence) / GO (SP1 inferred); expect SP1 advantage from sha3 precompile
FN-DSA-512   -> fn-dsa-vrfy 0.4.0, verify in guest, sign on host                     GO (SP1 proven) / GO (RISC Zero inferred)
Ed25519      -> risc0 or sp1-patched curve25519-dalek 4.1.3                          GO / GO, precompiled (must be disclosed)
ECDSA-k256   -> risc0 or sp1-patched k256                                            GO / GO, precompiled (must be disclosed)
In every guest: verify-only, no keygen, and register a panic-stub custom getrandom backend.

blockers:
BLOCKER 1 (medium, will definitely be hit): the getrandom link error. fips204, fips205 and ed25519-dalek all pull rand_core -> getrandom, which has no bare-metal backend. It surfaces as a LINK failure, not a compile failure, so it appears late in the build. FALLBACK: register a panic-stub custom backend exactly as AXIOM does (getrandom 0.2 `custom` feature + register_custom_getrandom, or a getrandom 0.3 custom backend), and always set `default-features = false` to drop `default-rng`. AXIOM also had to pin `getrandom = "=0.3.2"` against risc0-zkvm-platform 2.2.2 — expect version-pinning pain on RISC Zero 3.x.

BLOCKER 2 (medium): 192 MB total guest memory on riscv32im-risc0-zkvm-elf covers text+data+heap+stack combined. Batching N signatures into ONE guest run will hit this before it hits anything else, and SLH-DSA signatures are large. FALLBACK: batch via continuations/segments or via N separate guest runs aggregated on the host, and state the batching mode explicitly. Round 1 already established prover time and RAM grow linearly in N, so nothing scientific is lost.

BLOCKER 3 (low probability, high embarrassment): pqcrypto-* has zero public zkVM precedent, needs alloc, and has unresolved libc-std / newlib questions. FALLBACK: do not use it at all — pure-Rust fips204 / fips205 / fn-dsa-vrfy cover all three FIPS schemes and remove the C toolchain from CI entirely.

BLOCKER 4 (positioning, not technical): the PostFiat SP1 ML-DSA-65 measurement is dated 2026-07-18, five days before this research. Any copy asserting "nobody has measured ML-DSA in a zkVM" will be refuted immediately. Reposition to what is actually empty: ML-DSA-44 specifically; cross-prover RISC Zero vs SP1 side-by-side; and Falcon/FN-DSA cycle counts (zmanian/sp1-falcon proves it runs but published nothing).

BLOCKER 5 (correctness labelling): fn-dsa is explicitly NOT the final standard - "what is implemented here is _not_ the 'real' FN-DSA". Label that row "Falcon (fn-dsa 0.4.0, pre-standard draft)", never "FIPS 206".

BLOCKER 6 (fairness disclosure): SP1 has a sha3/SHAKE precompile and RISC Zero does not list one. SHAKE is 29.55% of an ML-DSA-65 verify per PostFiat's measurement, and dominates SLH-DSA entirely. So a large part of any cross-prover PQ gap will be a SHAKE-precompile artifact rather than a prover-quality result. This must appear on every chart alongside the Ed25519/ECDSA asymmetry from Round 1.

VERIFICATION GAP I could not close: I compiled nothing. Everything above is documentation plus third-party source evidence. The cheapest de-risking step is a hello-world guest doing one fips204 ml-dsa-44 verify on both provers, before any harness work.

topic: What "verify 1000 signatures in one proof" means operationally in a zkVM, and what breaks (batching, memory, continuations/recursion, amortization curve shape, real large-batch precedents, and realistic local batch sizes)
bottom_line: Yes, a batch is literally a loop over N verifications in one guest program; you do not hit a "batch limit" so much as three separate ceilings: guest address space (RISC Zero hard-caps guest memory at GUEST_MAX_MEM = 0xC000_0000 = 3,221,225,472 bytes), the per-segment/per-shard trace budget (RISC Zero DEFAULT_SEGMENT_LIMIT_PO2 = 20, i.e. 1,048,576 cycles; SP1 shards on trace area, ELEMENT_THRESHOLD = (1 << 28) + (1 << 27) with MAX_SHARD_SIZE = 1 << 24), and host prover RAM per segment. Continuations/sharding mean prover peak RAM is bounded by segment size, not by total cycles ("Lowering this value will reduce the memory consumption of the prover" — risc0 segment_limit_po2 docs), so on a 64 GB machine the wall is WALL-CLOCK TIME, not OOM, as long as you lower the segment/shard size. CRITICAL CORRECTION to the round-1 finding: measured data shows amortized per-item cost is NOT flat at small N — it is dominated by a large constant floor and only becomes flat once the batch saturates one shard. On an RTX A6000, SP1-AVX2 proving Keccak took 15.3s at N=100, 15.3s at N=1,000, 15.3s at N=10,000, 15.9s at N=100,000, then 26.3s at N=1,000,000 and 2m11.5s at N=10,000,000; RISC Zero took 5.1s / 5.2s / 5.7s / 17.6s / 1m58.3s / 19m10.1s over the same sizes. So per-item cost falls ~100x over the first two decades of N and only then flattens. Round 1's "flat" claim is correct in the asymptote (batch 128-1024 of an already-expensive signature is likely already past saturation) but wrong as a description of the whole curve, and the sub-saturation knee is exactly where a 1-vs-10-vs-100 chart lives.
details:
ACCESS DATE for every URL below: 2026-07-23.

=====================================================================
1. IS A BATCH JUST A LOOP? WHAT LIMITS N?
=====================================================================
Yes. Nothing in either zkVM has a "batch" concept; you write `for sig in sigs { verify(sig) }` in the guest and the prover splits the resulting execution automatically. The limits are:

(a) GUEST ADDRESS SPACE (RISC Zero, hard).
Source: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/risc0/risc0/main/risc0/zkvm/platform/src/memory.rs (fetched; primary source).
Verbatim: `pub const GUEST_MAX_MEM: usize = 0xC000_0000;` and `pub const GUEST_MIN_MEM: usize = 0x0000_4000;`, `pub const STACK_TOP: u32 = 0x0020_0400;`, `pub const TEXT_START: u32 = 0x0020_0800;`.
0xC000_0000 = 3,221,225,472 bytes. This is a hard ceiling on the guest's whole memory image, so it caps how many signatures+pubkeys you can hold in guest RAM simultaneously — but note ML-DSA-44 sig (2420 B) + pk (1312 B) x 1000 is only ~3.7 MB, so this is NOT the binding constraint at N=1000. It becomes binding only in the millions.

(b) SEGMENT / SHARD SIZE.
RISC Zero (primary source, GitHub code search on risc0/risc0):
- `risc0/circuit/rv32im/src/execute/mod.rs`: `pub const DEFAULT_SEGMENT_LIMIT_PO2: usize = 20;` (= 1,048,576 cycles per segment by default).
- `risc0/zkp/src/lib.rs`: `pub const MIN_CYCLES_PO2: usize = 13;` / `pub const MIN_CYCLES: usize = 1 << MIN_CYCLES_PO2; // 8K` / `pub const MAX_CYCLES_PO2: usize = 24;` / `pub const MAX_CYCLES: usize = 1 << MAX_CYCLES_PO2; // 16M`.
- https://docs.rs/risc0-zkvm/3.0.6/risc0_zkvm/constant.DEFAULT_MAX_PO2.html : `pub const DEFAULT_MAX_PO2: usize = 22;` documented as "Maximum segment size, as a power of two (po2) that the default verifier parameters will accept." The page notes a default of 21 was chosen for ~97 bits of security and roughly 1 bit is lost per po2 increase (~94 bits at po2 24). NOTE the internal inconsistency between the constant value (22) and the prose ("a default of 21") — flagging it rather than picking one.
- Execution is split automatically once the segment limit is exceeded; segments are chained by Merkle-tree memory images (RISC Zero continuations).

SP1 (primary source, fetched in full): https://github.com/succinctlabs/sp1 file `crates/core/executor/src/opts.rs` (default branch as of 2026-07-23, blob SHA 6fcda3f60a943de863ac940be1f81e781d8ef014). Verbatim constants:
- `const MAX_SHARD_SIZE: usize = 1 << 24;`  (16,777,216)
- `pub const ELEMENT_THRESHOLD: u64 = (1 << 28) + (1 << 27);` documented as "The trace area threshold for a shard." (= 402,653,184)
- `pub const HEIGHT_THRESHOLD: u64 = 1 << 22;` documented as "The height threshold for a shard." (= 4,194,304)
- `pub const MINIMAL_TRACE_CHUNK_THRESHOLD: u64 = 16_777_216;` with the comment "16,777,216 entries x 16 B/entry = 256 MiB per chunk. With [`DEFAULT_TRACE_CHUNK_SLOTS`] slots in the SHM ring buffer, this caps the trace-ring shared memory at ~5 x 256 MiB x 10/9 ~= 1.43 GiB."
- `pub const DEFAULT_TRACE_CHUNK_SLOTS: usize = 5;`
- `pub const DEFAULT_MEMORY_LIMIT: u64 = 24 * 1024 * 1024 * 1024;` (24 GiB) documented as "Default memory limit for SP1 programs ... For native executor, it is the limit on total process memory(resident set size, or RSS) of this entire child process."
- Every one of these is overridable by env var: `MINIMAL_TRACE_CHUNK_THRESHOLD`, `TRACE_CHUNK_SLOTS`, `MEMORY_LIMIT`, `SHARD_SIZE`, `ELEMENT_THRESHOLD`, `HEIGHT_THRESHOLD`.
KEY STRUCTURAL POINT: modern SP1 shards on TRACE AREA and CHIP HEIGHT, not on a fixed cycle count. `shard_size` is documented as "The size of a shard in terms of cycles. Used for estimating event counts when allocating records." — i.e. it is an allocation hint, not the split criterion. Anyone who writes "SP1 shards every 2^21 cycles" is quoting a stale (pre-v6) design. This matters for our benchmark: a signature-heavy loop is precompile/trace-area heavy, so shard boundaries will not land on round cycle numbers.

(c) PAGING COST (RISC Zero, a real cycle tax that grows with working set).
https://dev.risczero.com/api/zkvm/optimization : "A page-in or page-out operation takes between 1094 and 5130 cycles; 1130 cycles on average." Memory is split into 1 kB pages; first access to a page traverses the full Merkle tree (~5130 cycles), later ~1130. Every dirty page is paged out at each segment boundary except the last. Consequence for us: a large signature batch held in one big buffer pays a per-segment page-out tax proportional to its dirty working set, so RISC Zero cost is superlinear-ish in guest working set even when the arithmetic is linear. This is a legitimately publishable second-order effect.

=====================================================================
2. AT WHAT CYCLE COUNT DOES 64 GB BECOME IMPRACTICAL?
=====================================================================
Short answer: with continuations/sharding on, essentially never from RAM — you run out of patience first. Peak prover RAM is set by segment/shard size, not by total cycles.

Primary source, https://docs.rs/risc0-zkvm/latest/risc0_zkvm/struct.ExecutorEnvBuilder.html (segment_limit_po2): "Set a segment limit, specified in powers of 2 cycles. Lowering this value will reduce the memory consumption of the prover." Memory usage scales roughly linearly with segment size; reducing the value by 1 approximately halves memory consumption. Valid range MIN_CYCLES_PO2..=MAX_CYCLES_PO2 (13..=24). Also `session_limit`: "Set a session limit, specified in number of cycles."

Published RAM floors (vendor docs, not independent measurements):
- RISC Zero, https://dev.risczero.com/api/zkvm/ : local proof generation is stated as "requiring at least 16GB RAM".
- SP1, https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/getting-started/hardware-requirements (no last-updated date shown on the page): Mock/Network — CPU "1+, single-core perf matters", Memory "8GB+, more is better", Disk 10GB+. Core/Compress — CPU "16+, more is better", Memory "16GB+, more if you have more cores". Groth16 and PLONK (EVM) — "Groth16: 16GB+, PLONK: 64GB+". Also: "The final wrapping step requires roughly 14GB of memory for Groth16 and 60GB for PLONK", "CPU is critical for performance/latency", CUDA path requires "CUDA Compute Capability >= 8.0", >=24 GB VRAM recommended, Linux x86_64 only.

Independent measured peak RAM (these are the only real measurements I could verify):
- Fenbushi Capital, "Benchmarking zkVMs: Current State and Prospects", https://fenbushi.vc/2025/08/29/benchmarking-zkvms-current-state-and-prospects/ , published 2025-08-29. Hardware: Ubuntu 24.04, 8 vCPUs, 192 GB RAM, NVIDIA RTX 5090 (32 GB VRAM). zkVMs: SP1, RISC Zero, OpenVM, Pico, ZKM, Jolt, Nexus, Novanet. Peak RAM on 100k-iteration Fibonacci, CPU only: Nexus/Novanet ~4 GB, OpenVM 8.2 GB, RISC Zero 9.2 GB, Pico 21 GB, Jolt 28 GB, ZKM 82 GB. Prover time (GPU, 100k Fib): SP1 (GPU) 3.4s, RISC Zero (GPU) 3.6s, OpenVM (CPU) 7.5s, Pico (CPU) 20s. Proof sizes: RISC Zero 222 kB, Jolt 232 kB, ZKM 415 kB, OpenVM 838 kB, SP1 1.8 MB. Scaling: "Most implementations exhibited proof generation time increases that were" approximately linear or slightly super-linear with input size.
  READ THIS CAREFULLY: 9.2 GB peak for a 100k-iteration Fibonacci is already >half of RISC Zero's own 16 GB floor at a trivially small cycle count. RAM is dominated by the fixed segment/shard machinery, not by the workload.
- Logos/Vac Research, "zkVM Testing Report", https://research.logos.co/rlog/zkVM-testing/ , published 2024-09-26. Hardware: "CPU: AMD EPYC 7713 'Milan' 64-core processor (128 threads total)", "600GiB DDR4 3200MHz ECC RAM", Supermicro AS-2024US-TRT. Stage 1 (Hept 100): SP1 16.95 s / 3.108 MB proof / 2.1 GB peak RAM; RISC0 9.73 s / 217.4 KB / 1.9 GB; Nexus 12.06 s / 46 MB / 9.7 MB; ZkMIPS 9.32 s / 4.3 MB / 17.3 GB; ZkWASM 42.7 s / 18 KB / 8.2 GB; Valida < 1 s / 280 KB / N/A. Stage 2 (Vec 10000): SP1 20.85 s / 3.17 MB / 1.9 GB; RISC0 16.63 s / 217.4 KB / 2.3 GB; Nexus 56 m / 46 MB / 1.9 GB; ZkMIPS 42.57 s / 4.898 MB / 18.9 GB; ZkWASM 323 s / 334 KB / 58.8 GB. This report is nearly 2 years old and predates SP1 v6 / RISC Zero v3 — do not use it for current absolute performance, only as evidence that peak RAM was low and roughly workload-independent.

NOT PUBLISHED: neither RISC Zero nor Succinct publishes a peak-RAM-vs-cycle-count curve, or an "at N cycles you OOM on X GB" table. I could not find one. Producing one would itself be a novel contribution for PQ-STARK-BENCH.

DISCONFIRMATION (report this): the search engine attributed a claim of "memory peaked at 50 GB during the final Shard Proof generation when proving a Taiko block with 488 million cycles on a system with 96 GB of RAM" to https://medium.com/@gwrx2005/comparative-analysis-of-sp1-and-risc-zero-zero-knowledge-virtual-machines-4abf806daa70 . I fetched that article (published 2025-08-03) and IT DOES NOT CONTAIN THAT CLAIM — no memory figures, no cycle counts, no shard sizes at all. Treat the 488M-cycles/50 GB number as UNSOURCED; do not cite it. (What the article does say: RISC Zero's "1.0 benchmarks included experiments up to 167 million RISC-V cycles in a single proof generation run"; SP1's network "had already handled 'trillions of cycles' worth of computation by 2024" — both are the article's own unfootnoted assertions.)

=====================================================================
3. RECURSION / CONTINUATIONS FOR LARGE N
=====================================================================
Both provers already do this automatically; it is not an opt-in optimization you have to build.

RISC Zero, https://dev.risczero.com/api/recursion (fetched):
- `lift` "verifies a STARK proof from the RISC-V Prover, using the Recursion Prover" (constant-time verification).
- `join` "verifies two STARK proofs from the Recursion Prover, using the Recursion Prover"; repeated application compresses many receipts into one.
- `identity_p254` "verifies a STARK proof from the Recursion Prover using the Recursion Prover with the Poseidon254 hash function" — the final step before Groth16.
- `resolve` "verifies two STARK proofs using the Recursion Prover" for proof composition (removing assumptions).
- Receipt types: Composite, Succinct (STARK, "~200kB"), Groth16 (on-chain via the RISC Zero Verifier Contract).
- CRUCIAL COST FACT: https://docs.rs/risc0-zkvm/3.0.6/risc0_zkvm/constant.RECURSION_PO2.html — `pub const RECURSION_PO2: usize = 18;` "Number of rows to use for the recursion circuit witness as a power of 2." The recursion circuit is a FIXED 2^18 = 262,144-row circuit. So every lift and every join costs the SAME regardless of what it is proving. With S segments you pay S lifts + (S-1) joins + 1 identity_p254 + 1 Groth16 wrap. Recursion therefore adds a term LINEAR in segment count (not log) to total prover work, with only the critical path being log-depth if you parallelize.

SP1, https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/generating-proofs/proof-types (fetched):
- Core (default) "generates a list of STARK proofs that in aggregate have size proportional to the size of the execution."
- Compressed "generates STARK proofs that have constant size"; required for recursive verification inside SP1.
- Groth16 (recommended) "generates a SNARK proof that is ~260 bytes large and can be verified onchain for around ~270k gas" (Aztec Ignition trusted setup).
- PLONK "generates a SNARK proof that is ~868 bytes large and can also be verified onchain for around ~300k gas"; takes "approximately 1 minute 30 seconds longer to generate than a compressed proof"; no trusted setup.
- https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/writing-programs/proof-aggregation : the "compressed" type is what you aggregate; to verify an SP1 proof inside SP1 you must generate a compressed proof.

EFFECT ON THE COST CURVE: recursion changes what amortizes and what does not.
- Prover TIME: still ~linear in N (every shard is proved), PLUS a linear-in-shards recursion term. Does not amortize.
- Peak RAM: bounded and roughly constant in N (that is the whole point of continuations). Amortizes trivially — it is flat.
- Proof BYTES and on-chain verify gas: become CONSTANT in N (compressed ~constant size; Groth16 ~260 B / ~270k gas). This is the only quantity that genuinely amortizes, exactly as round 1 established.
- Latency: can be made ~log(N) with enough parallel workers; on one laptop it is linear.

=====================================================================
4. WHAT THE AMORTIZATION CURVE ACTUALLY LOOKS LIKE
=====================================================================
This is the most important finding of this round and it REFINES (not refutes) the round-1 conclusion.

MEASURED, from https://zkbenchmarks.com/ (Aligned; page states hardware "GPU: NVIDIA RTX A6000 (48GB)", "RAM: 44GB", "Ubuntu 22 LTS", plus "AMD EPYC 8534P 64-Core Processor" for CPU runs; page update date reported as March 14-25, 2025; zkVM versions NOT published on the page — flag this). Keccak, varying N:
  RISC Zero:        N=100 -> 5.1s | 1,000 -> 5.2s | 10,000 -> 5.7s | 100,000 -> 17.6s | 1,000,000 -> 1m58.3s | 10,000,000 -> 19m10.1s
  SP1-AVX2:         N=100 -> 15.3s | 1,000 -> 15.3s | 10,000 -> 15.3s | 100,000 -> 15.9s | 1,000,000 -> 26.3s | 10,000,000 -> 2m11.5s | 100,000,000 -> 19m48.4s
  SP1-AVX2-Groth16: N=100 -> 53.9s | 1,000 -> 53.6s | 10,000 -> 54.3s | 100,000 -> 54.0s | 1,000,000 -> 1m4.5s | 10,000,000 -> 2m52.1s | 100,000,000 -> 20m29.3s
Fibonacci, same source:
  RISC Zero:        10,000 -> 1.7s | 100,000 -> 5.6s | 1,000,000 -> 48.9s | 10,000,000 -> 7m45.5s
  SP1-AVX2:         10,000 -> 13.9s | 100,000 -> 13.3s | 1,000,000 -> 22.8s | 10,000,000 -> 1m46.8s
  SP1-AVX2-Groth16: 10,000 -> 53.1s | 100,000 -> 53.7s | 1,000,000 -> 1m9.2s | 10,000,000 -> 2m27.1s

Read the shape: total time is FLAT across 3-4 decades of N, then goes linear. Therefore amortized cost PER ITEM falls like 1/N through the flat region (SP1-AVX2 Keccak: 153 ms/item at N=100 -> 15.3 ms at N=1,000 -> 1.53 ms at N=10,000 -> 0.159 ms at N=100,000 -> 0.026 ms at N=1,000,000) and only THEN flattens out at the marginal cost. Note SP1-AVX2 at N=100,000 (15.9s) is barely above N=100 (15.3s) yet is 1000x the work — the entire first 1000x is free. Note also the Groth16 line sits a near-constant ~38s above the plain SP1-AVX2 line at every size: the SNARK wrap is a pure fixed cost.

CAUSES OF THE FIXED OVERHEAD (each grounded above):
  (i) Minimum trace size: RISC Zero cannot prove a trace smaller than MIN_CYCLES = 1 << 13 = 8,192 rows. A one-signature batch pays a full 8,192-row segment proof.
  (ii) Power-of-two trace padding: STARK traces are padded to a power of two, so cost is a STAIRCASE in N, not a line. Expect visible steps in our charts, and expect two adjacent batch sizes to cost identically.
  (iii) Fixed-size recursion circuit: RECURSION_PO2 = 18 (262,144 rows) per lift/join, independent of payload.
  (iv) Fixed SNARK wrap: SP1's "final wrapping step requires roughly 14GB of memory for Groth16 and 60GB for PLONK"; PLONK adds "approximately 1 minute 30 seconds" over compressed; RISC Zero's identity_p254 + Groth16 stage.
  (v) One-time setup: proving-key/circuit-artifact load and disk checkpointing (SP1 docs list disk as "used to checkpoint the state of the program execution").
  (vi) Guest program load and RISC Zero page-in of the ELF at ~1094-5130 cycles/page.

IMPLICATION FOR PQ-STARK-BENCH POSITIONING: do NOT publish a chart implying "cost per tx is flat" without also publishing the sub-saturation knee, and do NOT publish the knee while implying it is a discovery. The honest framing is: proof bytes and on-chain gas amortize to a constant (known since at least ePrint 2021/1048); prover time and RAM do not amortize in the asymptote (also known); but the LOCATION OF THE KNEE — how many ML-DSA-44 / Falcon-512 / SLH-DSA verifications fit inside one shard before marginal cost kicks in — is scheme-specific, prover-specific, and as far as I can find, unpublished for any FIPS PQ scheme. THAT is a defensible headline.

=====================================================================
5. PUBLISHED LARGE-BATCH SIGNATURE VERIFICATION WITH REAL TIMINGS
=====================================================================
The best real precedent is Ethereum L1 block proving: every tx in a block carries an ECDSA signature, so a proven block IS a proven batch of a few hundred signature verifications (plus a lot of EVM).

- SP1 Hypercube, https://blog.succinct.xyz/real-time-proving-16-gpus/ , published 2025-11-18. Verbatim: "the latest version of SP1 Hypercube delivers real time proving of 99.7% of L1 Ethereum blocks under 12s"; also 95.4% of blocks under 10s; hardware "16 NVIDIA RTX 5090 GPUs"; test set 954 random blocks, block numbers 23,807,739 to 23,812,008.
- Pico Prism 2.0, https://blog.brevis.network/2026/05/12/pico-prism-2-0-a-5-3x-efficiency-leap-in-real-time-ethereum-proving/ , published 2026-05-12. Verbatim: "16 RTX 5090 GPUs across 2 machines"; average proving time "6.1 seconds"; "99.9%" of blocks under 12 seconds; gas limit "60M (current Ethereum mainnet)"; hardware cost "~$100K". Their own efficiency arithmetic, verbatim: "Pico Prism 1.0: 8.1s x 64 GPUs / 60M gas = 8.64 GPU-seconds per million gas" vs "Pico Prism 2.0: 6.1s x 16 GPUs / 60M gas = 1.63 GPU-seconds per million gas".
- SP1 Blobstream (Celestia), https://blog.succinct.xyz/celestia-sp1/ , published 2024-09-27. This verifies Celestia's Tendermint consensus INCLUDING its validator set's ed25519 signatures inside SP1 — i.e. a real production PQ-adjacent batch-signature workload. Published numbers: SP1 program is "less than 300 lines of code"; on-chain verification "280k gas"; in production on Ethereum mainnet, Arbitrum and Base. PROVING TIME AND CYCLE COUNT ARE NOT PUBLISHED. Say so loudly if you cite it — it is a qualitative precedent only.
- SP1 Benchmarks 8/6/24, https://blog.succinct.xyz/sp1-benchmarks-8-6-24/ , published 2024-08-06. Programs: Tendermint light client, Reth Block 17106222, Reth Block 19409768. Hardware: AWS g6.xlarge / g6.2xlarge / g6.16xlarge, Lambda Labs NVIDIA A6000 and A100. Versions: SP1 v1.1.1 vs RISC0 v1.0.0. Claims "the cheapest cloud costs vs. alternative zkVMs by up to 10x" and precompiles "decreasing RISC-V cycle counts between 5-10x for many programs". The actual numeric tables live in a linked Google Sheet and a repo (github.com/succinctlabs/zkvm-perf) which I did NOT fetch — do not quote its numbers until someone opens the sheet.

DISCONFIRMATIONS / DEAD ENDS (all valuable, please record them):
- https://github.com/NebraZKP/proof-aggregation-benchmarks — this is EXACTLY our experiment shape ("This proof is verified N times to simulate aggregating a batch of size N", covering "Risc Zero and SP1"), but the repository PUBLISHES NO RESULTS. I fetched the full README (only methodology, 1196 bytes) and listed the repo root: `.gitignore`, `README.md`, `groth16/`, `nebra-upa/`, `test-risc0/`, `test-sp1/`. There is no results file, no table, no hardware spec, no versions. Anyone citing "the Nebra zkVM batch benchmark" is citing code, not data.
- https://risczero.com/blog/continuations — returned HTTP 500 Internal Server Error on fetch (2026-07-23). The continuations blog post is the canonical citation for RISC Zero segmenting and it is currently unfetchable; cite dev.risczero.com and the source constants instead.
- reports.risczero.com — DNS failure (ENOTFOUND). The RISC Zero benchmark reports site appears to be gone.
- https://dev.risczero.com/api/zkvm/benchmarks — exists but contains NO NUMBERS. It only tells you to run `cargo run --release --example datasheet` (with `-F metal` or `-F cuda`) and `cargo bench --bench fib`. RISC Zero does not currently publish a benchmark datasheet; you have to generate it. GOOD NEWS FOR US: `risc0/zkvm/examples/datasheet.rs` exists in the repo and takes a `po2` argument validated against MIN_CYCLES_PO2..MAX_CYCLES_PO2 — that is a ready-made harness for a segment-size sweep on our Mac.
- https://eprint.iacr.org/2026/525.pdf ("SoK: Understanding zkVM: From Research to Practice", Yang/Cheng/Tang/Yang/Zhang/Ren; abstract page dated received 2026-03-16, last revised 2026-03-24) — the PDF returned HTTP 403 and the abstract page shows no tables. A search snippet attributed to it the claim that RISC0 "pads all executions to a fixed trace of 32,768 cycles" and proof sizes SP1 ~1.8 MB / Ziren ~2.5 MB / RISC0 ~209 KB / Jolt 66-75 KB. I COULD NOT VERIFY ANY OF THAT — treat as unverified. (The 32,768 = 2^15 figure is also in tension with the source constant MIN_CYCLES = 2^13 = 8,192, so someone needs to open that PDF properly.)
- https://sigmaprime.io/blog/sp1-zkvm-security-guide/ (published 2025-09-24) — despite the title, it does NOT cover sharding, shard size, recursion, proof types, cycles, or memory. Not useful for us.
- https://blog.alignedlayer.com/zkbenchmarks/ (published 2025-03-24) covers SP1, Risc0, Pico on Fibonacci / Keccak / RSP; states SP1 "currently outperforms RISC0 for proving Ethereum blocks" and Pico is best for CPU Keccak, but the prose carries no cycle counts or memory specs — the numbers live on zkbenchmarks.com (quoted above).
- Sei, https://blog.sei.io/research/optimizing-ecdsa-signature-verification-exploring-zero-knowledge-proofs-in-the-sei-giga-blockchain/ , published 2025-02-05: for ECDSA verification the zkVM (SP1) took "a few seconds", their Spartan/efficient-ECDSA custom circuit "200 ms for the verification of the proof" vs "around 1 s in our benchmarks" baseline, cutting "over 10,000 constraints". No batch sizes, no amortization, no hardware. And they ultimately abandoned the ZK approach. Weak source; mention only as colour.

=====================================================================
6. PRACTICAL ADVICE FOR OUR HARDWARE, AND WHERE TO EXPECT THE WALL
=====================================================================
Given round-1's established constraints (macOS aarch64; SP1 v6.3.1 = scalar CPU only, no AVX and no CUDA; RISC Zero v3.0.6 = Metal GPU available but Groth16 unsupported on Apple Silicon; neither has an ML-DSA/NTT/lattice precompile; SP1 has a sha3/SHAKE precompile, RISC Zero does not):

a) CALIBRATE AGAINST THE RIGHT BASELINE. Every published number above is GPU (A6000, RTX 5090) or 64-core EPYC. Our local SP1 runs are scalar CPU with no AVX and no CUDA — expect our absolute times to be one to two orders of magnitude worse than zkbenchmarks.com. Publish our hardware in every chart caption and never compare our absolutes to Succinct's or Brevis's.

b) DO THE CHEAP AXIS FIRST. Both zkVMs let you EXECUTE without proving and report cycle counts. Run the executor for N = 1, 2, 4, 8, ..., 4096 for all schemes and both zkVMs and publish the full cycles-vs-N curve — that is nearly free, is per-scheme meaningful, is the thing nobody has published for ML-DSA-44, and it lets you predict where the proving wall is before you hit it. Publish cycles-per-signature as the headline metric alongside seconds, because cycles are hardware-independent and survive prover version bumps.

c) EXPECTED ACHIEVABLE BATCHES (inference, clearly labelled as such — the per-verification cycle costs for ML-DSA-44/Falcon-512/SLH-DSA in these zkVMs are NOT PUBLISHED, which is exactly the gap round 1 identified):
   - N = 1, 10, 100: expect these to complete locally for all three FIPS schemes, and expect N=1 and N=10 to cost nearly the SAME wall-clock (the flat region above). Budget minutes to low tens of minutes each on scalar CPU.
   - N = 1000: this is the coin-flip. If a single ML-DSA-44 verification is ~1-5M cycles unaccelerated, N=1000 is ~1-5 billion cycles = roughly 1,000-5,000 RISC Zero default segments. At a scalar-CPU proving rate of order 10^5 cycles/s that is hours to days per data point. Plan for it to be a multi-hour overnight run at best, and plan to report a NEGATIVE RESULT with a measured cycle count plus an extrapolation rather than a completed proof.
   - SLH-DSA (SPHINCS+) verification is far heavier than ML-DSA; expect its wall one or two decades of N earlier.

d) THE WALL WILL BE TIME, NOT MEMORY, ON 64 GB. Because continuations/sharding bound peak RAM by segment size, you can always trade RAM for time: lower `segment_limit_po2` (RISC Zero) or `SHARD_SIZE`/`ELEMENT_THRESHOLD`/`HEIGHT_THRESHOLD` (SP1). Only two things can genuinely OOM you: (i) the final SNARK wrap — SP1 says Groth16 needs ~14 GB and PLONK ~60 GB for the wrap, so on 64 GB avoid PLONK entirely; (ii) SP1's 24 GiB `DEFAULT_MEMORY_LIMIT` on the executor process. Both are avoidable.

e) PIN THE SEGMENT/SHARD SIZE EXPLICITLY AND REPORT IT. This is a methodology landmine: SP1's `SP1CoreOpts::default()` reads `SHARD_SIZE`, `ELEMENT_THRESHOLD`, `HEIGHT_THRESHOLD`, `MEMORY_LIMIT`, `TRACE_CHUNK_SLOTS` from the ENVIRONMENT, and several sources report SP1 auto-tuning shard parameters from free machine memory. If you do not pin them, two machines will silently use different shard sizes and your cross-machine numbers will be incomparable — and a reviewer will find it. Do the same for RISC Zero (`segment_limit_po2`) and put the value in the results JSON schema as a first-class field, alongside prover version, hardware, and whether GPU/Metal/AVX was active.

f) REPORT BOTH RECEIPT TIERS SEPARATELY. Locally you can produce RISC Zero composite + succinct and SP1 core + compressed. You CANNOT produce RISC Zero Groth16 on Apple Silicon (round-1 finding). So publish "core/composite proving time" and "compressed/succinct proving time" as separate series, and mark the SNARK-wrap column as "not measurable on our hardware" rather than omitting it — the wrap is a large fixed cost (~38s constant in the zkbenchmarks SP1 data) and hiding it would flatter the small-N numbers.

g) BE HONEST ABOUT THE PRECOMPILE ASYMMETRY IN THE BATCH CONTEXT TOO. SP1's sha3/SHAKE precompile helps all three FIPS schemes on the hashing path; RISC Zero has no SHAKE precompile. That asymmetry does not vanish with batching — it scales with N — so the SP1-vs-RISC-Zero gap on PQ schemes will look larger at large N. Disclose it in the same caption as the Ed25519/ECDSA asymmetry.

h) THE SPECIFIC NEGATIVE RESULT WORTH PUBLISHING. "On <exact hardware>, ML-DSA-44 batch verification in SP1 v6.3.1 (scalar CPU) hit a wall at N = X, where a single proof required T hours; measured cycle count was C, extrapolating to Y hours at N=1000." A measured wall with a cycle count and a stated extrapolation is a genuine contribution given that literally nobody has published ML-DSA-44 cycle counts in a general-purpose zkVM. A missing data point that you never attempted is not.

blockers: (1) The single biggest gap: per-verification CYCLE COUNTS for ML-DSA-44, Falcon-512 and SLH-DSA in RISC Zero or SP1 are NOT PUBLISHED anywhere I could find, so every wall-clock estimate for N=100/1000 in section 6 is my own inference, not a measurement. Fallback: run the executor (no proving) first — it is cheap and produces the exact cycle counts, which then make the time predictions arithmetic instead of guesswork. Do this before committing to any batch-size ladder. (2) The amortization-curve evidence (zkbenchmarks.com) is Keccak/Fibonacci on an RTX A6000, not signatures on our Mac, and the page does not publish zkVM VERSIONS — so the shape of the curve transfers but the absolute knee location does not. Fallback: reproduce the shape ourselves with a cheap workload (Keccak-N) on our hardware before running the expensive PQ sweep, to locate our own knee. (3) Three sources that should have settled things did not: risczero.com/blog/continuations returns HTTP 500, reports.risczero.com no longer resolves, and ePrint 2026/525's PDF is 403 — so the RISC Zero trace-padding minimum (2^13 from source vs an unverified 2^15 claim) is unresolved. Fallback: measure it directly with `cargo run --release --example datasheet` from the risc0 repo, which accepts a po2 argument and is designed for exactly this sweep. (4) SP1's shard parameters are read from environment variables and are widely reported to auto-tune from free machine memory; if we do not pin SHARD_SIZE / ELEMENT_THRESHOLD / HEIGHT_THRESHOLD / MEMORY_LIMIT (and RISC Zero's segment_limit_po2) and record them in the results JSON, cross-machine and cross-run numbers will be silently incomparable and a reviewer will invalidate the benchmark. This is the highest-probability methodology failure in the whole project. (5) On our hardware SP1 has no AVX and no CUDA and RISC Zero cannot produce Groth16 at all, so the SNARK-wrap fixed cost (a ~38s constant in the SP1 reference data) cannot be measured locally; report it as an explicitly missing column rather than omitting it, and plan a cloud/Linux-x86_64 run for the wrap numbers.

topic: Grounding the dollar-cost model for PQ-STARK-BENCH: AWS instance pricing, proving-market rates, Ethproofs cost methodology, and a defensible framing for "$X per 1000 tx"
bottom_line: Use a two-number model: report the primitive (prover wall-clock seconds and peak RAM on named hardware) as the measured result, then publish dollars only as a derived, reproducible transform: cost = (measured seconds / 3600) x (published $/hr for a NAMED reference instance, with URL + date), clearly labelled "modelled, not billed". AWS us-east-1 on-demand Linux as of the 2026-07-23 price feed: c7a.16xlarge $3.2844800000/hr, r7a.16xlarge $4.8688000000/hr, g6.xlarge $0.8048000000/hr, g5.xlarge $1.0060000000/hr, p4d.24xlarge $21.9576420000/hr, p5.48xlarge $55.0400000000/hr. No proving market (Succinct, Boundless, Aligned, Nexus) publishes a fixed $/proof or $/Mcycle rate - all are auction-cleared; the only hard doc-published numbers are Boundless broker CONFIG examples (min_mcycle_price = "0.02 USD"), which are defaults, not clearing prices. Ethproofs is the one live public $/proof source and it publishes 1-2 cent per-proof figures under an explicitly-documented formula.
details:
ACCESS DATE FOR EVERYTHING BELOW: 2026-07-23.

=====================================================================
1. AWS ON-DEMAND HOURLY PRICING (MEASURED-AND-PUBLISHED, official AWS feed)
=====================================================================
The human-readable page https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/pricing/on-demand/ ("Amazon EC2 On-Demand Pricing") renders its table client-side and returns NO instance rates to a fetcher - I tried and got only prose ("Pricing is per instance-hour consumed for each instance, from the time an instance is launched until it is terminated or stopped."). SAY THIS LOUDLY if anyone claims to have "read the price off the AWS pricing page": you cannot, via plain fetch.

The rates below come from the gzip-JSON feed that page itself consumes:
  https://b0.p.awsstatic.com/pricing/2.0/meteredUnitMaps/ec2/USD/current/ec2-ondemand-without-sec-sel/US%20East%20(N.%20Virginia)/Linux/index.json
Its embedded manifest states verbatim:
  {"serviceId": "ec2", "accessType": "publish", "hawkFilePublicationDate": "2026-07-23T05:59:00Z", "ETLIngestionTriggerDate": "2026-07-23T05:59:00Z", "currencyCode": "USD", "source": "ec2-ondemand-without-sec-sel"}
So the publication date is 2026-07-23T05:59:00Z - same day as access. Prices are Linux, On-Demand, no pre-installed software, "No License required".

This is a genuinely useful robustness fact: for our representative instances, region choice does NOT move the headline number between the two most common US regions. Say that explicitly in the paper so nobody accuses us of region-shopping.

RECOMMENDED REFERENCE INSTANCES FOR PQ-STARK-BENCH:
 - CPU reference: c7a.16xlarge @ $3.28448/hr (64 vCPU / 128 GiB). Closest commodity analogue to a many-core prover box.
 - Memory reference: r7a.16xlarge @ $4.8688/hr (64 vCPU / 512 GiB). REQUIRED because our own Round-1 finding is that STARK prover RAM grows LINEARLY with batch N - at large N the binding constraint is GiB, not vCPU, and a c7a will OOM before it runs slow. Report which instance class each batch size actually fits in.
 - GPU reference: g6.xlarge @ $0.8048/hr as the cheap floor; p5.48xlarge @ $55.04/hr as the "real-time proving" ceiling. NOTE the disclosure trap: RISC Zero uses Metal on Apple Silicon and CUDA on Linux; SP1 CUDA is Linux x86_64 only. A GPU $/hr is only legitimate if we actually measured on a GPU.

=====================================================================
2. PROVING MARKETS - IS THERE A PUBLISHED $/PROOF OR $/Mcycle?
=====================================================================
SHORT ANSWER: NO. Every one of the four is a bid/auction market. None publishes a rate card. Anyone who cites "the Succinct rate" or "the Boundless rate" as a fixed number is fabricating it.

SUCCINCT PROVER NETWORK - NO PUBLISHED USD RATE.
 - https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/optimizing-programs/prover-gas ("Prover Gas | Succinct Docs"): defines the unit only. Verbatim: "Prover gas is a more accurate metric for estimating the real proving 'costs' of an SP1 program introduced in versions >= 4.1.4." and "Prover gas has been roughly calibrated to be of a similar magnitude to RISC-V cycle count". NO USD conversion, NO $PROVE conversion, NO fee formula on this page.
 - https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/provers/how-it-works/overview ("Overview | Succinct Docs"): describes a reverse auction - user sets "maximum fee", provers bid, "The lowest bid wins the right to fulfill the request". NO base fee value, NO min/max, NO USD figure.
 - https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/provers/introduction ("Introduction | Succinct Docs"): verbatim "If your bid wins, you're responsible for delivering a valid proof on time. If you succeed, you earn the bid amount in $PROVE tokens." Plus an explicit disclaimer, verbatim: "Participation in the network as a prover does not guarantee a return on investment or compensation. There are currently no additional incentives to participate in the network."
 - DISCONFIRMATION TO RECORD: https://docs.succinct.xyz/docs/network/developers/pricing returns HTTP 404. There is no "pricing" page. The fee STRUCTURE (Base Fee + bid price per PGU x PGUs) is widely repeated in secondary/press coverage but I did not find it stated with any numeric rate in first-party docs; treat the structure as doc-supported and any number attached to it as vendor/secondary claim.

BOUNDLESS (RISC Zero) - CONFIG DEFAULTS PUBLISHED; MARKET RATE NOT PUBLISHED.
 - IMPORTANT INFRA NOTE / DISCONFIRMATION: https://docs.beboundless.xyz/... FAILS TLS ("certificate has expired") as of 2026-07-23. beboundless.xyz 308-redirects to boundless.network. The live docs host is docs.boundless.network. Any citation to docs.beboundless.xyz in our bibliography must be repointed.
 - https://docs.boundless.network/provers/broker (raw markdown verified at https://docs.boundless.network/provers/broker.md). Verbatim from the config table: "min_mcycle_price | The minimum price per mega-cycle (i.e. million RISC-V cycles) for the broker to attempt to lock an order. Accepts \"<value> ETH\" (e.g., \"0.00002 ETH\") or \"<value> USD\" (e.g., \"0.02 USD\"); USD values are converted to ETH at runtime via the price oracle."
   Worked example block, verbatim:
     [market]
     min_mcycle_price = "0.02 USD"
     max_collateral = "100 USD"
   Per-requestor override example, verbatim: min_mcycle_price = "0.01 USD" and min_mcycle_price = "0.03 USD".
   Tuning guidance, verbatim: "Decreasing the min_mcycle_price would tune your Broker to bid at lower prices for proofs. This value can be specified in ETH (e.g., \"0.00001 ETH\") or USD (e.g., \"0.02 USD\")."
   Also verbatim: "expected_probability_win_secondary_fulfillment ... Default: 50."
   THESE ARE DOCUMENTATION EXAMPLES / OPERATOR FLOOR SETTINGS, NOT OBSERVED CLEARING PRICES. Cite them as "the reference broker config uses $0.02 USD per megacycle as an example price floor", never as "Boundless costs $0.02/Mcycle".
 - https://docs.boundless.network/developers/tutorials/request (raw at .../request.md), requestor side, verbatim:
     .min_price(Amount::parse("0.40 USD", None)?)   // or: parse_ether("0.001")?
     .max_price(Amount::parse("1.00 USD", None)?)   // or: parse_ether("0.002")?
     .max_price_per_cycle(Amount::parse("0.00001 USD", None).unwrap())  // or: parse_units("0.1", "gwei").unwrap()
     .min_price_per_cycle(Amount::parse("0.000001 USD", None).unwrap()) // or: parse_units("0.01", "gwei").unwrap()
   FLAG AN INTERNAL INCONSISTENCY (my inference, state it as such): if max_price_per_cycle really is per single cycle, $0.00001/cycle = $10 per megacycle, which is 500x the broker-side $0.02/Mcycle floor. Either the tutorial's "per cycle" is loose naming or the two examples are not meant to be comparable. DO NOT build a cost model on max_price_per_cycle. Use min_mcycle_price semantics, which are unambiguous ("per mega-cycle (i.e. million RISC-V cycles)").
   Also verbatim, on defaults: "By default, the parameters maximize the chance of request fulfillment. They use relaxed timeouts, dynamic market pricing, and recommended collateral. Change them only when necessary."
 - Live clearing price: per the docs/community guidance, the way to observe it is per-order in the Boundless explorer under "ETH per Megacycle". There is no published aggregate average. Secondary sources claim cycle prices have fallen to "near zero Wei" - VENDOR/SECONDARY CLAIM, unverified, do not cite as a rate.

ALIGNED - CATEGORY ERROR RISK. Aligned prices proof VERIFICATION/aggregation, not proof GENERATION. https://docs.alignedlayer.com/ and https://whitepaper.alignedlayer.com/ publish relative reductions ("nearly 90% for Groth16", "nearly 99% for STARKs" per secondary summarisation of the docs) but I found NO absolute $/proof rate card. If we cite Aligned at all it belongs in a "settlement/verification cost" column, never in the prover-cost column. Mixing them would be the single most embarrassing error available in this table.

NEXUS - NOT PUBLISHED. No first-party $/proof found. Nexus markets a permissionless prover network runnable on consumer hardware ("as little as 1 GB of RAM" per secondary coverage). No rate card located. Record as "not published".

=====================================================================
3. ETHPROOFS - THE ONE LIVE PUBLIC $/PROOF SOURCE
=====================================================================
https://ethproofs.org/ (accessed 2026-07-23) currently displays per-proof costs in its real-time-proving cohort table, verbatim:
  Axiom (16x5090): "$0.0101"
  AntChain OpenLabs (zkDTVM 8x5090): "$0.0052"
Also on-page, verbatim performance context: "63.3% sub-10s", target "≥70%", eligibility window "≤10 secs", and the reading key "Key insight: Green shows sub-10s successes (the goal). Yellow (stunners) and red (paralyzers) should be minimized. A healthy cohort is ≥70% green."
https://ethproofs.org/metrics chart caption, verbatim: "Orange line (latency) should trend down toward 10s threshold. Green line (cost) should trend down toward $0.01. Blue shaded area shows gas limit increases — efficiency improving despite harder blocks is the success story." The page has a card titled "GPU price index" / "weekly average GPU hourly price" - but the server-rendered HTML shows "$0.00/hr" for current and all-time-low; the real value hydrates client-side and is NOT retrievable by fetch. So the current numeric index value is NOT machine-readable; report it as "displayed on-site, not fetchable".
DISCONFIRMATION: https://ethproofs.org/learn contains NO cost methodology at all (only "$300k in grants"). Do not cite /learn for methodology. Also, ethproofs.org/api/v1/* returns the Next.js HTML shell, not JSON - there is no trivially-scrapable public cost API at those paths.

METHODOLOGY (first-party, HackMD, by willcorcoran):
 - "Post 2: The Ethproofs 5090 Price Index", https://hackmd.io/@willcorcoran/Sk8lhzXfbe, dated December 2025. Formula verbatim:
     Cost_per_proof = ( num_gpus x 5090_index ) x ( proving_time_ms / 3,600,000 )
   Index construction verbatim: "Query Vast.ai" filtered to RTX 5090 with 99.9% host reliability across all regions, taking the median price; published as a QUARTERLY global median price for one 5090 GPU-hour. Stated typical range verbatim: "$0.37–$0.47/hr". Four "Prover Personas" are defined (the post details Persona 1 - Single-GPU 1:100, and Persona 2 - Multi-GPU 1:1).
 - "Ethproofs 2025 Review & 2026 Roadmap for Real-Time Proving", https://hackmd.io/@willcorcoran/S1A840ZMZg, dated December 6, 2025. Verbatim: costs "fell from $1.69 to under a penny" per proof. On why they moved off cloud pricing, verbatim: "Our current cost-per-proof system relies on stale cloud pricing, unverifiable assumptions about operator choices, and backend complexity." And: "all cost will be normalized through a single, fixed hourly rate for an RTX 5090." And: "Dollar-denominated cost is nice to see, but far from a northstar metric."
   Replacement primary metric, verbatim: Energy per proof (kWh) = num_gpus x TDP x proving_time_ms / 3,600,000
 - THIS IS THE MOST IMPORTANT METHODOLOGICAL FINDING FOR US. The most credible public zk-cost benchmark in existence looked at exactly our problem and concluded that dollar-denominated cost is fragile, and moved to (a) a single fixed normalising hardware rate and (b) an energy metric. We should copy both moves.

=====================================================================
4. OTHER 2025-2026 $/PROOF AND HARDWARE-RATE FIGURES
=====================================================================
 - Ethproofs live cohort, 2026-07-23: $0.0101 and $0.0052 per Ethereum block proof (above).
 - Ethproofs trajectory: "$1.69" -> "under a penny" per proof over 2025; a reported average of "3.54 cents per proof" as of December 2 (secondary summarisation of Ethproofs commentary - treat as SECONDARY unless we re-verify on the Ethproofs charts).
 - Brevis "Pico Prism 2.0: A 5.3x Efficiency Leap in Real-Time Ethereum Proving", https://blog.brevis.network/2026/05/12/pico-prism-2-0-a-5-3x-efficiency-leap-in-real-time-ethereum-proving/, dated 2026-05-12. Verbatim: "Total hardware cost ~$100K"; "16 RTX 5090 GPUs across 2 machines"; "Average proving time 6.1s"; "Blocks proven within 12s 99.9%"; block gas limit "60M (current Ethereum mainnet)"; "Pico Prism 2.0: 6.1s × 16 GPUs ÷ 60M gas = 1.63 GPU-seconds per million gas" and "Pico Prism 1.0: 8.1s × 64 GPUs ÷ 60M gas = 8.64 GPU-seconds per million gas". NOTE: this post publishes GPU-seconds and capex, NOT a $/proof - it deliberately avoids a dollar figure. That is itself a signal about what the serious teams consider defensible. The GPU-second-per-unit-work metric is directly borrowable for us as "prover-core-seconds per signature".
 - RunPod public rate card, https://www.runpod.io/pricing, accessed 2026-07-23 (no visible publication date; it is a live page): RTX 5090 "$0.69/hr" Community Cloud / "$0.99/hr" Secure Cloud; RTX 4090 "$0.34/hr" / "$0.69/hr"; H100 SXM "$2.69/hr" / "$2.99/hr"; H100 PCIe "$1.99/hr" / "$2.89/hr"; H100 NVL "$2.59/hr" / "$3.19/hr".
   USEFUL TENSION TO PUBLISH: Ethproofs' Vast.ai-median 5090 index is "$0.37–$0.47/hr" while RunPod's 5090 is $0.69-$0.99/hr - a 1.5x to 2.7x spread for the SAME GPU on the same day. That spread IS the honest error bar on any dollar claim in this field, and we should state it as such rather than pretending to 3 significant figures.

=====================================================================
5. RECOMMENDED FRAMING - HOW TO STATE "$X PER 1000 TX" DEFENSIBLY
=====================================================================
Rule 0: DOLLARS ARE NEVER THE MEASUREMENT. The measurement is (prover wall-clock seconds, peak RSS bytes, proof bytes, cycle count) on a named machine. Dollars are a published TRANSFORM applied to that measurement. Keep them in separate columns and separate files (raw results JSON must not contain a dollar field; a separate cost.json holds the rate table).

Rule 1: STATE THE TRANSFORM AS AN EQUATION, IN THE CHART CAPTION, EVERY TIME.
  modelled_cost_per_1000_tx = (measured_prover_seconds_for_batch_N / 3600) x reference_rate_usd_per_hour x (1000 / N)
Publish reference_rate_usd_per_hour with instance type, region, OS, "On-Demand", source URL, and the feed's hawkFilePublicationDate. Example caption text we can ship:
  "Modelled at $3.28448/hr (c7a.16xlarge, Linux On-Demand, us-east-1; AWS price feed published 2026-07-23T05:59:00Z). Measured on <laptop spec>, NOT on this instance. See §Cost Model."

Rule 2: NEVER SAY "COSTS $X". SAY "MODELLED AT $X UNDER ASSUMPTION SET A." Bin the words: "measured" is reserved for seconds/bytes/cycles; "modelled" for dollars; "vendor-claimed" for anything from a company blog; "market-observed" only if we actually pull live auction fills. Put those four words in a legend.

Rule 3: DISCLOSE THE HARDWARE MISMATCH IN THE SAME BREATH AS THE NUMBER, WITH A DIRECTION AND A BOUND. The honest sentence is:
  "All timings were measured on an Apple Silicon laptop (single-socket, scalar CPU for SP1 - no CUDA on macOS, no AVX on aarch64; RISC Zero uses Metal). We do not have measurements on the priced instance. The dollar figures are therefore a UNIT-CONVERSION of laptop seconds at cloud rates, not a cloud benchmark. Laptop-to-c7a.16xlarge throughput is unmeasured; we expect the 64-vCPU instance to be FASTER in wall-clock for parallel provers, which means our dollar figures are an UPPER BOUND on time but potentially a LOWER BOUND on cost (a $3.28/hr instance idles 63 cores if the prover is single-threaded)."
  That last clause is the one most people get wrong. If you price laptop-seconds at a 64-vCPU hourly rate you are billing for cores you never used. Two defensible fixes, pick one and say which:
   (a) NORMALISE PER-CORE: report $/vCPU-hour and multiply by measured core-seconds. c7a.16xlarge = $3.28448 / 64 = $0.0513200 per vCPU-hour (my arithmetic, flag as derived). This is defensible for a scalar prover.
   (b) MEASURE ON THE PRICED BOX. Strictly better. Budget: even 100 hours of c7a.16xlarge is $328.45. This is cheap enough that "we couldn't afford it" is not a credible excuse, and a reviewer WILL say so. Recommend we plan for (b) and use (a) only for the preliminary release, labelled PRELIMINARY.

Rule 4: PUBLISH A RANGE, NOT A POINT. Give the same measurement at three rates and show the spread: cheapest credible (RunPod/Vast-class commodity, e.g. the Ethproofs 5090 index band "$0.37–$0.47/hr"), mid (AWS c7a.16xlarge $3.28448/hr), and premium (AWS p5.48xlarge $55.04/hr if GPU-proved). The 1.5-2.7x same-day spread between Vast-median and RunPod for an identical RTX 5090 is our evidence that a point estimate is not meaningful; cite it.

Rule 5: PUBLISH THE HARDWARE-NEUTRAL METRICS AS THE HEADLINE AND DOLLARS AS THE FOOTNOTE. Follow Ethproofs and Brevis:
  - core-seconds (or GPU-seconds) per signature verified
  - peak RAM per signature at batch N
  - cycles / prover-gas per signature (this is the ONE number that survives hardware, price, and prover-version churn, and it is what both Succinct's PGU and Boundless's mcycle pricing are denominated in)
  - optionally kWh per proof via the Ethproofs formula: num_gpus x TDP x proving_time_ms / 3,600,000
  A cycles-per-signature figure lets any reader apply THEIR OWN rate, including the Boundless min_mcycle_price semantics, without trusting our dollar assumptions. This is the single highest-leverage design decision in the cost model.

Rule 6: DO NOT QUOTE A PROVING-MARKET PRICE AS OUR COST. We verified there is no published rate card at Succinct, Boundless, Aligned, or Nexus. If we want a market-anchored number we must either (a) actually submit requests and report fills with tx hashes, or (b) state "no public rate card; markets are auction-cleared" - which is itself a publishable finding and protects us from the reviewer who checks.

Rule 7: VERSION AND DATE-STAMP THE RATE TABLE IN THE REPO. Store cost.json with {instance, region, os, tenancy, usd_per_hour, source_url, source_publication_date, accessed_date}. Re-fetch on every release and diff. AWS's feed exposes hawkFilePublicationDate, so this is automatable - one curl, no scraping, no API key.

Rule 8: THE SENTENCE THAT SURVIVES ADVERSARIAL REVIEW. Template:
  "ML-DSA-44 verification inside <prover vX.Y.Z>, batch N=<n>: <s> s prover wall-clock and <g> GiB peak RSS measured on <exact machine>. Amortised over the batch that is <s/n> s per signature. At the AWS us-east-1 c7a.16xlarge On-Demand rate of $3.28448/hr (price feed published 2026-07-23T05:59:00Z), and assuming throughput parity with the measurement machine (NOT verified), that models to $<x> per 1000 transactions. Under the commodity-GPU rate band used by Ethproofs ($0.37–$0.47 per 5090-hour, December 2025) the same measurement models to $<y>–$<z>. We report cycles per signature (<c>) so readers can substitute their own rate."
  Every clause in that sentence is either measured, quoted with a URL and date, or explicitly flagged as an unverified assumption. That is the bar.
