blockers: (1) AWS's human-readable pricing page cannot be fetched for rates - all AWS numbers here come from the awsstatic JSON feed that page consumes. If a reviewer insists on the rendered page as the source, we need a browser screenshot; the feed URL is the reproducible citation and I recommend we cite the feed and say why. (2) The Ethproofs current 5090 index VALUE is not machine-readable (server HTML renders $0.00/hr); we only have the December 2025 published band \"$0.37–$0.47/hr\". Fallback: cite the band with its December 2025 date and label it as possibly stale, or screenshot the live card. (3) NO proving market publishes a rate card - if the paper needs a market-anchored dollar figure the only defensible path is to actually submit paid requests and report on-chain fills, which costs money and requires wallets; fallback is to publish \"no public rate card; auction-cleared\" as a finding. (4) The Boundless max_price_per_cycle example ($0.00001 USD/cycle = $10/Mcycle) is 500x its own broker-side min_mcycle_price floor ($0.02/Mcycle); I could not resolve which is authoritative, so do not build any model on the per-cycle example. (5) THE BIG ONE: we are pricing laptop-measured seconds at cloud-instance hourly rates for hardware we never ran on, and a scalar prover billed at a 64-vCPU rate is being charged for 63 idle cores. Fallback is per-vCPU-hour normalisation ($3.28448/64 = $0.05132/vCPU-hr, my arithmetic) for a PRELIMINARY release, but the real fix is ~$328 of c7a.16xlarge time to measure on the priced box - cheap enough that a reviewer will not accept the excuse.

topic: Stwo / Cairo as a third prover column for PQ-STARK-BENCH, and the s2morrow-adjacent ecosystem (accessed 2026-07-23)
bottom_line: A Stwo column is technically runnable on macOS aarch64 (Stwo's SIMD backend has an aarch64/NEON path, and `scarb prove`/`scarb verify` are documented as Linux+macOS, "not available on Windows"), but it is NOT a week of work: Cairo is not a Rust zkVM, so every signature scheme must be re-implemented in the Cairo language rather than reusing your Rust crates. The bigger finding is a disconfirmation: **you cannot cite s2morrow prover numbers, because s2morrow has not published any.** Its README roadmap still shows `- [ ] Stwo proving benchmarks` unchecked, last commit 2026-03-23. So the "cite them instead of measuring" fallback does not exist as stated.
details:
## 1. What Stwo is, status, license, third-party runnable

- Repo: https://github.com/starkware-libs/stwo — "StarkWare's next gen prover". README (fetched 2026-07-23) describes it as "a production-grade Circle STARK prover and verifier, written in Rust", over the Mersenne prime field M31 (2^31 − 1). License: **Apache-2.0**. Requires **Rust nightly** (pinned in `rust-toolchain.toml`).
- Status: README states Stwo "is production" and powers SHARP, StarkWare's Shared Prover. Vendor-claimed, not independently measured by me.
- Version signal is INCONSISTENT and I flag it loudly: the GitHub README surfaced "v1.0.0, July 18 2025", while StarkWare's blog https://starkware.co/blog/s-two-2-0-0-prover-for-developers/ (dated **January 27, 2026**) announces **S-Two 2.0.0** on crates.io with semver. I could NOT resolve this: `https://api.github.com/repos/starkware-libs/stwo/releases` returned **HTTP 403** to WebFetch, so the release list is unverified. Treat the exact current stwo version as **not verified**; stwo-cairo's own `Cargo.toml` pins `stwo-cairo-prover = "~1.2.2"` (verified via GitHub code search, repo starkware-libs/stwo-cairo, `Cargo.toml`).
- Performance story: README points to a benchmark site (starkware-libs.github.io/stwo/dev/bench) and mentions a Poseidon2 script proving 2^18 permutations end-to-end single-threaded. **No prover-seconds/RAM figure is quoted in the README** — treat as not published in a citable form. The blog's only quantitative claim is market share ("approximately 20% of the perpetual derivatives market"), which is not a performance number.
- Platform: README lists SIMD backends "AVX2, AVX-512, NEON, WebAssembly SIMD". Verified in source via GitHub code search on starkware-libs/stwo: `crates/stwo/src/prover/backend/simd/fft/mod.rs` contains `if #[cfg(all(target_arch = "aarch64", target_feature = "neon"))]` and `crates/stwo/src/prover/backend/simd/m31.rs` says "Fallback to portable SIMD (works on aarch64/neon, wasm, etc.)". **This is a real advantage over SP1 on Apple Silicon**, where (per your Round-1 finding) proving is scalar CPU.

## 2. Cairo toolchain: can an outsider prove on macOS aarch64?

Supported path (measured-from-docs):
- https://docs.swmansion.com/scarb/docs/extensions/prove-and-verify.html — "Scarb integrates the stwo-cairo prover" via `scarb prove` / `scarb verify`. Verbatim caveats: **"The prover is not available on Windows."**, **"Soundness of the proof is not yet guaranteed by Stwo"**, and the docs warn the prover "performs significantly slower through Scarb versus direct usage". Recommended source build flags quoted: `RUSTFLAGS="-C target-cpu=native -C opt-level=3" --features="std"`.
- Cairo Book https://www.starknet.io/cairo-book/ch01-03-proving-a-prime-number.html gives the exact flow: `scarb execute -p <pkg> --print-program-output --output standard --arguments 17`, then `scarb prove --execution-id 1`, then `scarb verify --execution-id 1`; requires **Scarb 2.16.1 or later**, `cairo_execute = "2.16.1"`. It repeats the warning "soundness of proof is not yet guaranteed by Stwo, use at your own risk."
- **`cairo-prove` is GONE.** stwo-cairo's README (via GitHub code search, exact fragment) says: "`starkware-libs/proving-utils` — end-user proving utilities (**successor to the removed `cairo-prove` CLI**)". Any plan or blog referencing a `cairo-prove` binary is stale. The current end-user binaries live in https://github.com/starkware-libs/proving-utils (Apache-2.0): `cairo_program_runner`, `stwo-vm-runner`, `stwo-run-and-prove`.
- stwo-cairo (https://github.com/starkware-libs/stwo-cairo, Apache-2.0) needs Rust **nightly**, plus **Scarb 2.15.0** and **Starknet Foundry 0.33.0** for the Cairo verifier; its adapter consumes `cairo-vm` 3.2.0 traces. Note the version skew vs the Cairo Book's 2.16.1 — pin carefully.
- I did **not** run any of this. macOS aarch64 support is inferred from (a) docs saying Linux/macOS and (b) the NEON code path; it is **not** an executed verification.

## 3. Is there a Cairo zkVM that runs arbitrary Rust?

**No.** Cairo is its own language/VM; Stwo proves Cairo VM execution. Contrast with the Rust-targeting zkVMs: SP1 "can prove the execution of arbitrary Rust (or any LLVM-compiled language) programs" (https://github.com/succinctlabs/sp1) and RISC Zero targets `riscv32im-risc0-zkvm-elf` (https://doc.rust-lang.org/beta/rustc/platform-support/riscv32im-risc0-zkvm-elf.html). Nothing in stwo/stwo-cairo/proving-utils offers a Rust→Cairo path — the only Rust in that stack is the prover itself. **Consequence for scoping: your Rust guest programs do not port. A Stwo column means writing/porting each scheme in Cairo, which is month-scale, not week-scale, and it also breaks apples-to-apples (different guest source, different language, different hash primitives).**

## 4. Reusable Cairo implementations and licenses

- **s2morrow** — https://github.com/starkware-bitcoin/s2morrow (the `keep-starknet-strange/s2morrow` URL redirects here). **MIT License, "Copyright (c) 2025 Michael Zaikin"** (LICENSE fetched verbatim). README verbatim: "ZKVM: Cairo / STARK prover: Stwo / Signature schemes: NIST finalist — Falcon (lattice based), alternative Sphincs+ (hash based)". Roadmap verbatim: `- [x] Falcon512 verification`, `- [x] Sphincs+ 128s with SHA2 (simple mode) verification`, `- [ ] Stwo proving benchmarks`; follow-up `- [x] Sphincs+ 128s with Blake2s and 4-byte aligned address encoding`, `- [ ] Falcon512 with probabilistic polynomial multiplication checking`. Last commit `831bb51` "fix blake2s hasher (#7)", **2026-03-23**, author michael.zaikin@starkware.co. 21 commits total, 16 stars.
- s2morrow's Makefile (fetched verbatim) shows the actual prove path is NOT `scarb prove`: it installs `stwo_run_and_prove` from **`ssh://git@github.com/m-kus/proving-utils.git`** at rev `efbaeebfdce3463aa61e16d7d8e6069f03df0994` under `cargo +nightly-2025-07-14`, and proves a **bootloader** program (`resources/simple_bootloader_compiled.json`) with `--prover_params_json prover_params.json --proof-format cairo-serde --verify`. `m-kus/proving-utils` is a public fork of starkware-libs/proving-utils (Apache-2.0), so the ssh:// URL can be swapped to https, but you are pinned to one person's fork at one rev. `.tool-versions` says **`scarb 2.14.0`** — older than both stwo-cairo's 2.15.0 and the Cairo Book's 2.16.1.
- s2morrow `prover_params.json`, verbatim: `{"channel_hash": "blake2s", "pcs_config": {"pow_bits": 26, "fri_config": {"log_last_layer_degree_bound": 0, "log_blowup_factor": 1, "n_queries": 70}}, "preprocessed_trace": "canonical_without_pedersen"}`. **This is a citable security-parameter set** — note `log_blowup_factor: 1` and 70 queries + 26 pow bits, which is a different soundness point than RISC Zero/SP1 defaults and MUST be disclosed in any cross-prover chart.
- **Garaga** — https://github.com/keep-starknet-strange/garaga, **MIT** (LICENSE present). **DISCONFIRMATION: Garaga does not ship Falcon in Cairo at the commit I inspected (307c00af).** `src/src/signatures/` contains exactly `ecdsa.cairo`, `eddsa_25519.cairo`, `rsa.cairo`, `schnorr.cairo` — no Falcon, no SPHINCS+, no ML-DSA. GitHub code search for "falcon" in that repo returns only 2 hits, both inside `.claude/skills/benchmarking-cairo/` (a `--package falcon` example), i.e. tooling scaffolding, not a shipped package. Anyone citing "Falcon via Garaga" is likely mis-reading the s2morrow.xyz gas table, where "garaga" rows are **secp256r1/secp256k1**, not Falcon.
- **s2morrow.xyz** (https://www.s2morrow.xyz/) publishes only **on-chain gas**, verbatim: "Falcon-512: ~9.5M", "secp256r1 syscall: ~27.4M", "secp256r1 garaga: ~4.6M", "secp256k1 syscall: ~16.6M", "secp256k1 garaga: ~4.5M" (L2 gas), plus "512 Zq coefficients packed into 29 felt252 storage slots. 1,030 felts reduced to 62 felts on-chain", "65% savings", "17x calldata compression". **No proving time, no proof size, no batch size, no hardware.** These are verification-gas numbers, orthogonal to your prover-cost headline metric — do not let them be mistaken for prover data.
- **ML-DSA-44 in Cairo: not found.** No Cairo/Starknet ML-DSA implementation surfaced in search. This is consistent with your Round-1 finding that ML-DSA-44 is the genuinely empty cell — it is empty on the Cairo/Stwo side too, not just RISC-V zkVMs.
- Related but off-path: ZKNoxHQ/ETHFALCON is a **Solidity** verifier + Python signer for Ethereum, not Cairo.
- Prior-art context re-confirmed: Ethan Heilman's delvingbitcoin post "Post Quantum Signatures and Scaling Bitcoin with STARKs" (2025-04-07) has sizes/throughput estimates ("the smallest is 1.5kb for the public key + signature", "~87 txns/sec", "555 txns/second") but **no proving time, proof size, or hardware**.

## 5. Verdict

- **Do not plan to "cite s2morrow numbers" for prover cost — they do not exist.** That is the headline disconfirmation of this round and it is worth stating publicly in LITERATURE.md, because it also means the PQ-in-Stwo prover-cost cell is *open*, not occupied.
- A Stwo column is feasible-but-expensive for a small project. Cost drivers: (a) no Rust reuse, everything in Cairo; (b) the s2morrow path depends on a personal fork of proving-utils at a pinned rev with a bootloader program, not the documented `scarb prove` flow; (c) three conflicting Scarb pins in play (2.14.0 / 2.15.0 / 2.16.1); (d) `cairo-prove` was removed, so much online guidance is stale.
- Recommended shape if you want Stwo without a month of work: reuse s2morrow **as-is** (MIT permits it) — build its `falcon` and `sphincs_plus` packages, run `scarb execute --print-resource-usage` for step counts, and produce the **Stwo proving benchmarks its own roadmap leaves unchecked**, over batch sizes, on named hardware, with its `prover_params.json` disclosed. That is a genuine, creditable contribution and it does not require you to write Cairo crypto. Credit s2morrow for the Cairo implementations; the measurement is yours.
- Mandatory disclosure if you publish a Stwo column: different guest language, different hash (blake2s channel), different FRI/soundness parameters (`log_blowup_factor: 1`, `n_queries: 70`, `pow_bits: 26`), Stwo's own "soundness not yet guaranteed" warning, and the fact that Stwo gets NEON SIMD on Apple Silicon while SP1 does not. Without those four lines the cross-prover chart is misleading.

topic: Lattice-native / native PQ signature aggregation (LaBRADOR, Chipmunk/Squirrel/Lemur, Ethereum hash-based aggregation) as the competing approach to zkVM PQ-signature verification — with the honest 2026 comparison
bottom_line: Native aggregation is not a "maybe" — it is measured, published, open-source, and roughly 1000x cheaper per signature on the prover than a general-purpose zkVM. Two hard reference points, both on named consumer hardware with public code: LaBRADOR/LaZer aggregates 10,000 Falcon-512 signatures in 5.9565 s single-threaded into a 74.07 KB proof (~0.6 ms amortized prover per signature); leanEthereum's leanVM aggregates XMSS at "1426 XMSS/s - 327 KiB" on an M4 Max 48GB CPU-only (~0.7 ms per signature). Our README must state up front that PQ-STARK-BENCH measures a DIFFERENT question — generic zkVM verification of unmodified FIPS schemes over arbitrary transaction messages — and must NOT claim to be competitive on amortized prover cost with a purpose-built aggregator. The defensible framings are: generality vs specialization; the Drake-Khovratovich-Kudinov-Wagner "random oracle paradox" that rules ML-DSA and seeded Falcon out of the hash-native path entirely; and the fact that per-signature cost is flat in both worlds, so the axis that matters is the per-signature constant, not batch size. Separately: a 2026 ePrint says Chipmunk's headline parameters are ~30-bit, not 112-bit, security — never cite Chipmunk's "8192 sigs into ~136 KB" without that caveat.
details:
ACCESS DATE FOR ALL SOURCES: 2026-07-23.

METHODOLOGY WARNING (read first): eprint.iacr.org PDF endpoints (e.g. https://eprint.iacr.org/2022/1341.pdf, /2024/311.pdf, /2025/055.pdf) return **HTTP 403 with a text/html body** to both WebFetch and curl with a browser UA. The ePrint *landing* pages (no .pdf) fetch fine and carry the full abstract + dates. Where I needed in-paper tables I used non-ePrint mirrors (cic.iacr.org, iacr.org slide archive, GitHub raw, ethresear.ch Discourse .json). Anything I could not open is marked "not retrieved" rather than guessed.

=====================================================================
1. LaBRADOR — what it is and its published numbers
=====================================================================
"LaBRADOR: Compact Proofs for R1CS from Module-SIS", Ward Beullens and Gregor Seiler (IBM Research Europe). https://eprint.iacr.org/2022/1341 — Received: October 7, 2022; Approved: October 10, 2022. CRYPTO 2023.
Abstract, verbatim: "LaBRADOR proves knowledge of a solution for an R1CS mod $2^{64}+1$ with $2^{20}$ constraints, with a proof size of only 58 KB, an order of magnitude more compact than previous quantum-safe proofs."
It is a *lattice* (Module-SIS) recursive-folding argument, NOT hash-based. That is exactly why it is the natural competitor: proving "I know a short (s1,s2) with s1 + h*s2 = t mod q" is a native lattice relation, so a lattice proof system pays almost nothing for it, whereas a zkVM must simulate NTTs and SHAKE in a RISC-V trace.
DISCONFIRMATION: the ePrint landing page for 2022/1341 contains **no prover-time figures and no mention of Falcon or Dilithium**. Anyone citing "LaBRADOR prover time" from the abstract is citing something that is not there. Prover times below come from the LaZer slides and the PSE benchmarks.

Vendor/author-presented (IBM, RWC 2024): "LaZer: a Lattice Library for Zero-Knowledge and Succinct Proofs", Vadim Lyubashevsky, Gregor Seiler, Patrick Steuer, IBM Research Europe. Slides: https://iacr.org/submit/files/slides/2024/rwc/rwc2024/125/slides.pdf (22 slides, fetched OK).
- Slide 15, verbatim: "LaBRADOR SNARK ... Small concrete proof size of about 60 KB (optimized params) ... Fully vectorized polynomial arithmetic using AVX-512 instructions".
- Slide 19, verbatim heading: "Dachshund Example: Signature Aggregation • Prove many Falcon signatures for same message under different public keys". Table verbatim:
    No of signatures: 100 / 1'000 / 10'000
    Size: 84.82 KB / 81.2 KB / 83.91 KB
    Prover Time: 102 ms / 590 ms / 5.56 s
    Verifier Time: 81 ms / 396 ms / 3.22 s
  NOTE: no hardware is stated on the slide. Treat as vendor-claimed, hardware unspecified.
- Slide 21, verbatim, "Comparison to STARKs for n=2^24": Brakedown-PC 93767 KB / commit 150 s / prove 13 s / verify 2.56 s; Ligero-PC 14383 KB / 169 s / 12.4 s / 0.402 s; Greyhound 91 KB / 23.5 s / 8.47 s / 1.28 s.

Follow-up paper tying LaBRADOR to Falcon: "Aggregating Falcon Signatures with LaBRADOR", Marius A. Aardal, Diego F. Aranha, Katharina Boudgoust, Sebastian Kolby, Akira Takahashi. https://eprint.iacr.org/2024/311 — Received: February 23, 2024; Revised: August 9, 2024. CRYPTO 2024 (LNCS 14920, pp. 71-106). Abstract opens verbatim: "Several prior works have suggested to use non-interactive arguments of knowledge with short proofs to aggregate signatures of Falcon, which is part of the first post-quantum signatures selected for standardization by NIST."
The paper's own concrete proof-size table was **not retrieved** (PDF 403; hal.science/hal-04700114 mirrors also returned HTML, not PDF). Its contributions per the abstract are the first knowledge-soundness analysis of non-interactive LaBRADOR plus "predicate special soundness (PSS)". Code: https://github.com/dfaranha/aggregate-falcon.

MEASURED, THIRD-PARTY, NAMED HARDWARE — the number to actually cite:
"Lattice-based signature aggregation", ethresear.ch topic 22282, posted 2025-05-07 by miha-stopar; "joint report by David Nevado, Dohoon Kim, and Miha Stopar" (PSE). https://ethresear.ch/t/lattice-based-signature-aggregation/22282
Verbatim: "We measured proving times for the aggregation of batches between 3,000 to 10,000 Falcon-512 signatures. Benchmarks are obtained with single-thread executions on a 11th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-11400H @ 2.70GHz processor."
Table verbatim (Signatures / Proving Time (s) / Verification Time (s) / Proof Size):
    3000  1.6921 ± 0.2220  0.7739 ± 0.0888  77.83 KB
    4000  2.1991 ± 0.1403  1.0321 ± 0.1044  69.82 KB
    5000  3.0182 ± 0.4394  1.3380 ± 0.2021  72.45 KB
    6000  3.7914 ± 0.5716  1.6779 ± 0.2989  72.11 KB
    7000  4.3709 ± 0.4716  1.8586 ± 0.1928  71.83 KB
    8000  5.1447 ± 0.5469  2.1430 ± 0.2175  74.02 KB
    9000  5.5085 ± 0.4382  2.3821 ± 0.1915  72.27 KB
    10000 5.9565 ± 0.3750  2.6492 ± 0.1848  74.07 KB
Verbatim key-takeaway: "10k Falcon-512 signatures can be aggregated with LaBRADOR resulting in: 74.07KB proof size. 5.95s proof generation. 2.65s proof verification." And: "the verification time stands out as the biggest obstacle for adoption."
They benchmarked via "the agg_sig.py script from the Lazer repository, which wraps the LaBRADOR C implementation", using "our fork of LaBRADOR, which includes modifications to support larger signature batches (exceeding 2,000 signatures) ... increase the modulus to approximately 2^{48}".
MY INFERENCE (not published): 5.9565 s / 10,000 = ~0.60 ms amortized prover per Falcon-512 signature, single-threaded, on a 2021 mobile i5. Prover time is essentially LINEAR in N (1.69 s at 3k -> 5.96 s at 10k) while proof size is FLAT (~70-78 KB) — the exact amortization shape our Round-1 finding established for STARKs. Native aggregation does not escape linear prover cost; it has a ~1000x smaller constant.
Their head-to-head table, verbatim (Metric | LaBRADOR + Falcon (10000 sigs, 1 thread) | Hash-Based (8192 sigs, 4 threads)):
    Proof Size: 74.07 KB | 1.7 MB (targeting ~128 KB with optimization)
    Proving Time: 5.95 s | 5 s
    Verification Time: 2.65 s | 106 ms
    PQ Security: Yes (lattice-based) | Yes (hash-based: Poseidon2 signature, Keccak in PCS merklization)
    Parallelization: To be explored | Very good — 4 threads used; scales almost linearly up to 4
BLS baseline from the same thread, posted 2025-05-12 by user "asn", explicitly hedged as "I don't have precise benchmarks right now": "Aggregate signature size: 96 bytes / Prover time: 10ms (just 10k G_1 additions) / Verifier time: 1ms (essentially two pairings) / PQ security: No".

SAME-MESSAGE vs DIFFERENT-MESSAGE — a caveat we must disclose:
LaZer slide 19 says "same message under different public keys" (multi-signature setting). The PSE write-up states the aggregated relation as "s_{i,1} + h_i s_{i,2} + q v_i = t_i for i=1,...,N" — per-signature message hash t_i, i.e. different messages. These framings are not reconcilable from the sources I could open, and I could not open the CRYPTO'24 paper to settle it. The README must not assert either way. This matters enormously: transaction settlement is inherently different-message.

=====================================================================
2. Native PQ signature aggregation without a zkVM: Squirrel / Chipmunk / Lemur
=====================================================================
"Chipmunk: Better Synchronized Multi-Signatures from Lattices", Nils Fleischhacker, Gottfried Herold, Mark Simkin, Zhenfei Zhang. https://eprint.iacr.org/2023/1820 — Received 2023-11-25; Revised 2023-11-27. CCS 2023.
Abstract verbatim: "Our aggregate signature size is 5.6 × smaller and for 112 bits of security our construction allows for compressing 8192 individual signatures into a multi-signature of size around 136 KB. We provide a full implementation of Chipmunk and provide extensive benchmarks studying our construction's efficiency."
Predecessor: "Squirrel: Efficient Synchronized Multi-Signatures from Lattices" (Fleischhacker, Simkin, Zhang), https://eprint.iacr.org/2022/694, CCS 2022.

*** TOP-VALUE DISCONFIRMATION *** "Lemur: Scalable Post-Quantum Synchronized Multi-Signatures", Yini Lin, Muhammed F. Esgin, Amin Sakzad, Ron Steinfeld (Monash), Markku-Juhani O. Saarinen (Tampere). https://eprint.iacr.org/2026/1161 — Received 2026-06-03; Approved 2026-06-08.
Abstract, verbatim: "Our revisit of the framework is also motivated by the fact that our evaluation of Chipmunk's parameter sets, using the state-of-the-art lattice security estimation methods, suggests a substantially lower concrete security level (approximately 30 bits rather than the claimed 112 bits)."
Also verbatim: "After rectifying the parameters of Chipmunk for a fair comparison, our results show that Lemur's KOTS size achieves up to an order of magnitude improvement over Chipmunk's KOTS. In particular, aggregating 1 million one-time signatures requires under 8 KB. For the total multi-signature size, Lemur demonstrates around 2× improvement over Chipmunk. To showcase our design, we provide a full-fledged Rust implementation. Our benchmarks demonstrate an aggregate signature size of 380 KB for 2^20 signers. Stateful signing takes roughly 4.2 ms for a Merkle tree of height 20, while batch verification for an aggregate of 1024 signers completes in 15.0 ms (≈ 14.6 μs per signer)."
This is Lemur-authors-claimed, not independently replicated. But it is a published 2026 ePrint saying a CCS 2023 paper's headline parameters are ~30-bit. Cite Chipmunk *with* this caveat or the first cryptographer who reads our README will.
CRITICAL SCOPE LIMIT of this whole family: these are SYNCHRONIZED multi-signatures — same message, same time step, a-priori bounded number of signings, stateful keys. Chipmunk abstract verbatim: "it is only required that signatures for the same time step are aggregatable". They are a BLS replacement for consensus attestations, NOT a mechanism for aggregating N different users' signatures over N different transactions. They are therefore not a substitute for what PQ-STARK-BENCH measures — but only if we say so precisely.
No aggregation scheme for *unmodified* ML-DSA / FIPS 204 surfaced anywhere in this search. Native aggregation always requires either Falcon-specific algebra or a purpose-designed new scheme.

=====================================================================
3. Ethereum's hash-based direction (leanSig / "Lean Ethereum") — published numbers
=====================================================================
"Hash-Based Multi-Signatures for Post-Quantum Ethereum", Justin Drake, Dmitry Khovratovich (Ethereum Foundation), Mikhail Kudinov (TU Eindhoven), Benedikt Wagner (EF). IACR Communications in Cryptology Vol. 2 No. 1, 59 pages, doi 10.62056/aey7qjp10 — full PDF fetched from https://cic.iacr.org/p/2/1/13 (ePrint mirror is 2025/055). This is the design doc for the XMSS+Poseidon2 direction.
- §8.1 hardware, verbatim: "We benchmark this implementation with Criterion on a MacBook Pro with Apple M3 Pro chip, 18 GB memory. We have not implemented any parallelization." Parameters: "security levels kC = 128 (classical) and kQ = 64 (quantum)". Code: https://github.com/b-wagn/hash-sig and https://github.com/b-wagn/hashsig-parameters.
- Table 3 (Poseidon2, lifetime L=2^20), verbatim row for the recommended set: "TSW w= 2,δ= 1.1  668.14 [Gen s]  853.66 [Sign µs]  354.09 [Ver µs]  2.7 [Sig KiB]  105 [π16 AC]  59 [π24 AC]  105 [π16 WC]  59 [π24 WC]".
- Table 2 (SHA-3-256, L=2^20) same encoding: "TSW w= 2,δ = 1.1  65.06  54.77  26.82  2.36  269.91  269.91" (Gen s / Sign µs / Ver µs / Sig KiB / Hash AC words / Hash WC words).
- §8.2 verbatim: "we observe that Poseidon2-based instantiations are significantly slower than their SHA-3-based counterparts (concretely, a factor of about 10)". Slower to evaluate natively, far cheaper to prove — that trade is the whole point.
- §8.3, verbatim and directly relevant to us: "While we do not provide concrete benchmarks for signature aggregation using pqSNARKs, we give a high-level discussion on the topic. We emphasize that the following estimates are preliminary." Two candidates, verbatim: "1. Custom Circuit Approach. ... e.g., via post-quantum instances of the Plonky3 framework or stwo." and "2. zkVM-Based Approach. Alternatively, one could utilize zkVMs, which can generically prove the verifier's code. This approach simplifies the process of writing an Ethereum specification and is less error-prone. However, it comes at the cost of reduced efficiency."
- Aggregate size estimate, verbatim: "Our preliminary estimates suggest that the aggregate signature size using Plonky3 (which employs FRI) ranges from 2 MB to 3 MB. This means as soon as we have more than 1000 signatures, aggregation saves space."
- Aggregation throughput estimate, verbatim: "Assuming that verifying a single signature requires approximately 160 hash operations (or Poseidon2 permutations), which corresponds to TSW with parameters w = 2, δ = 1.1, we estimate that aggregating up to 10, 000 signatures within one second is feasible. This estimation is based on the requirement to prove approximately 1.75 · 10^6 hashes per second".
*** THE QUOTE THAT COMPLICATES THE NAIVE "JUST PUT ML-DSA IN A zkVM" PITCH ***, §2.3 verbatim: "Using any signature scheme based on the Fiat-Shamir heuristic in combination with a succinct argument would cause the paradoxical situation mentioned in the introduction. Namely, one would, at the same time, treat hash functions as random oracles and as explicit circuits. In particular, this applies to Dilithium and to MPC-in-the-head and VOLE-in-the-head signatures such as FAEST or Biscuit. It also holds for Falcon signatures if random seeds are used. A variant of Falcon without random seeds in combination with a pqSNARK would be a reasonable route for further exploration, because in this case the random oracle can be evaluated on the message outside of the circuit that is proven."
This is a soundness/proof-model objection to exactly what PQ-STARK-BENCH benchmarks. Our README must acknowledge it: our numbers are engineering costs, and the EF authors argue the resulting construction lacks a clean security proof. That is both an honesty point and our differentiator — nobody has published the engineering cost of the thing they are declining to do.
Follow-up: "Technical Note: LeanSig for Post-Quantum Ethereum", same four authors, https://eprint.iacr.org/2025/1332 — Received 2025-07-21; Revised 2025-09-12. Abstract verbatim: "In this note, we present a new instantiation of the hash-based multi-signature framework introduced by Drake, Khovratovich, Kudinov, and Wagner (CiC Vol 2 Issue 1, eprint 2025/055) for Ethereum's consensus layer." The landing page carries **no** benchmark numbers.

MEASURED aggregation throughput — the real number, from open code:
https://github.com/leanEthereum/leanMultisig (the URL now resolves to the "leanVM" project — "Minimal hash-based zkVM, for a Post-Quantum Ethereum"). README fetched raw from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/leanEthereum/leanMultisig/main/README.md.
Proving system, verbatim: "multilinear with WHIR, allowing polynomial stacking (reducing proof size)"; "SuperSpartan, with AIR-specific optimizations"; "Logup, with a system of buses similar to OpenVM". "The VM design is inspired by the famous Cairo paper."
Benchmarks header verbatim: "Machine: M4 Max 48GB (CPU only)" and "*Expect incoming perf improvements.*"
XMSS aggregation table verbatim (WHIR rate | Proven Regime | Proximity Gaps Conjecture):
    1/2 | 1426 XMSS/s - 327 KiB | 1481 XMSS/s - 171 KiB
    1/4 | 1027 XMSS/s - 220 KiB | 1049 XMSS/s - 122 KiB
Invocation verbatim: "cargo run --release -- xmss --n-signatures 1550 --log-inv-rate 1".
Recursion table (n previously-aggregated proofs, "each containing 700 XMSS"), verbatim rows: "1 | 1/2 | 0.22s = 1 x 0.22s - 268 KiB | 0.16s = 1 x 0.16s - 136 KiB"; "2 | 1/2 | 0.53s = 2 x 0.26s - 265 KiB | 0.33s = 2 x 0.16s - 152 KiB"; "4 | 1/4 | 0.87s = 4 x 0.22s - 199 KiB | 0.64s = 4 x 0.16s - 105 KiB".
Security, verbatim: approximately "124 bits of provable security" via Johnson bound with a degree-5 extension of the koala-bear field.
MY INFERENCE: 1426 XMSS/s ≈ 0.70 ms amortized prover per hash-based signature on an M4 Max, CPU-only — within ~1.2x of LaBRADOR's per-Falcon-signature cost, ~5x better than the paper's own preliminary "10,000 signatures within one second" target, and at 327 KiB rather than the "2 MB to 3 MB" estimated for Plonky3/FRI.
Corroborating secondary source (lower confidence): https://blog.lambdaclass.com/ethereum-signature-schemes-explained-ecdsa-bls-xmss-and-post-quantum-leansig-with-rust-code-examples/ , dated February 9, 2026, verbatim: "It benchmarks aggregating hundreds of XMSS signatures per second on consumer hardware (M4 Max)." This UNDERSTATES the repo's own README (1426/s vs "hundreds"). Prefer the repo.

zkVM-in-this-lane prior art we must cite (closest existing thing to our benchmark):
"HAPPIER: Hash-Based, Aggregatable, Practical Post-quantum Signatures Implemented Efficiently with Risc0", Arda Saygan, Muhammed Said Gündoğan, Atakan Arslan, Mehmet Emin Gönen. In *Lightweight Cryptography for Security and Privacy* (LightSec 2025), published January 31, 2026. https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-032-15541-2_1 (reachable only after following two redirects; full text paywalled — abstract-level only).
Reported: signatures of "2–3 MB"; "up to 2^16 signatures on a standard laptop"; built on XMSS; "RISC0's zkVM with STARK proofs (Groth16 composition disabled to maintain quantum resistance)"; claims first PQ aggregation scheme with multi-level/incremental aggregation. It contrasts itself against prior work allegedly requiring "hundreds of gigabytes of RAM to aggregate 2^10 signatures".
*** FLAG LOUDLY *** I could not verify that "hundreds of gigabytes of RAM to aggregate 2^10 signatures" claim, and it looks wrong. PSE measured 10,000 (~2^13.3) Falcon-512 signatures aggregating in 5.9565 s single-threaded on a mobile i5-11400H — a machine without hundreds of GB of RAM. Either HAPPIER means a different prior work, or the claim is stale/incorrect. Do NOT repeat it. It is also a risk in the other direction: HAPPIER is RISC Zero + hash-based PQ signatures with real numbers, so any "no one has benchmarked PQ signatures in a zkVM" framing is FALSE for hash-based schemes and must be narrowed to ML-DSA/FIPS-204 specifically.
Also in this lane, still empty: https://github.com/starkware-bitcoin/s2morrow ("S2morrow — STARK-based aggregation of quantum-resistant signatures", ZKVM: Cairo, prover: Stwo, schemes: Falcon-512 and SPHINCS+-128s). Roadmap verbatim: "[x] Falcon512 verification / [x] Sphincs+ 128s with SHA2 (simple mode) verification / [ ] Stwo proving benchmarks". Its stated goal is verbatim "compare proving time for different signature schemes, as well as benchmark vs other approaches (e.g. LaBRADOR)" — StarkWare is aiming at almost exactly our benchmark and has not shipped numbers. Default branch is `master`; `main` 404s.

=====================================================================
4. The honest comparison for a 2026 PQ-chain designer
=====================================================================
This is my synthesis, grounded in the numbers above; label it as such in the README.
(a) SAME message across the batch (consensus attestations/validator votes): use native aggregation, full stop. Synchronized lattice multi-sigs (Lemur: 380 KB for 2^20 signers, 15.0 ms batch verify for 1024) or hash-native leanVM (1426 XMSS/s, 327 KiB) beat any zkVM by orders of magnitude, and the EF has already chosen this path.
(b) DIFFERENT messages (transaction settlement — our case) with Falcon: LaBRADOR/Dachshund is the strongest published option (~74 KB, ~0.6 ms/sig prover), but (i) verification is 2.65 s single-threaded, which the PSE authors call "the biggest obstacle for adoption", (ii) it is Falcon-specific algebra that does not generalize to ML-DSA, and (iii) the same-message/different-message question above is unresolved in my sources.
(c) ML-DSA (FIPS 204) or seeded Falcon: native aggregation does not exist, and the EF paper argues a pqSNARK over it is proof-theoretically problematic (random-oracle paradox). A zkVM is the only mechanism that works today, and its cost has not been published. That, narrowly stated, is our contribution.
(d) THE MOST IMPORTANT NUMBER FOR OUR README IS NOT OURS — it is the EF's own conclusion that at Ethereum's current scale, aggregation may not be worth it at all. "Revisiting Falcon signature aggregation for PQ mempools", ethresear.ch topic 24431, posted 2026-03-18 by b-wagn; authors Antonio Sanso, Thomas Thiery, Benedikt Wagner. https://ethresear.ch/t/revisiting-falcon-signature-aggregation-for-pq-mempools/24431
Verbatim: Falcon signature size "666 Byte" vs ECDSA "< 100 Byte". Case 3 (aggregation) "crosses below Case 1 around N \approx 200". Verbatim on their aggregate-size model: "When computing a_N in our scripts below, we call the scripts from Falcon signature aggregation with LaBRADOR in a black-box way. Of course, not only LaBRADOR can be used for aggregation, but any SNARK. We note that hash-based SNARKs (which seem to be favored in Ethereum these days) are larger than LaBRADOR, and so using LaBRADOR is a very optimistic estimate for size of aggregated signatures." And verbatim: "We do not consider the case of key recovery with aggregation, as this is not covered by the work on Falcon signature aggregation. This is primarly because the statement to be proven here would involve hashes and would have less algebraic structure, which means it is less efficient to prove".
A commenter (manel1874, 2026-03-18) adapts it to the consensus layer where validator indexes replace public keys: "assuming the proof size stays more or less constant at about ~70 KB, the break even point should be around 100 signatures". Another (latifkasuli) adds, verbatim: "the N ≈ 200 crossover assumes a stable batch, but mempool churn (replacements, expiries, per-peer differences) means the effective crossover for propagation bandwidth is probably further to the right than the storage-only plot suggests. Aggregation over a moving set is a harder problem than aggregation over a fixed set." — to which b-wagn replied "Thanks, this is a valid point!"
So: for PQ *transactions*, three EF researchers concluded in March 2026 that plain Falcon with key recovery and NO aggregation may be the sweet spot at today's ~250-tx blocks. Our README should lead the "why not native aggregation" section with this, because it reframes the debate: the honest answer for a 2026 chain designer is often "aggregate nothing", and the batch sizes where any of this pays off (N > 100-200 for size, far higher for prover economics) are exactly the batch sizes our sweep should cover.

SUGGESTED README ANSWER TO "why not LaBRADOR?" (one paragraph, every claim sourced above):
"Because LaBRADOR is Falcon-shaped. It proves the native lattice relation s1 + h·s2 = t directly, which is why LaZer/Dachshund aggregates 10,000 Falcon-512 signatures in 5.9565 s single-threaded into a 74.07 KB proof (PSE, ethresear.ch, May 2025, i5-11400H) — about 0.6 ms of prover per signature, roughly 1000x cheaper than any general-purpose zkVM. We are not competing with that number and we say so on every chart. What LaBRADOR cannot do is ML-DSA, or any FIPS scheme you did not co-design the proof system around, or a verifier written in ordinary Rust an auditor can read. Its verification is 2.65 s and single-threaded. And Ethereum's own researchers argue that putting a Fiat-Shamir scheme like ML-DSA inside a pqSNARK is proof-theoretically paradoxical (Drake-Khovratovich-Kudinov-Wagner, CiC 2:1:13, §2.3) — which is precisely why the engineering cost of doing it has never been published. That cost is what this benchmark measures."

topic: Falcon / FN-DSA floating-point split, FIPS 206 status, verify-in-zkVM feasibility, and Rust implementation choice for PQ-STARK-BENCH
bottom_line: Confirmed from the Falcon spec itself: floating point is confined to signing (and part of keygen); Verify (Algorithm 16) is "using only integer operations" — hash-to-point via SHAKE-256, Decompress, one polynomial product mod q=12289, and a squared-L2-norm check against ⌊β²⌋=34,034,726. So sign-outside/verify-inside is not a workaround, it is the natural split, and it is what Algorand and s2morrow actually do. BIG CORRECTION: FIPS 206 has NO published draft as of 2026-07-23 — csrc.nist.gov/pubs/fips/206/ipd returns HTTP 404, FIPS 206 does not appear in NIST's FIPS publication listing, and Thomas Pornin's fn-dsa crate (updated 2026-07-22, one day before access) still states "no FN-DSA draft has been published yet". Do NOT write "FIPS 206 standard"; write "Falcon, selected by NIST in 2022 for standardization as FN-DSA/FIPS 206; the standard is not yet published, not even as a draft." For the guest, use Thomas Pornin's `fn-dsa-vrfy` v0.4.0 (Unlicense, `#![no_std]`, stack-only buffers, pure integer, no C toolchain).
details:
## 1. Which part of Falcon needs floating point — RESOLVED, from the spec

Source: Falcon specification v1.2, 01/10/2020, https://falcon-sign.info/falcon.pdf (accessed 2026-07-23; fetched as PDF and text-extracted locally with pypdf).

§2.7.2 "Limitations", verbatim:
> "Floating-point arithmetic. Our signing procedure uses floating-point arithmetic with 53 bits of precision. While this poses no problem for a software implementation, it may prove to be a major limitation when implementation on constrained devices – in particular those without a floating-point unit – will be considered."

§4.1 "Floating-Point", verbatim:
> "Signature generation, and also part of key pair generation, involve the use of complex numbers. These can be approximated with standard IEEE 754 floating-point numbers ("binary64" format, commonly known as "double precision")."

Note the precise scope: signing AND *part of* key pair generation (Babai's reduction in keygen uses FP; §4.4). Verification is never listed.

§3.10.2, Algorithm 16 Verify, verbatim:
```
Algorithm 16 Verify (m, sig, pk, ⌊β2⌋)
Require: A message m, a signature sig = (r, s), a public key pk = h ∈ Zq[x]/(ϕ), a bound ⌊β2⌋
Ensure: Accept or reject
1: c ← HashToPoint(r∥m, q, n)
2: s2 ← Decompress(s, 8·sbytelen − 328)
3: if (s2 = ⊥) then
4:   reject                     ▷ Reject invalid encodings
5: s1 ← c − s2h mod q           ▷ s1 should be normalized between ⌈−q/2⌉ and ⌊q/2⌋
6: if ∥(s1, s2)∥2 ≤ ⌊β2⌋ then
7:   accept
8: else
9:   reject                     ▷ Reject signatures that are too long
```
and immediately after, verbatim:
> "Computation of s1 can be performed entirely in Zq[x]/(ϕ); the resulting values should then be normalized to the ⌈−q/2⌉ to ⌊q/2⌋ range. In order to avoid computing a square root, the squared norm can be computed, using only integer operations, and then compared to ⌊β2⌋."

§2.7.1 "Advantages", verbatim:
> "Easy signature verification. The signature procedure is very simple: essentially, one just needs to compute [H(r∥m) − s2h] mod q, which boils down to a few NTT operations and a hash computation."

Corroboration from Thomas Pornin (Falcon co-author), https://github.com/pornin/rust-fn-dsa (accessed 2026-07-23), verbatim: "Key pair generation and signature verification use only integer operations."

Corroboration (vendor, but consistent), Algorand technical brief, published 2025-11-03, https://algorand.co/blog/technical-brief-quantum-resistant-transactions-on-algorand-with-falcon-signatures : "verification uses only integer arithmetic and is exceptionally fast—typically under 100 microseconds on modern CPUs" and "Falcon's signing process relies on floating-point arithmetic and fast Fourier sampling, making it more complex to implement correctly".

VERDICT: your premise is exactly right, with one refinement — it is signing PLUS part of keygen, not "keygen and signing" wholesale. Verification is pure integer.

## 2. FIPS 206 status as of 2026-07-23 — THE BRIEF'S WORDING NEEDS FIXING (disconfirmation)

Direct evidence that NO draft exists:
- `https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/fips/206/ipd` → **HTTP 404 Not Found** (fetched 2026-07-23). The IPD landing page pattern that exists for 203/204/205 does not exist for 206.
- `https://csrc.nist.gov/publications/fips` (accessed 2026-07-23) lists FIPS 203, 204, 205 all "Final, 8/13/2024". **FIPS 206 does not appear at all** — neither final nor draft.
- `https://csrc.nist.gov/publications/search?keywords-lg=206` returns no FIPS 206 record.
- NIST PQC project page https://csrc.nist.gov/projects/post-quantum-cryptography (accessed 2026-07-23) says only that the "Falcon digital signature algorithm" was "selected for ongoing standardization; that process is underway" — no FIPS 206 number, no draft, no date.
- Strongest freshness signal: `fn-dsa-vrfy` v0.4.0, **published 2026-07-22T16:38:34Z** (crates.io API), one day before access, crate-level doc comment verbatim:
> "**The FN-DSA standard is currently being drafted, but no version has been published yet. When published, it may differ from the exact scheme implemented in this crate, in particular with regard to key encodings, message pre-hashing, and domain separation. Key pairs generated with this crate MAY fail to be interoperable with the final FN-DSA standard.**"
  (source: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pornin/rust-fn-dsa/main/fn-dsa-vrfy/src/lib.rs). This is the Falcon co-author, updating the day before our access date, saying no draft is out.

What the secondary sources say (all *older* and *anticipatory*, none confirming publication):
- DigiCert, published **2025-09-05**, https://www.digicert.com/blog/quantum-ready-fndsa-nears-draft-approval-from-nist : "On August 28, 2025, NIST submitted the draft standard for FN-DSA (FIPS 206) for approval." Expects IPD "may coincide with the NIST PQC Standardization Conference in late September 2025"; "the public review will last about a year, with the final standard expected in late 2026 or early 2027." Title itself says *"Nears Draft Approval"* — i.e. not published.
- NIST pqc-forum thread "FIPS 206 Status Update", https://groups.google.com/a/list.nist.gov/g/pqc-forum/c/1HXzjlMUU6Y — the only status-ish statement retrievable is Phillip Gajland, Oct 14 2025: "Regarding the changes introduced in our original analysis, we believe NIST has decided to include them in the upcoming standard." Note "upcoming".
- The NIST slide deck https://csrc.nist.gov/csrc/media/presentations/2025/fips-206-fn-dsa-(falcon)/images-media/fips_206-perlner_2.1.pdf (Ray Perlner, 6th PQC Standardization Conf., Sept 2025) exists but is **image-only / not text-extractable**; I could not quote it. Flagging rather than paraphrasing.

**LOUD FLAG:** web-search AI summaries repeatedly asserted "the initial public draft has been published" and "NIST FIPS 206 is in final review as of 2026". **I could not verify either claim against any NIST URL, and the NIST publication listing and the /ipd 404 actively contradict "published".** Do not repeat those sentences. If anyone cites them, the citation trail dead-ends in a Manifold prediction market and SEO blog posts (qubitchain.io, encryptionconsulting.com), not NIST.

**Recommended benchmark wording:** "Falcon was selected by NIST in July 2022 for standardization as FN-DSA (FIPS 206). As of 2026-07-23 the standard has not been published — not even as an initial public draft (FIPS 206 does not appear in NIST's publication listing; csrc.nist.gov/pubs/fips/206/ipd 404s). Falcon numbers here are against the Falcon v1.2 submission spec / PQClean, not against a FIPS-conformant implementation." That distinction is a *feature* of the benchmark: ML-DSA (FIPS 204) and SLH-DSA (FIPS 205) are final; Falcon is not.

## 3. What Falcon-512 verification actually costs — feasible in an integer-only zkVM guest

Concrete constants (all quoted, not inferred):
- q = 12289. PQClean falcon-512/clean/vrfy.c, verbatim: `#define Q     12289` (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/PQClean/PQClean/master/crypto_sign/falcon-512/clean/vrfy.c).
- Norm bound, Falcon spec Table 3.3: "Max. signature square norm ⌊β2⌋ 34 034 726" (Falcon-512) / "70 265 242" (Falcon-1024). PQClean `common.c` `l2bound[9] = 34034726` — matches exactly.
- PQClean `common.c` comment, verbatim: "We use the l2-norm. Code below uses only 32-bit operations to compute the square of the norm with saturation to 2^32-1 if the value exceeds 2^31-1." → the norm check fits in u32. No bignum.
- `PQCLEAN_FALCON512_CLEAN_verify_raw` structure: "Reduces s2 modulo q / Computes -s1 = s2*h - c0 via NTT operations / Normalizes result to [-q/2..q/2] range / Returns result of is_short() check".
- Hash: SHAKE-256. Falcon spec §3.7, verbatim: "we use a unique XOF for all security levels: SHAKE-256". Algorithm 3 HashToPoint extracts 16 bits per candidate, `k ← ⌊2^16/q⌋`, rejecting `t ≥ kq`.
- Sizes: Falcon-512 pk 897 B, sig 666 B (spec Table 3.3, and Pornin's README).

**MY OWN INFERENCE (clearly labelled — none of the following is published):**
- HashToPoint rejection rate: k = ⌊65536/12289⌋ = 5, kq = 61445, so P(reject) = 4091/65536 ≈ 6.24%. Expect ≈ 546 sixteen-bit draws for 512 coefficients ≈ **~1092 bytes squeezed**. SHAKE-256 rate is 136 B/permutation → **~9 Keccak-f[1600] permutations** (plus absorb).
- Polynomial product: PQClean does NTT(s2), NTT(h), pointwise Montgomery mul, iNTT. Each length-512 NTT is (n/2)·log2(n) = 256·9 = **2304 butterflies**; three transforms ≈ **~6912 modular multiplies mod 12289**, plus 512 pointwise multiplies. All operands < 2^14, so every product fits in u32 — **no multi-precision arithmetic anywhere**.
- Norm: 1024 squarings + accumulation in u32.
- Decompress: bit-level parse of 666 bytes into 512 signed coefficients (Golomb-like; sign bit + 7 low bits + unary high bits).
- Therefore the *arithmetic* is trivial for a RISC-V zkVM: order 10^4 cheap 32-bit ops. **The dominant guest cost will be the ~9 Keccak permutations of SHAKE-256, not the lattice math.** This is exactly why the round-1 finding matters: SP1 has a sha3/SHAKE precompile and RISC Zero does not list one. Expect that single asymmetry to move Falcon-512 verify cycle counts between the two provers by a large factor, and it must be disclosed on every chart.
- Feasibility verdict: **yes, comfortably feasible, integer-only, no_std, stack-only.** Falcon verification is a better zkVM guest citizen than ML-DSA-44 verification (which has larger NTTs mod 8380417, a bigger hint/expansion structure, and much more SHAKE output).

Independent corroboration that this is understood by practitioners — s2morrow README, verbatim:
> "Falcon-512 operates over the ring Z_q[x]/(x^512 + 1) where q = 12289. Verification requires NTT, polynomial arithmetic, and norm checking — all expensive in a zkVM."

## 4. Rust implementation for verification-in-guest — RECOMMENDATION: `fn-dsa-vrfy`

| Crate | Version / date | License | no_std | Language | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **`fn-dsa-vrfy`** (Thomas Pornin, github.com/pornin/rust-fn-dsa) | **0.4.0, 2026-07-22** | **Unlicense** | **YES — `#![no_std]` is line 1 of lib.rs; crates.io category "No standard library"** | pure Rust, integer-only | **USE THIS** |
| `pqcrypto-falcon` (rustpq/pqcrypto) | 0.4.1 | MIT OR Apache-2.0 (wrappers only; PQClean impls under their own licenses — Falcon PQClean is MIT) | YES — `#![no_std]` + `extern crate alloc`, `std` behind a feature | **C via PQClean, requires `cc` build-dep** | Viable as a cross-check oracle on the host; **C-in-guest is a real friction point** for RISC Zero/SP1 toolchains and adds an FFI/allocator surface |
| `falcon-rust` (aszepieniec) | 0.3.0, 2026-07-19 | MIT | **not mentioned in README** — assume no | pure Rust | Honest README, but its own TODO list still has **"NIST KATs"** outstanding, and it warns "We do anticipate slight changes between the standard and the submission, and these changes might break compatibility". Its own perf table shows pure Rust "notably slower than optimized alternatives" |
| `falcon-rs` (lattice-safe) | 0.2.5, 2026-07-20, 1734 downloads, **4 GitHub stars** | MIT | claims yes ("works in embedded and WASM environments", "compiles to wasm32-unknown-unknown") | pure Rust | **AVOID — see red flag below** |
| s2morrow (feltroidprime) Cairo impl | MIT, 208 commits | MIT | n/a | **Cairo, not Rust** | Not usable as a zkVM guest crate; **but read it, see §Bonus** |

**LOUD RED FLAG on `falcon-rs` (lattice-safe):** its crates.io description reads "Native Rust implementation of FN-DSA (FIPS 206), **the NIST post-quantum digital signature standard**" and its README claims it "Passes all NIST Known Answer Tests (FN-DSA-512 & FN-DSA-1024), **full FIPS 206 domain-separation KAT vectors**". **There is no published FIPS 206, therefore there are no FIPS 206 KAT vectors to pass.** Either the crate is testing against Falcon-submission KATs and mislabelling them, or the claim is fabricated. 4 stars, single-maintainer, published 3 days before access. Do not put this in a public benchmark.

Note also: the `falcon-rs` referenced *inside the s2morrow README* (`../falcon-rs/` — "Rust crate — test vector generation, Poseidon hash-to-point, hint computation") is a sibling directory of feltroidprime's monorepo and is **not** the lattice-safe crates.io `falcon-rs`. Do not conflate them.

**Concrete recommendation:** guest depends only on `fn-dsa-vrfy` (Unlicense = maximally permissive, no attribution burden for a public benchmark repo; `#![no_std]`; verify() "uses stack allocation for its internal buffers (which are not large)" — no allocator needed in guest). Host-side, sign with `fn-dsa-sign` from the same family so encodings match by construction, and cross-check a sample against `pqcrypto-falcon` (PQClean C) on the host to prove the vectors are not self-consistent-but-wrong. **Pin the exact version and record it in results JSON**, because Pornin explicitly warns the encoding will change when the real standard lands ("backward compatibility will not be maintained until version 1.0, which will only arrive after the final standard is published").

## 5. Sign outside, verify inside — SOUND, and it is what everyone does

Evidence that this is the actual industry pattern, not a shortcut:
- **s2morrow** (Starknet, Cairo, MIT, https://github.com/feltroidprime/s2morrow): signing is off-chain — the README describes a "Next.js app in `apps/demo/` demonstrates Falcon-512 signing via WASM in the browser", while the Cairo package is the "Production verifier". The signer even *supplies proving hints* to the verifier: "Instead of computing it on-chain, the signer provides `mul_hint = INTT(NTT(s1) * pk_ntt)` as a hint. On-chain verification only needs two forward NTTs to check correctness."
- **Algorand** (technical brief, 2025-11-03): signing happens offline via a CLI tool; verification is the on-chain `falcon_verify` opcode in the AVM.
- Round-1 established BTQ + StarkWare did Falcon on Starknet in 2024 — same shape.

**Soundness argument (my inference, but it is elementary):** Verify is a deterministic, public predicate over (pk, msg, sig). The STARK/zkVM proves "I executed Verify(pk, msg, sig) and it returned accept". Nothing about that statement requires the prover to hold the signing key, and no floating-point behaviour enters the trace. Conversely, signing inside the guest would be actively wrong for a settlement benchmark: it would put the secret key in the witness for no benefit, and it would force IEEE-754 double semantics into an arithmetic circuit, which is the hard problem the whole design avoids. **State this explicitly in the methodology page** — "amortized prover cost per transaction" is cost of *verification* work, and a reader who assumes signing is inside will misread every number by orders of magnitude.

One caveat worth writing down: the *hint* trick (s2morrow) changes the measured object. A hint-assisted verifier does 2 NTTs instead of 3 and shifts work to the signer. If PQ-STARK-BENCH measures the naive PQClean-shaped verifier, say so, and note that a hint-optimized guest is a known further optimization — otherwise a reviewer who knows s2morrow will say the numbers are 1.5x pessimistic on the NTT portion.

## Bonus: s2morrow is the closest published prior art to your Falcon lane — and its published numbers do not add up

Published Cairo step counts (README, "Profiled at commit `a1f5ed9` with `cairo-profiler` (cumulative steps)"):
- `verify` (e2e) — **63,177 steps** — "Full verification including hash-to-point"
- `verify_with_msg_point` — **26,301 steps**
- `hash_to_point` — **5,988 steps** — "Poseidon XOF squeeze (22 permutations)"
- "Total test cost: 119,034 steps (includes deserialization overhead)"
- "L2 gas: `verify` ~13.2M | `verify_with_msg_point` ~56.6M"

**LOUD FLAG — two inconsistencies, both worth citing:**
1. `verify_with_msg_point` has **fewer than half the steps** of `verify` (26,301 vs 63,177) yet is quoted at **4.3x the L2 gas** (56.6M vs 13.2M). On Starknet, L2 gas is dominated by steps. These two gas figures are almost certainly swapped or mislabelled in the README.
2. The **project website https://www.s2morrow.xyz/ says Falcon-512 is "~9.5M L2 gas"**, while the repo README says `verify` is "~13.2M". Same project, two different headline numbers, neither dated. Also note 63,177 ≠ 26,301 + 5,988, so "cumulative steps" is not decomposing the way the table implies.

Also note s2morrow **replaces SHAKE-256 with Poseidon** ("Uses Poseidon (Starknet-native) instead of SHAKE-256"). That is a **deviation from the Falcon spec** — spec §3.7 mandates SHAKE-256 as the XOF, and the spec itself warns "any variant deviating from the procedure expressed in algorithm 3 implies that the same message will hash to a different value, which breaks interoperability." So s2morrow's ~9.5M/13.2M gas is **not** a number for standard Falcon-512. If PQ-STARK-BENCH cites it as prior art, cite it with that caveat — and it is a strong argument for your benchmark existing: nobody has published spec-conformant SHAKE-256 Falcon-512 verification cost in a general-purpose zkVM.
