topic: Benchmark-suite conventions required for credibility with cryptographers and zk researchers (Ethproofs, SUPERCOP/eBACS, zkVM benchmark repos), plus concrete MacBook-harness pitfalls and a METHODOLOGY.md checklist for PQ-STARK-BENCH
bottom_line: Three separate communities have already converged on the same core conventions, and none of them are exotic: report a robust central statistic over many repetitions with dispersion (SUPERCOP: median/quartiles, now "stabilized second quartile"), pin and publish the exact machine + software version + compiler in machine-readable form, and disclose every accelerator/precompile asymmetry loudly because that is the #1 documented criticism of zkVM benchmarks specifically. For our harness the honest position is that a MacBook CANNOT be frequency-pinned the way SUPERCOP tells you to pin an x86 box (Apple Silicon has no cpufreq governor; macOS/hardware sets cluster frequency by workload, QoS and thermal status), so we must stop pretending to precision we do not have: report medians + IQR of >=5 independent proving runs, record wall-clock + peak RSS + thermal/power telemetry per run, mark laptop numbers as "indicative, not comparable across machines", and treat a pinned Linux cloud box as the only citable headline hardware. Criterion is the wrong tool for the prover loop (a single ML-DSA proof takes seconds to minutes; Criterion's defaults are 100 samples over 5s measurement time) — use Criterion ONLY for the native/host-side signature verification baselines, and a bespoke repeat-N runner for the prover.
details:
ACCESS DATE FOR ALL OF THE BELOW: 2026-07-23.

===========================================================
1. SUPERCOP / eBACS — the gold standard (VERIFIED, fetched)
===========================================================
URL: https://bench.cr.yp.to/supercop.html
Title: "SUPERCOP" (eBACS: ECRYPT Benchmarking of Cryptographic Systems)
Visible version on page: page version 2026.06.27, describing SUPERCOP version 20260627.

Verbatim, on the central statistic:
- "It records all the resulting measurements. It also runs the measuring program multiple times in case there are variations from one run to another (from, e.g., randomized memory layouts). Medians and quartiles are reported on the web pages (or stabilized quartiles starting with supercop-20260214)."
  => MEDIAN + QUARTILES, not mean. And explicitly MULTIPLE INDEPENDENT PROCESS RUNS, not just multiple iterations inside one process, because memory layout randomization changes results between runs. This is the single most transferable convention for us.
- Search-surfaced (Google snippet of the same page, not independently re-fetched at the sentence level): "any severe discrepancies are flagged in red." Treat as PLAUSIBLE, lower confidence than the block-quoted sentence above.

Verbatim, on cycle counting:
- SUPERCOP "generally uses the RDTSC instruction" (counts "cycles since CPU reset"), but since 2021 "favors the RDPMC instruction on Linux systems that make RDPMC available to applications" because "The cycle counter provided through RDPMC runs at the actual CPU frequency."
- RDTSC by contrast counts "the cycles of an artificial clock always running at the nominal CPU frequency."
  => The distinction matters: RDTSC-derived "cycles" are a disguised wall-clock measurement; only RDPMC gives true core cycles. Anyone reporting "cycles" without saying which counter is being sloppy.

Verbatim, on frequency scaling / Turbo:
- Goal is "measuring performance at the nominal CPU frequency".
- Instruction to operators: "disable underclocking, disable overclocking, and check for adequate cooling."
- On picking the frequency to report: when "the top two frequencies are separated by just 1000, the larger is Turbo Boost; use the smaller".
  => eBACS deliberately EXCLUDES turbo. Note also a search-surfaced eBACS statement that overclocking "is a security problem; overclocking is normally disabled" — lower confidence, snippet only.

Verbatim, on per-machine metadata:
- "Computer name; e.g., utrecht. There is a separate page providing more information about the computers: e.g., utrecht's CPU is a 2400MHz Intel Core 2 Quad Q6600 (6fb)."
- Recorded metadata explicitly includes: "SUPERCOP version," "Application Binary Interface (ABI)," "Benchmark start date," "CPU identifier," "compiler version," "implementation used," and "number of CPU cycles per second."
  => Note the ABI field and the "implementation used" field. eBACS benchmarks MANY implementations of the same primitive and reports which one won. For us the analogue is: which ML-DSA crate, which commit, which feature flags, compiled for which zkVM target.

DISCONFIRMATION: https://cr.yp.to/talks/2024.09.09/slides-djb-20240909-ebacs-4x3.pdf (DJB eBACS slides, 2024-09-09) FAILED TO FETCH — the fetcher errored with "Parse Error: Content-Length can't be present with Transfer-Encoding". I did NOT read those slides. Do not cite them.

===========================================================
2. Ethproofs (VERIFIED formula; but docs are thin)
===========================================================
Cost formula, quoted verbatim from the Ethproofs V2 design document at https://hackmd.io/xXHBmJS6R9-CeoFyNoojhw :
  "∑(hourly price per instance × instances per cluster) × proving time / (1000 × 60 × 60)"
The same formula was surfaced in search results as: "∑(hourly price per instance * instances per cluster) * proving time / (1000 * 60 * 60)", where hourly price per instance is the per-hour USD rate charged by AWS for one instance of the hardware cluster most equivalent to the prover's setup.
  => The 1000 in the denominator implies proving time is in MILLISECONDS. So: cost_usd = (cluster $/hr) * proving_time_ms / 3.6e6. This is trivially portable to us and I recommend we adopt it verbatim, with our own instance price table.

IMPORTANT CORRECTION / DISCONFIRMATION about that HackMD: it is NOT a critique document. Fetched 2026-07-23, it is "a technical specification/wireframe for the Ethproofs V2 website interface design" — navigation structure, dashboard layout, data table columns, tooltip text, hardware pricing methodology. It contains NO criticisms of Ethproofs methodology and NO dates. If anyone cites "Ethproofs V2 Analysis" as a critique, that is wrong.

Hardware normalization, from https://hackmd.io/@willcorcoran/ry16VMmfbx ("Ethproofs 2025 Review & 2026 Roadmap", dates in body: launched 2025-01-28; community call announcement 2025-12-19):
- The standardized cost unit is the "5090 GPU-hour". Verbatim rationale: this "removes cloud-mapping distortions and gives each Persona a clean, comparable, decentralization-aligned cost baseline."
- Personas: Persona 1 = Single-GPU, 1:100 proving ratio; Persona 2 = Multi-GPU, 1:1 proving ratio. (Search results additionally listed four modes: Single GPU 1:100 "altruistic", Single GPU 1:1 "real-time", Multi GPU 1:100 "batch", Multi GPU 1:1 "real-time" — snippet-level, medium confidence.)
- Headline deltas for Jan 28 – Dec 8, 2025: latency "16m 44s → 15.9s"; cost "$1.69 → $0.0376".
  => The key structural lesson: they moved from "describe your own cluster" to "report only the number of GPUs, we normalize", because self-reported heterogeneous hardware made the leaderboard meaningless. Search snippet, verbatim-ish: "With standardized hardware, provers report only the number of GPUs, and Ethproofs handles the rest (power, capacity, normalization)."

DISCONFIRMATIONS on Ethproofs docs (all fetched 2026-07-23):
- https://ethproofs.org/learn — does NOT contain metric definitions, cost formula, or hardware normalization methodology. It only says Ethproofs "aggregates data from various zkVM teams ... including key metrics such as cost, latency, and proving time" and links to /metrics.
- https://ethproofs.org/metrics — does NOT contain formal metric definitions or formulas either. It has targets/insights only: latency should "trend down toward 10s threshold"; cost should "trend down toward $0.01"; sub-10s rate target "≥70%"; GPU pricing as "weekly average GPU hourly price"; and the observation that "1:1 on-prem leads on performance and cost efficiency".
- https://github.com/ethproofs/ethproofs — repo landing page contains NO methodology, NO metric definitions, NO cost formula, NO prover-submission API schema, NO hardware/cluster field list.
  => Bluntly: Ethproofs' published methodology is weaker than its reputation. The formula only exists in a HackMD design wireframe. If we publish a real METHODOLOGY.md we will already be ahead of them on documentation, and we can say so.

===========================================================
3. zkVM benchmark repos and their documented criticisms
===========================================================
succinctlabs/zkvm-perf, https://github.com/succinctlabs/zkvm-perf (README fetched via raw.githubusercontent.com):
- "Each benchmark run produces a CSV file with detailed performance metrics" — but the README does NOT enumerate the CSV columns.
- Hardware: GPU runs on EC2 g6.16xlarge; CPU runs on EC2 r7i.16xlarge. Matrix strategy runs both instance types. Orchestration: adhoc-matrix.yml + run-on-runner.yml.
- Programs benchmarked: loop10k, loop100k, loop1m, loop3m, loop10m, loop30m, loop100m, fibonacci, tendermint, reth1, reth2.
- Operational gotcha worth stealing verbatim: "run one round of a small program (e.g., Fibonacci) to download the R0 docker image before benchmarking to avoid affecting benchmark times."
- CRITICISM (mine, grounded in the fetch): the README says "The CSV includes the instance type, allowing for easy comparison between GPU and CPU performance" and "provides no explicit disclaimers about the fairness of such comparisons." A vendor-run harness whose default prover is 'sp1' and which ships no fairness caveat is exactly the thing we must not replicate.
- NethermindEth/zkvm-benchmarks exists and was "originally forked from zkvm-perf" (search-level, medium confidence).

RISC Zero benchmarks — TWO DEAD LINKS, report loudly:
- https://reports.risczero.com/benchmarks — DNS FAILURE, "getaddrinfo ENOTFOUND reports.risczero.com". The host does not exist. Many papers and blog posts still cite this URL.
- https://dev.risczero.com/api/benchmarks — HTTP 404 Not Found.
- https://www.mintlify.com/risc0/risc0/resources/benchmarks — HTTP 410 Gone.
  => I could not reach ANY live first-party RISC Zero benchmarks page on 2026-07-23. Search snippets claim RISC Zero "provides tools to measure execution time, throughput, and cycle counts across different hardware configurations" and that "You can generate a datasheet to get performance data for your exact system, or to benchmark a different RISC Zero zkVM version" — that datasheet-generation model (benchmark on YOUR machine, publish the datasheet) is a good pattern for us, but I could not verify it against a live page. Treat as UNVERIFIED.

Fenbushi Capital, "Benchmarking zkVMs: Current State and Prospects", https://fenbushicapital.medium.com/benchmarking-zkvms-current-state-and-prospects-ba859b44f560 , published 2025-06-18. Verbatim criticisms:
- "most existing benchmarks — such as the SP1 launch post, the Vac report, and zkbenchmarks.com — employ incomparable methodologies"
- Precompiles as confound, with a concrete number: RISC Zero achieved 0.54 seconds on SHA2-2048 while Jolt took 2.7 seconds, attributed to Jolt's "current absence of lookup table support."
- Hardware dependency: "SP1's GPU performance" shows "dependency on underlying hardware capabilities", differing across EC2 instances.
- Workload selection: they used four distinct tasks because "performance variability" is "contingent upon the specific program being executed."
- Missing security metadata: results may be "misleading if security levels differ substantially"; some projects "remain in development phases and may lack comprehensive security validation."
  => The security-level point is DIRECTLY load-bearing for us: STARK security parameters (FRI blowup, number of queries, conjectured vs proven soundness, field size) differ between SP1 and RISC Zero and between their own configurations. A prover-time comparison at unequal claimed security bits is not a comparison. We must publish the security parameter set per row.

a16z crypto, "Understanding Jolt: clarifications and reflections", https://a16zcrypto.com/posts/article/understanding-jolt-clarifications-and-reflections/ , published 2024-06-03. Verbatim:
- "when attempting to compare two different SNARKs, there are endless confounding factors that often make an 'apples to apples' comparison all but impossible."
- On precompile credit: granting credit for optimizations means "New approaches will forever be at a disadvantage in any benchmark simply because those implementing them have not had time commensurate with older projects."
- On disclosure: "builders will have to provide the full context, details, and rationales behind any benchmarks so the community can understand and discuss them."
  => This is the citation to put in METHODOLOGY.md justifying our mandatory precompile-asymmetry disclosure. It also gives us the honest framing for the Round-1 finding that neither SP1 nor RISC Zero has a lattice/NTT precompile while both accelerate Ed25519/ECDSA: the PQ penalty we measure is an ENGINEERING-MATURITY penalty, not an information-theoretic one, and we should say that in the chart caption, not the appendix.

Ethereum Foundation zkEVM, "Benchmarking zkVMs for Ethereum", https://zkevm.ethereum.foundation/blog/benchmarking-zkvms , published 2025-10-22. Verbatim/near-verbatim:
- Three-stage method: "Stage 1: Create benchmark cases, Stage 2: Run benchmarks in all possible EL/zkVM combinations, Stage 3: Analyze results", with stages reiterated as optimizations emerge.
- They measure "worst-case blocks" rather than average cases — deliberate adversarial workload selection.
- They enumerate hardware as a first-class reported dimension: "Underlying hardware configuration (which CPU, GPU(s), etc)."
- Precompiles: zkVMs "target the most challenging cases with a concept of 'zkVM precompiles'" — patched implementations or native syscalls.
- Reproducibility: results in a "public repository" with ongoing updates; open-source tooling (ere, workload) enabling "reproducible runs on different hardware", with community contributions invited.
  => "All possible combinations" + "worst case, not average" + separate reusable runner tool (ere) is the structure most worth copying. Our analogue of "all possible EL/zkVM combinations" is the full cross product {ML-DSA-44, Falcon-512, SLH-DSA-128s, Ed25519-control} x {SP1, RISC Zero} x {batch sizes}. Publishing an incomplete cross product with unexplained holes is how a suite loses credibility; publishing the holes as explicit "not supported / OOM / >6h timeout" cells is how it keeps it.

===========================================================
4. Criterion — and why it is the WRONG tool for the prover loop
===========================================================
https://bheisler.github.io/criterion.rs/book/analysis.html and https://docs.rs/criterion/latest/criterion/struct.Criterion.html (crate version shown: criterion 0.8.2, released 2026-06-22).

Verbatim, four phases: "Warmup - The routine is executed repeatedly to fill the CPU and OS caches and (if applicable) give the JIT time to compile the code; Measurement - The routine is executed repeatedly and the execution times are recorded; Analysis - The recorded samples are analyzed and distilled into meaningful statistics, which are then reported to the user; Comparison - The performance of the current run is compared to the stored data from the last run to determine whether it has changed, and if so by how much".

Verbatim documented DEFAULTS (from docs.rs, criterion 0.8.2):
- Sample size: "100 measurements"
- Warm-up time: "3 s"
- Measurement time: "5 s"
- Bootstrap size: "100 000 resamples"
- Noise threshold: "0.01 (1%)"
- Confidence level: "0.95"
- Significance level: "0.05"

Verbatim, outlier classification (Tukey fences): "values less than (25th percentile - 1.5 * IQR) or greater than (75th percentile + 1.5 * IQR) are considered outliers. Criterion.rs creates additional fences at (25pct - 3 * IQR) and (75pct + 3 * IQR); values outside that range are considered severe outliers."
Verbatim, outlier handling: "Outlier samples are not dropped from the data, and are used in the following analysis steps along with all other samples."
Reported statistics: it calculates "mean, standard deviation, median and median absolute deviation" plus slope estimates via linear regression.

CONSEQUENCE FOR US (my inference, clearly labeled): 100 samples x a multi-second-to-multi-minute ML-DSA-in-zkVM proof = hours to days per cell, and Criterion's 5s measurement_time default would silently collapse to the minimum sample count. Criterion is correct and should be used for the HOST-SIDE native verification baselines (microsecond-scale ML-DSA-44 / Falcon-512 / Ed25519 verify) where 100 samples is cheap and the bootstrap CI is meaningful. For the PROVER we write our own runner: N independent process invocations (SUPERCOP's "runs the measuring program multiple times" rule), N>=5 for expensive cells and N>=11 for cheap ones, report median + IQR + min + max + N, keep every raw sample in the results JSON, and never drop outliers (Criterion's own rule) — instead flag them.

===========================================================
5. The MacBook problem, honestly (this is the hard part)
===========================================================
The eBACS instruction "disable underclocking, disable overclocking, and check for adequate cooling" is NOT EXECUTABLE on Apple Silicon. Grounding (eclecticlight.co, Howard Oakley, multiple dated posts incl. 2024-02-19 "Apple silicon: 1 Cores, clusters and performance", 2025-01-20 "What are CPU core frequencies in Apple silicon Macs?", 2026-04-13 "CPU core frequencies updated for all current Apple silicon Macs"): P/E cores run "at a range of different frequencies"; cores are grouped into clusters of 2-6, and "macOS sets the frequency of each cluster according to workload, Quality of Service, power mode and thermal status." Frequency management is hardware-automated per cluster. There is no cpufreq governor to pin. QoS, not the user, decides core placement: a min-QoS thread ran "exclusively on the two E cores ... at an average frequency of 1200 MHz", while P cores reach e.g. M4 4512 MHz / M5 4608 MHz. E-core low-QoS floor cited as 744 MHz on older chips.
=> So a Rust thread whose QoS class is not explicitly set can land on E cores and be 3-5x slower with no error and no visible cause. This is a real silent-corruption-of-results risk, on the same footing as the SelectX-class bugs we chase.

Thermal throttling magnitude (search-level, MEDIUM confidence, vendor-blog sources not first-party): MacBook Pro chassis "typically lose 10–20% of sustained throughput to throttling", Mac Studio/Mac Pro "generally stay under 5%", and a fanless MacBook Air "throttles after 5-10 minutes of sustained inference". A GPU-heavy sustained load was reported to shed "~50–60% of their throughput". I did not verify these against a primary measurement and they are NOT for citing as fact — but the ORDER OF MAGNITUDE justifies the design rule: a 12-hour batch sweep on a laptop will produce a downward-drifting time series that looks like an algorithmic effect and is not one.
Tooling that IS first-party available: `powermetrics` (built-in macOS CLI, gives clock speeds, power draw, thermal pressure); `sudo powermetrics --samplers smc,cpu_power`. Third-party asitop wraps it.

HONEST HANDLING (my recommendation, not a citation):
(a) Never publish a headline cross-scheme or cross-prover number from a MacBook. Laptop runs are for development, regression detection, and shape-of-curve only. The citable table is a pinned Linux x86_64 cloud box.
(b) RANDOMIZE / INTERLEAVE the run order across the whole sweep instead of running scheme-by-scheme. If ML-DSA is always measured in hour 1 (cold) and SLH-DSA always in hour 9 (hot), thermal drift becomes a fake scheme effect. Interleaving converts a systematic bias into variance you can see in the IQR.
(c) Emit a thermal/frequency sidecar per run (thermal pressure level, average package power, elapsed since sweep start) and PLOT prover-time vs sweep-position as a published diagnostic. If that plot has a slope, the run is invalid and we say so.
(d) Set thread QoS explicitly to userInteractive/userInitiated so work does not get demoted to E cores; record the QoS used.
(e) Insert a fixed cooldown between cells and record it. Record whether the lid was open, whether AC power was connected (macOS behaves differently on battery), and the low-power-mode setting.
(f) State plainly in METHODOLOGY.md: "Apple Silicon provides no mechanism to pin CPU frequency; we therefore do not report cycle counts on macOS and do not claim cross-machine comparability for macOS rows."

===========================================================
6. THE CHECKLIST — implement in harness, state in METHODOLOGY.md
===========================================================
A. STATISTICS
 1. Median + IQR (Q1/Q3), never bare mean. [eBACS]
 2. Report N explicitly per cell. N>=5 independent PROCESS invocations for prover cells; N>=11 for host-side verify. [eBACS: "runs the measuring program multiple times"]
 3. Keep and publish every raw sample in the JSON. Never publish only the aggregate.
 4. Do not drop outliers; classify them with Tukey fences (1.5*IQR mild, 3*IQR severe) and flag them. [Criterion]
 5. Publish min and max alongside median; a max/median ratio > 1.3 gets a warning badge.
 6. Criterion (0.8.2 defaults: 100 samples, 3s warmup, 5s measurement, 100k resamples, 95% CI) is used ONLY for native/host verification baselines. Prover cells use the bespoke runner. State this split.

B. DETERMINISM AND FAIRNESS
 7. Fixed test-vector corpus with a published SHA-256, ideally NIST/ACVP KATs for ML-DSA-44 / SLH-DSA, and the Falcon reference KATs. Same messages for every scheme and prover.
 8. Fixed RNG seed for anything randomized; publish the seed.
 9. Equal SECURITY PARAMETERS across compared rows, published per row: STARK field, FRI blowup factor, query count, claimed conjectured-vs-proven soundness bits, recursion/wrap stage present or not. Refuse to put two rows on the same chart at different claimed bits. [Fenbushi]
10. PRECOMPILE DISCLOSURE TABLE, printed on every chart, not in an appendix: for each (scheme, prover) cell, which precompiles/syscalls were active (sha256, keccak, sha3/SHAKE, ed25519, secp256k1, bigint) and which were NOT available. Explicitly state that no lattice/NTT/ML-DSA/Falcon precompile exists in either prover, and that the Ed25519 control DOES get accelerated, so the PQ:classical ratio overstates the intrinsic gap. [a16z, Fenbushi, EF]
11. Publish the FULL cross product with explicit failure reasons for empty cells (unsupported / OOM at X GB / timeout at X h / build failure), never a silent hole. [EF "all possible combinations"]
12. Adversarial/worst-case inputs alongside typical ones where meaningful (e.g. Falcon signatures near the norm bound, max-length messages). [EF "worst-case blocks"]
13. Pre-warm caches and pre-pull any container image before timing. [zkvm-perf's R0 docker note]
14. Interleave/randomize run order across the sweep; publish the order.

C. WHAT TO REPORT PER CELL (the metric set)
15. Prover wall-clock (ms), median + IQR + N.
16. Peak resident memory (RSS, bytes) — this is the metric that kills laptop feasibility and the one the 2021/1048 paper reported; do not omit it.
17. zkVM cycle count (deterministic, hardware-independent — this is the ONE number that IS comparable across machines for the same prover; but NOT comparable across provers, say so).
18. Proof size (bytes), and separately the size of any wrapped/recursed proof.
19. Verifier time (ms) and verifier memory.
20. AMORTIZED per-signature: prover_ms/N, RSS/N, proof_bytes/N. Present alongside the Round-1 established fact that time and RAM are ~flat per signature and only BYTES amortize — frame it as confirmation of prior work, not discovery.
21. Cost in USD using the Ethproofs formula verbatim: cost = SUM(hourly price per instance * instances per cluster) * proving_time_ms / (1000*60*60). Publish the price table and its retrieval date, because prices move. [Ethproofs]
22. Native (non-zkVM) verification time for the same scheme on the same host, as the denominator for a "zk overhead factor".
23. Energy, if measurable (powermetrics on macOS) — optional, mark as best-effort.

D. MANDATORY METADATA IN EVERY RESULTS FILE (JSON schema fields)
24. schema_version of the results file itself.
25. run_id (UUID), run_started_utc, run_finished_utc, timezone-independent ISO-8601.
26. Machine: hostname/label, CPU model string, core counts (P/E on Apple Silicon), nominal frequency, whether turbo/boost was enabled or unpinnable, total RAM, GPU model + count + driver version, OS + kernel version, cloud instance type if applicable. [eBACS "CPU identifier", "number of CPU cycles per second"; EF "which CPU, GPU(s)"]
27. Toolchain: rustc version + host triple + target triple, ABI, cargo profile, opt-level, lto, codegen-units, RUSTFLAGS/target-features verbatim. [eBACS "compiler version", "Application Binary Interface (ABI)"]
28. Prover: SP1 / RISC Zero version string AND the exact git commit, plus prover mode (core / compressed / groth16 / plonk), GPU-enabled bool, and the acceleration backend actually used (CUDA / Metal / AVX / scalar). Round-1 already establishes that macOS SP1 = scalar CPU only and RISC Zero groth16 is x86-only — that must be a machine-readable field, not prose.
29. Scheme: name, parameter set (ML-DSA-44 etc.), implementation crate name + version + git commit + feature flags. [eBACS "implementation used"]
30. Batch size N, message size, corpus SHA-256, RNG seed.
31. Harness git commit (dirty flag included) and the exact command line invoked.
32. Environment hygiene flags: on_ac_power, low_power_mode, thread QoS class, cooldown_seconds_before_run, sweep_position_index, concurrent_load_detected, thermal_pressure_before/after, avg_package_power_w.
33. status field: ok / oom / timeout / unsupported / build_failed, with a human-readable reason. Never omit a failed cell.
34. Everything must be reproducible from the JSON alone: a reader should be able to reconstruct the exact command without reading our prose.

E. PUBLICATION DISCIPLINE
35. Open-source the harness and the raw JSON, not just the charts. [EF public repo + ere tooling]
36. Publish a "known limitations" section that names our own weakest results first. The Round-1 finding about the precompile asymmetry belongs there AND in every caption.
37. Never claim a bare "first". Cite BTQ/StarkWare Falcon-on-Starknet 2024 and ePrint 2021/1048 in the README, above the fold.
38. Version the results: results/v{schema}/{prover}/{scheme}/{N}.json, and keep old runs forever so regressions are visible. eBACS's credibility is largely from its longitudinal archive.
39. Date-stamp every external number we quote (cloud prices, prover versions) with its retrieval date, because we now have three dead RISC Zero benchmark URLs as proof that this ecosystem's links rot within months.

blockers:
1) THREE dead RISC Zero benchmark URLs (reports.risczero.com DNS failure, dev.risczero.com/api/benchmarks 404, mintlify 410). I could not verify ANY first-party RISC Zero benchmarking methodology on 2026-07-23. The "generate a datasheet on your own machine" model is search-snippet only and UNVERIFIED. Fallback: cite the RISC Zero GitHub repo's benchmark code directly (needs a follow-up fetch of github.com/risc0/risc0 benchmark dirs) rather than any docs page, and say in METHODOLOGY.md that first-party RISC Zero benchmark docs were unreachable on our access date.
2) Ethproofs' published methodology is much thinner than assumed: /learn, /metrics and the GitHub repo all lack the cost formula and metric definitions. The formula exists ONLY in a HackMD UI wireframe. If a reviewer challenges our "we follow Ethproofs' cost convention" claim, the only citable artifact is that wireframe. Fallback: define the formula ourselves in METHODOLOGY.md, credit Ethproofs as the origin, and note the citation weakness openly.
3) The Apple Silicon frequency/QoS claims come from search snippets of eclecticlight.co, not from direct fetches, and the thermal-throttling percentages (10-20% MacBook Pro, ~50-60% sustained GPU) come from vendor/hobby blogs I did not verify. Do NOT put those percentages in METHODOLOGY.md as facts. Fallback: measure our own throttling curve with powermetrics on the actual MacBook and publish OUR number as the citation. That is a half-day of work and it is strictly better than citing a blog.
4) DJB's eBACS slides failed to fetch (Transfer-Encoding parse error). Everything eBACS-related here rests on bench.cr.yp.to/supercop.html alone, which is fine, but a second eBACS source would harden it. Fallback: retry the PDF with curl via Bash, or cite the supercop.html page only.
5) zkvm-perf's README does not enumerate its CSV columns, so I cannot tell you what a respected vendor harness actually emits per row. Fallback: read the Rust source in succinctlabs/zkvm-perf to extract the struct that serializes to CSV — that is the ground truth and it is a 10-minute job.
6) Biggest methodological risk to the project itself: Criterion's defaults are wildly wrong for multi-second prover runs and will silently produce garbage sample counts if pointed at the prover. If the harness is already scaffolded with Criterion around the prover loop, that needs to change before any numbers are published.
